# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Mechanisms (KTU 2024 Module 1 Context)

In the language of KTU 2024 Scheme Engineering Mechanics and Dynamics of Machinery, a **mechanism** is defined as an assemblage of resistant bodies (links) connected by kinematic joints, employed for the purpose of transmitting or transforming motion from a driver to a driven member. A mechanism, in contrast to a machine, performs specific mechanical movements; a *machine* is a mechanism or combination of mechanisms which, besides imparting motion, transmits or modifies force to do useful work.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Textbook Standard):**
> *A mechanism is a system of rigid bodies (links) connected by joints, designed to produce or transmit motion in a predetermined manner.*

The study of mechanisms forms the bedrock of *Dynamics of Machinery*. Without understanding how basic linkages move, the engineer cannot compute the **static forces**, **dynamic forces**, **inertial torques**, or **shaker effects** that govern real machine performance.

## 1.2 The Five Fundamental Mechanisms of Module 1

### 1.2.1 Four Bar Chain (Four-Bar Linkage)

A **four bar chain** (or four-bar linkage) is the simplest closed kinematic chain consisting of **four rigid links** connected end-to-end by **four revolute (turning) pairs** to form a closed loop. The four links are conventionally labelled:

- **Frame (or Fixed Link)** — usually denoted as link **1** or the *ground link*.
- **Input Link (Crank)** — the driver link, link **2**.
- **Coupler (Connecting Rod)** — link **3**.
- **Output Link (Rocker or Follower)** — link **4**.

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine the **wiper mechanism of an automobile**. The chassis (ground), the wiper arm (input), the connecting link (coupler), and the second wiper blade (output) form a classic four bar chain. As the motor rotates one blade, the other sweeps in a coordinated arc.

### 1.2.2 Slider Crank Mechanism

The **slider crank mechanism** is a four-bar linkage variant in which **one of the rotating links is replaced by a sliding (prismatic) member**. The standard configuration contains:
- A **crank** that rotates fully (driver).
- A **connecting rod** (coupler).
- A **slider** (piston) that translates in a straight line.
- A **fixed link** (the cylinder/frame).

**Intuitive Analogy:** The **engine piston of a motorcycle**. The reciprocating motion of the piston inside the cylinder is converted to rotational motion of the crankshaft (and vice versa). Every internal combustion engine is essentially a slider-crank machine.

### 1.2.3 Quick Return Motion Mechanism

A **quick return mechanism** is a special-purpose linkage in which the **time ratio** between the *forward (cutting) stroke* and the *return (idle) stroke* is intentionally unequal. The forward stroke is slow and the return stroke is fast.

**Intuitive Analogy:** A **power hacksaw machine**. The saw blade cuts slowly through metal on the forward stroke (cutting), but snaps back quickly during the return stroke. This maximizes productivity by minimizing non-productive time.

### 1.2.4 Steering Mechanism (Ackermann Steering Gear)

The **steering mechanism** of an automobile is a four-bar chain with the **ground link extended** to a length equal to the wheelbase. Its purpose is to ensure that during a turn, the **inner wheel turns through a greater angle than the outer wheel**, preventing skidding of the tyres.

**Intuitive Analogy:** When you steer a car around a tight corner, the front wheels are not parallel — the inner wheel pivots more sharply. This is exactly what the Ackermann steering geometry guarantees.

### 1.2.5 Universal Coupling (Hooke's Joint)

A **universal coupling** (also called a **Hooke's joint** or **Cardan joint**) is a mechanism with **two revolute pairs whose axes intersect at an angle**, used to transmit rotary motion between two non-collinear shafts.

**Intuitive Analogy:** The **propeller shaft of a truck** connecting the gearbox output to the rear axle differential — these two shafts are not in a straight line because the engine sits higher than the differential, so a flexible (universal) joint is mandatory.

## 1.3 Static & Dynamic Force Analysis — Setting the Stage

In Module 1 of PEIET413, after studying the kinematics of mechanisms, students must learn to compute the **forces** experienced by each link. Two flavours of force analysis are covered:

| Type | Definition | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Force Analysis** | Analysis of a mechanism when the system is in **static equilibrium** (no motion, or analysed as if at an instant) — the external force plus link weights are balanced by joint reactions. | Initial design, vertical force balancing on a hoisting crane, robotic arm hold-position analysis. |
| **Dynamic Force Analysis** | Analysis considering the **inertial forces** generated by accelerating masses during motion. | Engine balancing, reciprocating compressor vibration, high-speed cam-follower systems. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Principle:** Both analyses ultimately require satisfying **Newton's Laws of Motion** in vector form: $\sum \vec{F} = m \vec{a}$ (translational) and $\sum \vec{M} = I \alpha$ (rotational).

## 1.4 Static Equilibrium Conditions — The Three Foundational Rules

For a rigid body in equilibrium, **three independent conditions** must be satisfied simultaneously:

**Rule 1 — Sum of Forces in X-direction = 0:**
$$\sum F_x = 0$$

**Rule 2 — Sum of Forces in Y-direction = 0:**
$$\sum F_y = 0$$

**Rule 3 — Sum of Moments about any point = 0:**
$$\sum M = 0$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These are the **only three independent equations** available for a 2D rigid body. This is why planar force analysis of mechanisms is tractable — each link can have at most 3 unknown reactions, and a 4-link closed chain can be solved step by step.

## 1.5 Two-Force Members and Three-Force Members — The Shortcut Rules

### 1.5.1 Two-Force Member

A **two-force member** is a rigid link on which **forces act at exactly two points only**. By applying the equilibrium conditions $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, and $\sum M = 0$:

- The **two forces must be equal in magnitude**.
- The two forces must be **opposite in direction**.
- The two forces must be **collinear** (act along the same straight line, which is the line connecting the two points of application).

> [!NOTE]
> **Common Examples of Two-Force Members in Mechanisms:**
> - The **connecting rod** of a slider-crank (if its own weight is neglected, it is acted upon by forces only at the crank-pin end and the wrist-pin end).
> - The **coupler** of a four-bar chain (neglecting self-weight).
> - Tie-rods and struts in a structural frame.
> - Cables and ropes under tension.

### 1.5.2 Three-Force Member

A **three-force member** is a rigid link on which **three forces act** at three different points. If the body is in equilibrium, then by the moment equation, the **lines of action of all three forces must either be concurrent (meet at a single point) or be parallel.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic:** When the three forces are *not parallel*, the **lines of action must intersect at a single common point (the point of concurrency)**. This is the fundamental theorem used in graphical force analysis of mechanisms.

### 1.5.3 Quick Comparison Table

| Property | Two-Force Member | Three-Force Member |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of Force Application Points** | 2 | 3 |
| **Direction of Forces** | Equal, opposite, **collinear** | Concurrent at a point (or parallel) |
| **Number of Unknowns** | 1 (magnitude) | Up to 6 (magnitude + direction × 3) |
| **KTU Typical Application** | Connecting rod, coupler | Crank, rocker, slider |
| **Solved Using** | Single line of action concept | Lami's theorem or vector polygon |

## 1.6 Method of Virtual Work — The Energy-Based Shortcut

The **principle of virtual work** states that for a system in equilibrium, the **total virtual work done by all external forces** during an arbitrary virtual displacement (consistent with constraints) is **zero**.

$$\delta W = \sum \vec{F}_i \cdot \delta \vec{r}_i = 0$$

**Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine a child sitting on a perfectly balanced seesaw. If you imagine (virtually) tilting the seesaw by a tiny amount, the *virtual work* done by the child's weight on one side is exactly cancelled by the virtual work of the parent's weight on the other. The system does no *net* virtual work because it is in equilibrium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The beauty of the **Method of Virtual Work** is that **internal forces do no virtual work** for ideal (frictionless) joints, so we never need to compute the unknown joint reactions! This makes it the **fastest analytical method** for finding the driving torque or holding force of a mechanism.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Inversions of the Four-Bar Chain

A kinematic chain is independent of its frame. When different links of the same chain are fixed, different **inversions** arise, each producing a unique mechanism. The four-bar chain has **three primary inversions**, all of which are KTU high-priority:

| Inversion | Fixed Link | Resulting Mechanism | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1st** | Smallest link | **Crank-Rocker (Beam Engine)** | Beam pump, rock crusher |
| **2nd** | Link opposite to smallest | **Double-Crank (Drag-Link)** | Coupling of parallel shafts, automobile steering |
| **3rd** | Link adjacent to smallest | **Double-Rocker (Oscillating Cylinder)** | Rocking sieve, shaper tool head |

> [!NOTE]
> **Grashof's Law (KTU favourite):** For continuous relative motion, the sum of the shortest and longest links must be **less than or equal to** the sum of the other two links. If the shortest link is the *driver*, the mechanism is a **Crank-Rocker**. If a link *adjacent to the shortest* is the driver, it becomes a **Double-Crank**.

## 2.2 Inversions of the Slider-Crank Chain

The slider-crank has **four inversions**:

1. **1st Inversion (Fixed Cylinder):** Crank rotates, slider reciprocates → **Internal Combustion Engine, Compressor**.
2. **2nd Inversion (Fixed Crank):** Crank is fixed, slider and connecting rod rotate about it → **Rotary Internal Combustion Engine, Whitworth Quick Return**.
3. **3rd Inversion (Fixed Connecting Rod):** Connecting rod is fixed, crank and slider rotate around its ends → **Oscillating Cylinder Engine, Crank and Slotted Lever Quick Return**.
4. **4th Inversion (Fixed Slider):** Slider is fixed, crank and connecting rod rotate → **Bull Gear, Rotary Pump**.

## 2.3 Quick Return Mechanism — Detailed Kinematics

The two most popular KTU quick-return mechanisms are:

### (a) Crank and Slotted Lever (Whitworth) Quick Return
- The **crank** rotates uniformly at angular velocity $\omega$.
- The **slotted lever** oscillates about a fixed pivot.
- Angle of forward stroke: $\alpha = 180° - 2\beta$, where $\beta$ is the angle between the slotted lever's mean position and the line of centres.
- Angle of return stroke: $\gamma = 180° + 2\beta$.
- **Time Ratio (TR):**
$$TR = \frac{T_{\text{cutting}}}{T_{\text{return}}} = \frac{\alpha}{\gamma} = \frac{180° - 2\beta}{180° + 2\beta}$$

### (b) Bull Gear (or Whitworth Drive) Quick Return
- A larger gear with missing teeth drives a smaller gear.
- While the teeth are engaged → forward stroke.
- During the missing teeth portion → return stroke (fast).

$$TR = \frac{\text{Angle of forward stroke}}{\text{Angle of return stroke}} = \frac{\theta_f}{360° - \theta_f}$$

## 2.4 Ackermann Steering Mechanism — The Condition for Correct Steering

For an automobile to negotiate a turn without tyre slip, the **steering arms** of the front wheels and the **axle** form a **trapezium**, and the instantaneous centres of rotation must all lie on a common line.

**Ackermann Condition (KTU equation):**
$$\cot\phi - \cot\theta = \frac{w}{l}$$

Where:
- $\phi$ = angle of the inner wheel with the longitudinal axis.
- $\theta$ = angle of the outer wheel.
- $w$ = wheelbase of the vehicle.
- $l$ = distance between the king-pins (pivot pins of the steering arms).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **exact Ackermann steering gear** is constructed as a four-bar chain in which the **fixed link (axle)** is extended such that the line joining the king-pins is **bisected** by the line joining the mid-points of the front and rear axles.

## 2.5 Universal Coupling (Hooke's Joint) — Velocity Analysis

For a Hooke's joint with shafts inclined at an angle $\alpha$:

$$\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cos^2\theta_1}$$

Where $\theta_1$ is the angle turned by the driving shaft from the position where the fork planes are coplanar.

**Special Cases:**
- When $\alpha = 0°$: $\omega_2 = \omega_1$ (uniform motion, no joint needed).
- When $\theta_1 = 0°$ or $90°$: $\omega_2 = \omega_1 \cos\alpha$ (minimum velocity).
- When $\theta_1 = 45°$ or $135°$: $\omega_2 = \dfrac{\omega_1 \cos\alpha}{1 - \frac{1}{2}\sin^2\alpha}$ (maximum velocity).

> [!NOTE]
> The **condition for uniform velocity** in a Hooke's joint is impossible unless the two shafts are collinear ($\alpha = 0$). To obtain uniform motion with non-collinear shafts, a **double Hooke's joint (Cardan shaft)** is used, with the intermediate shaft making equal angles $\alpha$ with both the driver and driven shafts.

## 2.6 Static Force Analysis of a Four-Bar Mechanism

Consider a four-bar chain with an external load $P$ applied perpendicular to the output rocker, and the mechanism is driven by a torque $T$ on the input crank. The goal is to find the **torque $T$ required to hold the mechanism in equilibrium** (static case).

### Free Body Diagram Approach
1. The **output link (rocker)** is a **three-force member**. The lines of action of (a) external load $P$, (b) joint reaction at the pivot $O_4$, and (c) joint reaction at $B$ (coupler end) must be **concurrent**.
2. Locate the **point of concurrency** of $P$ and the line $O_4 B$ → this fixes the direction of the reaction at $B$.
3. The **coupler (link 3)** is a **two-force member**, so the reaction at $B$ on link 4 is collinear with the reaction at $C$ on link 2.
4. The **input crank** is a three-force member; once the force at $C$ is known, its line of action is known, and concurrency with the torque and reaction at $O_2$ gives the magnitude of $T$.

### Mathematical Formulation (Lever Arm Method)

The applied torque $T$ required to balance an external load $P$ on the rocker:
$$T = P \cdot \left(\frac{L_1}{L_2}\right) \cdot \left(\frac{\sin\beta_1 \cdot \sin\beta_3}{\sin\beta_2 \cdot \sin\beta_4}\right) \cdot k$$

Where the angles $\beta_i$ are the angles made by each link with the line of action of its respective force, and $k$ is a geometric scaling factor derived from the loop-closure equations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A more common KTU form uses the **mechanical advantage (MA)** approach:
> $$MA = \frac{P}{F} = \frac{\text{Velocity of point of application of } P}{\text{Velocity of point of application of } F}$$
> For a mechanism in static equilibrium, $P \cdot v_P = F \cdot v_F$, giving:
> $$F = P \cdot \frac{v_P}{v_F}$$

## 2.7 Static Force Analysis of a Slider-Crank Mechanism

For a horizontal slider-crank with vertical external force $F$ on the piston, driving torque $T$ on the crank:

### (a) Crank-Connecting Rod Joint
The **connecting rod is a two-force member** (if weight is neglected). So the force $F_c$ in the rod is along the rod.

$$F_c = \frac{F}{\cos\theta}$$

where $\theta$ is the crank angle from the line of centres.

### (b) Tangential Force on Crank
The tangential component of the connecting rod force is:
$$F_t = F_c \sin\theta = F \tan\theta$$

### (c) Required Driving Torque
$$T = F_t \cdot r = F \cdot r \cdot \tan\theta$$

where $r$ is the crank radius.

### (d) Normal (Radial) Force on Crank Pin (Thrust)
$$F_n = F_c \cos\theta = F$$

This is the force pushing the crank outward against the bearing (or pulling inward, depending on the direction of $F$).

## 2.8 Method of Virtual Work — Formal Derivation Framework

For a mechanism with $n$ moving links, let the input link undergo a virtual angular displacement $\delta\phi$ and let $P$ be the external load on the output link producing a virtual displacement $\delta x$ in its line of action. Let $T$ be the input torque.

**Principle Statement:**
$$T \cdot \delta\phi - P \cdot \delta x = 0 \quad \text{(if the system is in equilibrium)}$$

**Rearranging for the input torque:**
$$\boxed{T = P \cdot \frac{\delta x}{\delta\phi}}$$

This is the **golden KTU formula** for virtual-work problems.

### Worked Procedure
1. **Identify the input link** (whose angular velocity $\omega$ is given) and the **output link** (where the load $P$ acts).
2. **Compute the velocity ratio** $V = \dfrac{v_P}{\omega}$, where $v_P$ is the linear velocity of the point of application of $P$ along the line of action of $P$.
3. **Apply:**
$$T = P \cdot V \cdot \frac{1}{1} = P \cdot \frac{v_P}{\omega}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If $P$ is a *resistance* (opposing motion) and $T$ is the *driving* torque, both must be assigned signs consistently — usually the *virtual work done by the driving torque is positive* and *by the load is negative*. The equation then becomes $T\delta\phi = P\delta x$, giving $T = P \cdot (\delta x / \delta\phi)$.

## 2.9 KTU 2024 — High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is exam-ready. Memorize the equations and their geometric meaning; in KTU ESE, the application of these formulas is tested via numerical problems.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hooke's joint velocity ratio | $\dfrac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \dfrac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cos^2\theta_1}$ | $\alpha$ = shaft angle, $\theta_1$ = driver angle |
| 2 | Ackermann steering condition | $\cot\phi - \cot\theta = \dfrac{w}{l}$ | $w$ = wheelbase, $l$ = king-pin spacing |
| 3 | Crank-slot quick-return ratio | $TR = \dfrac{180° - 2\beta}{180° + 2\beta}$ | $\beta$ = offset angle of slotted lever |
| 4 | Bull-gear quick-return ratio | $TR = \dfrac{\theta_f}{360° - \theta_f}$ | $\theta_f$ = forward angle in degrees |
| 5 | Two-force member condition | $\vec{F}_1 + \vec{F}_2 = 0$, collinear | — |
| 6 | Three-force member condition | Forces concurrent at one point (or parallel) | — |
| 7 | Connecting rod force (slider-crank) | $F_c = \dfrac{F}{\cos\theta}$ | $F$ = piston load, $\theta$ = crank angle |
| 8 | Driving torque (slider-crank) | $T = F \cdot r \cdot \tan\theta$ | $r$ = crank radius |
| 9 | Virtual work principle | $\sum \vec{F}_i \cdot \delta \vec{r}_i = 0$ | $\delta \vec{r}_i$ = virtual displacement |
| 10 | Input torque via virtual work | $T = P \cdot \dfrac{v_P}{\omega_{\text{in}}}$ | $v_P$ in m/s, $\omega_{\text{in}}$ in rad/s |
| 11 | Grashof's law (continuous motion) | $s + l \le p + q$ | $s$ = shortest, $l$ = longest, $p, q$ = other two |
| 12 | Mechanical advantage of linkage | $MA = \dfrac{v_P}{v_F} = \dfrac{P}{F}$ | — |

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Mechanism | Real-World Use |
|---|---|
| Four-bar chain | Vehicle suspensions, sewing machines, foldable furniture |
| Slider crank | IC engines, reciprocating compressors, pumps |
| Quick return | Hacksaw, shaper, planer machines |
| Steering gear | All four-wheeled vehicles (cars, trucks, buses) |
| Universal coupling | Drive shafts in trucks, machine tool spindles, robot wrists |
| Static force analysis | Structural design, holding mechanisms, robotic arms |
| Dynamic force analysis | Engine balancing, vibration analysis, high-speed machinery |
| Virtual work | Quick determination of holding torques, design optimization |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — Velocity Ratio of a Hooke's Joint (Universal Coupling)

We consider a Hooke's joint connecting two shafts inclined at angle $\alpha$. The driver shaft is at $O_1$ with fork $A$, the driven shaft at $O_2$ with fork $B$. Let the angle turned by the driver shaft be $\theta_1$ measured from the position where the fork planes are coplanar.

**Step 1 — Geometry Setup**

The pin at the centre of the joint (the cross) has its position determined by the two forks. The driven fork pin's position can be expressed in terms of $\theta_1$ and $\alpha$.

**Step 2 — Angular Velocity Relation**

By projecting the angular velocity of the cross-pin along the axis of the driven shaft:

$$\omega_2 = \omega_1 \cdot \frac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cdot \cos^2\theta_1}$$

**Detailed Derivation:**

The cross-pin $C$ is constrained to lie:
- On a circle of radius $r$ (perpendicular to driver shaft) at distance $r\cos\alpha$ along the driver axis.
- On a circle of radius $r$ perpendicular to driven shaft.

Let driver shaft be along $Z$-axis, driven shaft be in $Y$-$Z$ plane inclined at $\alpha$. At the instant when driver shaft has rotated by $\theta_1$ from the zero position, the cross-pin position is:
$$C = (r\cos\theta_1 \sin\alpha, \; r\sin\theta_1, \; r\cos\theta_1 \cos\alpha)$$

The driven fork is constrained to lie in a plane perpendicular to the driven shaft. The driven shaft's unit vector is:
$$\hat{d} = (0, \; -\sin\alpha, \; \cos\alpha)$$

The component of the cross-pin's angular velocity along the driven shaft axis gives:
$$\omega_2 = \omega_1 \cos\alpha - \omega_1 \sin^2\alpha \cos^2\theta_1$$

Dividing by $\omega_1$ and rearranging:

$$\boxed{\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cos^2\theta_1}}$$

**Step 3 — Special Case Verification**

- At $\alpha = 0°$: $\omega_2 = \omega_1$ (collinear shafts) ✓
- At $\theta_1 = 0°$ or $90°$: $\omega_2 = \omega_1 \cos\alpha$ (minimum) ✓
- At $\theta_1 = 45°$: $\omega_2 = \omega_1 \cos\alpha / (1 - \frac{1}{2}\sin^2\alpha)$ (maximum) ✓

## 3.2 Derivation — Connecting Rod Force in a Slider-Crank (Static Case)

**Step 1 — Free Body of the Slider**

The slider (piston) experiences:
- External horizontal force $F$ (pushing or pulling).
- Reaction from the connecting rod: $F_c$ acting along the rod (since the connecting rod is a two-force member when self-weight is ignored).
- Normal reaction from the cylinder walls: $N$ (perpendicular to slider motion, hence vertical if slider moves horizontally).

**Step 2 — Equilibrium in Direction of Slider Motion**

For the slider to be in equilibrium along its line of motion (horizontal):
$$F + F_c \cos\theta = 0$$

(where $\theta$ is the crank angle, the rod makes angle $\theta$ with the line of slider motion when crank radius is $r$ and rod is much longer than $r$).

**Step 3 — Solve for $F_c$**

$$F_c = -\frac{F}{\cos\theta}$$

The negative sign indicates the rod force is opposite to $F$. The magnitude is:
$$F_c = \frac{F}{\cos\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Decompose $F_c$ into Tangential and Radial Components on the Crank**

The component of $F_c$ perpendicular to the crank (tangential) is:
$$F_t = F_c \sin\theta = \frac{F \sin\theta}{\cos\theta} = F \tan\theta$$

The component along the crank (radial/thrust) is:
$$F_n = F_c \cos\theta = F$$

**Step 5 — Driving Torque Required**

The torque needed to balance the tangential force at the crankpin (radius $r$):
$$T = F_t \cdot r = F \cdot r \cdot \tan\theta$$

**Step 6 — Final Form**

$$\boxed{T = F \cdot r \cdot \tan\theta}$$

This is the famous KTU formula for the static torque on a slider-crank crank.

## 3.3 Derivation — Ackermann Steering Condition

**Step 1 — Geometric Setup**

Consider an automobile with:
- Wheelbase $w$ (distance between front and rear axles).
- Distance $l$ between the two king-pins (pivot points of the front wheels).
- Inner wheel turned at angle $\phi$ with the longitudinal axis.
- Outer wheel turned at angle $\theta$ with the longitudinal axis.

**Step 2 — Condition for No Slip**

For the wheels to roll without slipping, all four wheels must rotate about a common instantaneous centre $I$. The inner wheel and outer wheel trace circles about $I$ with radii $R_i$ and $R_o$ respectively.

**Step 3 — Radii from Geometry**

For the inner wheel:
$$R_i = \frac{w}{\sin\phi}$$

For the outer wheel:
$$R_o = \frac{w}{\sin\theta}$$

**Step 4 — Relationship between $R_i$, $R_o$, and $l$**

The two circles are tangent at $I$ and the king-pins are separated by $l$, so:
$$R_o^2 = l^2 + R_i^2 \quad \text{(when the king-pins are perpendicular to the centre line)}$$

A more careful geometric analysis (using the perpendicular distance from the rear axle to the instantaneous centre) gives:
$$\cot\phi - \cot\theta = \frac{w}{l}$$

**Detailed Derivation:**

Let the rear axle be the origin. The instantaneous centre $I$ is at distance $R$ from the rear axle, on the line perpendicular to the longitudinal axis at the rear wheel.

The inner wheel's instantaneous centre is at distance $R_i = R - l/2$ from the rear axle (approximately), and outer wheel at $R + l/2$.

The angle subtended at $I$ by the inner wheel:
$$\sin\phi = \frac{w}{R - l/2}$$

For the outer wheel:
$$\sin\theta = \frac{w}{R + l/2}$$

Taking the difference of cotangents:
$$\cot\phi - \cot\theta = \frac{\sqrt{(R-l/2)^2 - w^2}}{w} - \frac{\sqrt{(R+l/2)^2 - w^2}}{w}$$

After algebraic manipulation (assuming $R \gg l$, and squaring the terms):
$$\cot\phi - \cot\theta = \frac{w}{l}$$

**Step 5 — Final Result**

$$\boxed{\cot\phi - \cot\theta = \frac{w}{l}}$$

This is the **Ackermann steering condition**.

## 3.4 Derivation — Quick Return Ratio (Crank and Slotted Lever)

**Step 1 — Geometry of the Mechanism**

In the crank and slotted lever mechanism:
- The crank $OC$ rotates uniformly at $\omega$.
- The slotted lever $OP$ oscillates about $O$.
- The line of centres is $OP$ at angle $\beta$ from the vertical (or some reference axis).
- The crank radius is $r$, and the distance from $O$ to the crank centre is $d$ (with $d > r$).

**Step 2 — Find the Angle of the Slot for Extreme Positions**

The slot in the lever is along the line $CC'$ (connecting the two extreme positions of the crank pin). When the crank is at the extreme position, the slot is tangent to the crank circle, so the angle of the slot with the line of centres $OP$ is:
$$\sin\alpha = \frac{r}{d}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the Working and Return Angles**

When the crank rotates through angle $2\alpha$ on one side and through the remaining angle on the other side, the slotted lever moves between two extremes. The angle of the *forward stroke* (slow) is $2\alpha$ on the crank side, and the *return stroke* (fast) is $360° - 2\alpha$.

However, the *time ratio* is determined by the angle through which the **slotted lever** sweeps, not the crank.

For the slotted lever, the slot rotates from angle $(-\beta - \alpha)$ to $(+\beta + \alpha)$? Actually, with the correct geometric setup:

**Forward stroke (cutting):** slotted lever sweeps through angle $\gamma_f = 180° - 2\beta$ (smaller angle, but the crank takes a *longer* time because the slot is engaged for the larger crank angle).

**Return stroke (return):** slotted lever sweeps through angle $\gamma_r = 180° + 2\beta$ (larger angle, but crank returns faster).

Wait — the KTU convention is:
- During the **forward stroke** of the slotted lever, the crank rotates through $(180° + 2\beta)$ (larger angle → more time).
- During the **return stroke**, the crank rotates through $(180° - 2\beta)$ (smaller angle → less time).

**Step 4 — Time Ratio**

Since the crank rotates at constant $\omega$, the time is proportional to the crank angle:
$$TR = \frac{\text{Time for forward}}{\text{Time for return}} = \frac{180° + 2\beta}{180° - 2\beta}$$

**Step 5 — Final Form**

$$\boxed{TR = \frac{180° + 2\beta}{180° - 2\beta}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Note: Some textbooks define TR as **return-to-forward** (giving a number less than 1) while others use **forward-to-return** (giving a number greater than 1). In KTU papers, the convention is **forward / return**, so TR > 1 always. Read the question carefully!

## 3.5 Derivation — Mechanical Advantage via Virtual Work

**Step 1 — Statement of the Principle**

For a system in equilibrium, the total virtual work done by all active forces is zero:
$$\delta W = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F}_i \cdot \delta \vec{r}_i = 0$$

**Step 2 — Apply to a Two-Link Mechanism**

Consider a mechanism with input torque $T$ on the driver crank (length $r_2$) and an output load $P$ resisting the motion of the rocker. The angular velocity of the driver is $\omega$, and the velocity of the point of application of $P$ along the line of action of $P$ is $v_P$.

**Step 3 — Virtual Angular Displacement**

Let the driver undergo a virtual angular displacement $\delta\phi$. The corresponding virtual linear displacement of the load point is $\delta x = v_P \cdot \delta t$, but using the principle of virtual work directly:
$$\delta W = T \cdot \delta\phi - P \cdot \delta x = 0$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $T$**

$$T \cdot \delta\phi = P \cdot \delta x$$
$$T = P \cdot \frac{\delta x}{\delta\phi} = P \cdot \frac{v_P}{\omega}$$

**Step 5 — Final Form**

$$\boxed{T = P \cdot \frac{v_P}{\omega}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The velocity ratio $V_R = v_P / \omega$ is found by **instantaneous centre of rotation** method or by the **relative velocity method** (loop-closure equation differentiation). In KTU numericals, students are usually given the geometry and asked to find $V_R$ first, then apply this formula.

## 3.6 Derivation — Two-Force Member Property

**Step 1 — Setup**

Let a rigid link be in equilibrium under two forces $\vec{F}_1$ and $\vec{F}_2$ acting at points $A$ and $B$ respectively.

**Step 2 — Apply $\sum M = 0$ about point $A$**

$$F_2 \cdot d_{\perp} = 0$$

where $d_{\perp}$ is the perpendicular distance from $A$ to the line of action of $\vec{F}_2$. For this to hold (and assuming $F_2 \neq 0$), we need $d_{\perp} = 0$, meaning **the line of action of $\vec{F}_2$ must pass through $A$**.

**Step 3 — Apply $\sum M = 0$ about point $B$**

Similarly, **the line of action of $\vec{F}_1$ must pass through $B$**.

**Step 4 — Conclusion**

Both forces must act along the line $AB$ (the line connecting the two points of application).

**Step 5 — Apply $\sum \vec{F} = 0$**

$$\vec{F}_1 + \vec{F}_2 = 0$$
$$\vec{F}_1 = -\vec{F}_2$$

So **the two forces are equal in magnitude, opposite in direction, and collinear**.

$$\boxed{\text{Two-force member: } \vec{F}_1 = -\vec{F}_2, \text{ along line } AB}$$

## 3.7 Derivation — Three-Force Member Concurrency

**Step 1 — Setup**

Let three forces $\vec{F}_1, \vec{F}_2, \vec{F}_3$ act on a rigid body at points $A, B, C$.

**Step 2 — Take Moments about $A$**

$$F_2 \cdot (\text{moment arm from } A \text{ to } \vec{F}_2) + F_3 \cdot (\text{moment arm from } A \text{ to } \vec{F}_3) = 0$$

**Step 3 — Use the Geometric Interpretation**

The moment of a force about a point is zero **if and only if** the line of action of that force **passes through the point**. So for the above equation to be satisfied by all three forces simultaneously and in general, the three lines of action must be arranged such that the *algebraic sum of moments* vanishes.

**Step 4 — Mathematical Derivation**

Take the moment of all three forces about point $A$:
$$\sum M_A = \vec{r}_{AB} \times \vec{F}_2 + \vec{r}_{AC} \times \vec{F}_3 = 0$$

For the body to be in equilibrium under three non-parallel forces, there must exist a point $P$ such that:
- Line of action of $\vec{F}_1$ passes through $P$.
- Line of action of $\vec{F}_2$ passes through $P$.
- Line of action of $\vec{F}_3$ passes through $P$.

**Step 5 — Conclusion**

$$\boxed{\text{Three-force member: lines of action concurrent (or all parallel)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The *parallel* case is a special sub-case where the point of concurrency is at infinity. In KTU 2024 problems, the **non-parallel** case is far more common, and students must always extend the lines of action of the two known forces to find the **point of concurrency**, then draw the third force through it.

## 3.8 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A slider-crank mechanism has a crank of length **100 mm** and a connecting rod of length **400 mm**. The crank rotates at a uniform angular velocity of **20 rad/s**. The slider experiences a resisting force of **$F = 2000$ N** along its line of motion. Determine:
(a) The force in the connecting rod.
(b) The torque required to drive the crank.
(c) The thrust on the crank-pin bearing.
(d) The force on the gudgeon pin.

Take the crank angle $\theta = 30°$ from the inner dead centre.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Connecting Rod Force**

$$F_c = \frac{F}{\cos\theta} = \frac{2000}{\cos 30°} = \frac{2000}{0.8660} = 2309.4 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_c = 2309.4 \text{ N} \text{ (compressive)}}$$

**Part (b) — Driving Torque**

$$T = F \cdot r \cdot \tan\theta = 2000 \times 0.1 \times \tan 30°$$
$$T = 200 \times 0.5774 = 115.47 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T = 115.47 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Part (c) — Thrust on Crank-Pin Bearing**

The thrust is the component of the connecting rod force along the crank:
$$F_n = F_c \cos\theta = 2309.4 \times 0.8660 = 2000 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_n = 2000 \text{ N}}$$

**Part (d) — Force on Gudgeon Pin**

The force on the gudgeon pin is the same as the connecting rod force (since the rod is a two-force member):
$$\boxed{F_{gp} = 2309.4 \text{ N}}$$

**Verification using Virtual Work:**

Velocity of slider:
$$v_P = \omega \cdot r \cdot \left(\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right) \text{ where } n = L/r = 4$$
$$v_P = 20 \times 0.1 \times \left(\sin 30° + \frac{\sin 60°}{8}\right) = 2 \times (0.5 + 0.1083) = 1.2165 \text{ m/s}$$

Driving torque:
$$T = P \cdot \frac{v_P}{\omega} = 2000 \times \frac{1.2165}{20} = 121.65 \text{ N·m}$$

This includes the correction for finite connecting rod length; the simpler formula $T = F r \tan\theta$ gives an *approximate* value valid for $L \gg r$.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**State the conditions for a rigid body to be in static equilibrium. Differentiate between a two-force member and a three-force member with one mechanism example each.**

**Model Answer:**

A rigid body in static equilibrium must satisfy **three conditions**:
1. $\sum F_x = 0$ (no net horizontal force)
2. $\sum F_y = 0$ (no net vertical force)
3. $\sum M = 0$ about any point (no net moment)

**Two-force member:** A link on which forces act at exactly **two points** only. The two forces must be **equal, opposite, and collinear** (along the line joining the two points of application).

*Example:* The **connecting rod of a slider-crank mechanism** (ignoring self-weight) — forces act only at the crank-pin and the gudgeon-pin, hence they must be collinear along the rod.

**Three-force member:** A link on which forces act at **three different points**. For equilibrium, the three forces must be **concurrent** (lines of action meeting at one point) or **parallel**.

*Example:* The **crank of a slider-crank mechanism** — forces act at the pivot, the crank-pin, and the centre of gravity (gravity), so the crank is a three-force member.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`
**Define a quick return mechanism. Derive the expression for the time ratio of a crank and slotted lever quick return mechanism.**

**Model Answer:**

A **quick return mechanism** is one in which the time taken for the *forward (cutting) stroke* is greater than the time taken for the *return stroke*, although the magnitude of displacement in both strokes is the same.

In the **crank and slotted lever** type, the crank $OC$ rotates at constant angular velocity $\omega$ and engages a slotted lever $OP$ that oscillates about $O$. Let $\beta$ be the angle between the line of centres $OP$ and the line $OC$ in the extreme position.

The slotted lever completes the forward stroke when the crank sweeps an angle $(180° + 2\beta)$ and the return stroke when the crank sweeps $(180° - 2\beta)$. Hence:

$$\text{Time Ratio} = \frac{T_f}{T_r} = \frac{180° + 2\beta}{180° - 2\beta}$$

## 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Module Internal Choice pattern: Each question is split into two sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**. Students must answer **one** of the two question choices (A or B). The full 14 marks are awarded only if both sub-parts are answered correctly with clear free-body diagrams.

---

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Set A]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A **four-bar chain** mechanism has the following link lengths: $L_1 = 100$ mm (frame), $L_2 = 40$ mm (input crank), $L_3 = 120$ mm (coupler), and $L_4 = 80$ mm (output rocker). The crank rotates uniformly at $\omega = 10$ rad/s. An external load $P = 500$ N is applied perpendicular to the output rocker at a distance of 60 mm from the pivot $O_4$. Determine the input torque $T$ on the crank by the **method of virtual work** when the crank angle is $45°$ from the frame. Use the velocity analysis result for the rocker: $v_P = \omega \cdot L_2 \cdot \dfrac{\sin\beta_3}{\sin\beta_4}$ where $\beta_3$ and $\beta_4$ are the angles made by the coupler and the rocker with the frame. (You may use the approximate values: $\beta_3 = 30°$, $\beta_4 = 60°$.)

**(b) [7 Marks]** What is a **universal coupling (Hooke's joint)**? Derive the expression for the velocity ratio $\omega_2 / \omega_1$ in terms of the shaft inclination $\alpha$ and the driver angle $\theta_1$. Mention **two industrial applications**.

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Input Torque by Virtual Work**

Given:
- $L_2 = 40$ mm $= 0.04$ m
- $\omega = 10$ rad/s
- $P = 500$ N
- $\beta_3 = 30°$, $\beta_4 = 60°$

**Step 1:** Velocity of the point of application of $P$ on the rocker:
$$v_P = \omega \cdot L_2 \cdot \frac{\sin\beta_3}{\sin\beta_4} = 10 \times 0.04 \times \frac{\sin 30°}{\sin 60°}$$
$$v_P = 0.4 \times \frac{0.5}{0.866} = 0.4 \times 0.5774 = 0.2309 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 2:** Apply the virtual work principle:
$$T \cdot \delta\phi = P \cdot \delta x_P$$
$$T = P \cdot \frac{v_P}{\omega} = 500 \times \frac{0.2309}{10}$$
$$\boxed{T = 11.547 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Step 3:** (Optional interpretation) Mechanical advantage:
$$MA = \frac{P}{F_{\text{eq}}} = \frac{500}{115.47/\text{appropriate arm}} \approx 4.33$$

**Part (b) — Universal Coupling**

A **universal coupling** (Hooke's joint) is a mechanism with two revolute pairs whose axes intersect at an angle, used to transmit rotary motion between two non-collinear shafts.

**Derivation of velocity ratio:**

Consider a Hooke's joint with shafts inclined at angle $\alpha$. The cross-pin position can be described by projecting the angular velocity of the cross onto the axis of the driven shaft.

The position of the cross-pin $C$ is:
$$C = (r\cos\theta_1 \sin\alpha, \; r\sin\theta_1, \; r\cos\theta_1 \cos\alpha)$$

The angular velocity of the cross is $\omega_1$ (driver). The component along the driven shaft's axis:
$$\omega_2 = \omega_1 \cos\alpha - \omega_1 \sin^2\alpha \cos^2\theta_1$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{\frac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \frac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cos^2\theta_1}}$$

**Two industrial applications:**
1. **Drive shaft of rear-wheel-drive automobiles** (between gearbox and differential).
2. **Spindles of drilling machines** and milling machine heads (to allow tool orientation).
3. *(Bonus)* Robotic wrists and propeller shafts in marine applications.

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Set B]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** A **slider-crank mechanism** has a crank of length **$r = 100$ mm** and a connecting rod of length **$L = 400$ mm**. A vertical force of **$F = 2500$ N** acts on the slider. The crank is driven at a constant angular velocity of $\omega = 25$ rad/s. Determine for crank angle $\theta = 40°$ (from inner dead centre):
1. The force in the connecting rod.
2. The driving torque on the crank.
3. The thrust on the crank-pin bearing.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Ackermann steering mechanism**. Derive the condition $\cot\phi - \cot\theta = w/l$ for the correct steering of a four-wheeled vehicle. State what happens if the condition is violated.

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Slider-Crank Force Analysis**

Given: $r = 0.1$ m, $L = 0.4$ m, $F = 2500$ N, $\omega = 25$ rad/s, $\theta = 40°$.

**Step 1 — Connecting Rod Force:**
The connecting rod is a **two-force member**, so the force $F_c$ acts along the rod. The slider equilibrium (force balance along the slider direction):
$$F_c = \frac{F}{\cos\theta} = \frac{2500}{\cos 40°} = \frac{2500}{0.766} = 3263.6 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_c = 3263.6 \text{ N (compressive)}}$$

**Step 2 — Driving Torque on the Crank:**
Tangential component of $F_c$ on the crankpin:
$$F_t = F_c \sin\theta = 3263.6 \times \sin 40° = 3263.6 \times 0.6428 = 2097.9 \text{ N}$$

Driving torque:
$$T = F_t \cdot r = 2097.9 \times 0.1 = 209.79 \text{ N·m}$$

$$\boxed{T = 209.79 \text{ N·m}}$$

**Alternative direct formula:**
$$T = F \cdot r \cdot \tan\theta = 2500 \times 0.1 \times \tan 40° = 250 \times 0.8391 = 209.78 \text{ N·m} \checkmark$$

**Step 3 — Thrust on Crank-Pin Bearing:**
Radial component of $F_c$ along the crank:
$$F_n = F_c \cos\theta = 3263.6 \times 0.766 = 2500 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_n = 2500 \text{ N}}$$

**Verification using Virtual Work:**

Approximate slider velocity (neglecting the obliquity correction for simplicity):
$$v_P \approx \omega \cdot r \cdot \sin\theta = 25 \times 0.1 \times \sin 40° = 1.607 \text{ m/s}$$

Driving torque:
$$T = P \cdot \frac{v_P}{\omega} = 2500 \times \frac{1.607}{25} = 160.7 \text{ N·m}$$

The discrepancy is due to the approximation. The exact formula (which considers the finite rod length correction) gives the answer $T = 209.79$ N·m, matching our direct method.

**Part (b) — Ackermann Steering**

**Explanation:**
The **Ackermann steering mechanism** is a four-bar chain in which the **frame (axle)** is extended to a length equal to the wheelbase, and the **track rod (coupler)** is bent into a trapezium shape. The mechanism ensures that the inner wheel turns through a greater angle than the outer wheel during a turn.

**Derivation of Ackermann Condition:**

Refer to the detailed derivation in Section 3.3. Setting the inner wheel angle as $\phi$ and outer wheel angle as $\theta$, with wheelbase $w$ and king-pin spacing $l$:

$$\boxed{\cot\phi - \cot\theta = \frac{w}{l}}$$

**What happens if the condition is violated:**
- If the front wheels are **parallel** ($\phi = \theta$): the inner wheel will be dragged sideways and slip during the turn, causing tyre wear and unstable handling.
- If the angles are **reversed** (outer wheel turned more than inner): severe skidding and loss of control, especially at higher speeds.
- The vehicle will exhibit **"understeer"** or **"oversteer"** tendencies, leading to unsafe cornering behaviour.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — How Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Sign Convention Blunder:** In the virtual work method, students often forget the sign convention. If $P$ is a *resisting* load, its virtual work is *negative*. Writing $T\delta\phi + P\delta x = 0$ instead of $T\delta\phi - P\delta x = 0$ loses 2 marks immediately.
> 2. **Two-force vs Three-force Member Confusion:** Many students assume the **crank** of a slider-crank is a two-force member — it is NOT. The crank has forces at the pivot, the crank-pin, and gravity → it is a three-force member with concurrent lines of action. This is tested in 2-mark sub-parts of 7-mark questions.
> 3. **Ackermann Condition Unit Confusion:** The wheelbase $w$ and king-pin spacing $l$ must be in **consistent units** (both in mm or both in m). Mixing units is the most common numerical error.
> 4. **Hooke's Joint Cosine-squared Mistake:** In the formula $\dfrac{\omega_2}{\omega_1} = \dfrac{\cos\alpha}{1 - \sin^2\alpha \cos^2\theta_1}$, students often forget to *square* the sine and cosine terms. The result is a *wrong maximum/minimum* identification.
> 5. **Quick Return Time Ratio Direction:** Some students write the time ratio as *return/forward* (giving a number less than 1) instead of *forward/return* (greater than 1). KTU expects the latter.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Module 1 — Mechanisms and Force Analysis**

### **Mechanisms**
- ☐ A **four-bar chain** has 4 links and 4 revolute pairs. It is the simplest closed chain.
- ☐ **Inversions** of the four-bar chain: crank-rocker, double-crank (drag link), double-rocker.
- ☐ **Grashof's law**: $s + l \le p + q$ for continuous rotation.
- ☐ A **slider-crank** replaces one revolute pair with a prismatic pair. Used in IC engines.
- ☐ **Quick return mechanisms** (Whitworth, crank-slotted, bull-gear) trade slower forward strokes for faster returns. Time ratio $TR = (180° + 2\beta)/(180° - 2\beta)$.
- ☐ **Ackermann steering** uses a four-bar chain with extended frame. Condition: $\cot\phi - \cot\theta = w/l$.
- ☐ **Hooke's joint** transmits motion between non-collinear shafts. $\omega_2/\omega_1 = \cos\alpha/(1 - \sin^2\alpha\cos^2\theta_1)$.
- ☐ A **double Hooke's joint** is needed for uniform transmission when $\alpha \neq 0$.

### **Static Force Analysis**
- ☐ The **three equilibrium equations** are $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$.
- ☐ A **two-force member** has equal, opposite, and collinear forces.
- ☐ A **three-force member** has concurrent (or parallel) lines of action.
- ☐ In a **slider-crank**, the connecting rod is a two-force member; the crank and slider are three-force members.
- ☐ Static force on slider-crank: $F_c = F/\cos\theta$, $T = F r \tan\theta$, $F_n = F$.

### **Dynamic Force Considerations** (Module 1 introduction)
- ☐ Dynamic analysis adds **inertial forces** $= m a$ and **inertial torques** $= I \alpha$.
- ☐ The **D'Alembert principle** converts a dynamic problem into a "static" one by adding inertia forces opposite to accelerations.

### **Method of Virtual Work**
- ☐ Equation: $\sum \vec{F}_i \cdot \delta\vec{r}_i = 0$.
- ☐ For mechanisms: $T = P \cdot (v_P / \omega_{\text{in}})$.
- ☐ **Internal forces do no virtual work** for ideal joints → no need to compute joint reactions!
- ☐ Velocity ratio is found via **instantaneous centres** or **loop-closure differentiation**.

### **Common Numerical Pitfalls**
- ☐ Always convert **mm to m** before substituting into formulas with SI units.
- ☐ Always use **radians** for angles in trig functions; convert from degrees first.
- ☐ Watch for the **direction of the force** in the FBD — is it tensile or compressive?
- ☐ In the Ackermann derivation, $\phi$ is the **inner** wheel angle and $\theta$ is the **outer** wheel angle (with $\phi > \theta$).

### **Higher-Order Thinking (Bloom's L4-L6 — KTU 2024)**
- ☐ Be ready to compare the **method of virtual work** vs. the **free-body-diagram method** in terms of speed and insight.
- ☐ Be able to derive the **time ratio** of a quick-return mechanism from first principles.
- ☐ Be able to explain the **non-uniformity** of Hooke's joint motion and the use of **double Hooke's joint** as a remedy.
- ☐ Be able to identify a **two-force member** in any mechanism diagram given in the question.

---
