topics.title: Force & Moment Equilibrium, Inertial Forces, D'Alembert's Principle; Dynamic Analysis of Four-Bar and Slider-Crank Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET413 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Force & Moment Equilibrium, Inertial Forces, D'Alembert's Principle; Dynamic Analysis of Four-Bar and Slider-Crank Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Force & Moment Equilibrium in Machinery

**Static Force Analysis** is performed on a mechanism assuming all members are in equilibrium under the action of external forces, neglecting inertia effects. In reality, when a mechanism operates at finite speeds, every link experiences acceleration, which produces additional **inertia forces and inertia couples**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Equilibrium Condition (Dynamic):** For a rigid body in dynamic equilibrium,
> $$\sum \vec{F}_{ext} + \sum \vec{F}_{inertia} = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum \vec{M}_{ext} + \sum \vec{M}_{inertia} = 0$$

The **moment equilibrium** of a link of mass $m$ and mass moment of inertia $I_G$ about its center of gravity $G$ is:
$$\sum M_G = I_G \cdot \alpha$$

## 1.2 Inertial Forces — Definition

An **Inertia Force** is a fictitious force, equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the product of mass and linear acceleration of the center of gravity of the moving body.

$$\vec{F}_I = -m \cdot \vec{a}_G$$

An **Inertia Couple (Moment)** is a fictitious couple, equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the product of mass moment of inertia and angular acceleration of the body.

$$\vec{M}_I = -I_G \cdot \vec{\alpha}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Negative sign convention:** The inertia force *opposes* the direction of acceleration. If the center of gravity accelerates to the right, the inertia force on the body points to the left.

## 1.3 D'Alembert's Principle

**D'Alembert's Principle** (1743) converts a dynamic problem of motion into an equivalent static problem by introducing the concept of inertia force.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Statement:** "The sum of the applied forces, the inertia forces, and the couples acting on a body in motion is equal to zero at every instant; i.e., the system is in dynamic equilibrium under the action of these forces."

Mathematically, for a system of particles:
$$\vec{F}_{applied} - m\vec{a} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vec{F}_{applied} + \vec{F}_{inertia} = 0$$

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Braking Sensation"

Imagine sitting in a car that suddenly accelerates forward. You feel pushed *backward* into the seat. That "pushing back" sensation is the **inertia force** your body experiences — it is the body's resistance to the change in its state of motion. In machinery, every link undergoing acceleration similarly "resists" the motion, and this resistance must be accounted for when computing the loads on bearings, pins, and the frame. D'Alembert's principle allows us to treat these dynamic resistances as if they were static loads, so we can use our familiar **statics equilibrium equations** ($\sum F = 0$, $\sum M = 0$) on moving machinery.

## 1.4 Dynamic Analysis — Why Is It Needed?

| Purpose | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|
| Bearing & Pin Design | Compute actual dynamic bearing reactions (often much higher than static) |
| Flywheel Sizing | Determine required input torque fluctuations |
| Frame / Bedplate Design | Total load transferred to machine frame |
| Vibration Analysis | Identify unbalanced inertia forces causing shake |
| Shaking Forces | Calculate force transmitted to the foundation |

**Standard SI units used in this module:**
- Force: **N (Newton)**
- Mass: **kg (kilogram)**
- Acceleration: **m/s²**
- Moment: **N·m**
- Mass moment of inertia: **kg·m²**
- Angular acceleration: **rad/s²**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Procedure for Dynamic Force Analysis (Step-by-Step Logic)

The standard KTU-board procedure for dynamic analysis of any mechanism is:

1. **Kinematic Analysis First** → Determine the velocity and acceleration of every center of gravity $G_i$ of each link, and the angular velocity $\omega_i$ and angular acceleration $\alpha_i$ of each link. (Usually done using relative velocity/acceleration or instant center method.)
2. **Compute Inertia Force and Inertia Couple** of each link:
   - $\vec{F}_{Ii} = -m_i \vec{a}_{Gi}$
   - $\vec{M}_{Ii} = -I_{Gi} \alpha_i$
3. **Replace Moving Links by Equivalent Dynamic Systems** — Each link is shown with its external forces, weight, and the inertia force and couple applied at the center of gravity.
4. **Apply D'Alembert's Principle** — Treat the system as in equilibrium.
5. **Free Body Diagram (FBD) of Each Link** — Write $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$ for every link.
6. **Solve the System of Equations** — Normally starting from the simplest link (no inertia, e.g., the slider), proceeding back to the input link.
7. **Find the Input Torque / Shaking Force** required for equilibrium.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this sequence works:** D'Alembert's principle *fictionalizes* inertia as an external load. Once this is done, the moving mechanism obeys the same equations as a static structure. We just need to know the kinematics first because the inertia load *depends* on acceleration, which is a kinematic quantity.

## 2.2 Sign Conventions & Direction Rules (KTU Standard)

| Quantity | Direction Rule |
|---|---|
| $\vec{F}_I$ | Opposite to $\vec{a}_G$ |
| $\vec{M}_I$ | Opposite to $\vec{\alpha}$ |
| Centripetal Component of $\vec{a}_G$ | Directed from $G$ toward the instant center of rotation of that link |
| Tangential Component of $\vec{a}_G$ | Perpendicular to $G–I_C$ line, in direction of $\vec{\alpha}$ |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

### General Dynamic Equilibrium

| # | Equation | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sum \vec{F} + \sum \vec{F}_I = 0$ | Force equilibrium with inertia |
| 2 | $\sum \vec{M} + \sum \vec{M}_I = 0$ | Moment equilibrium with inertia |
| 3 | $F_I = m \cdot a_G$ | Magnitude of inertia force |
| 4 | $M_I = I_G \cdot \alpha$ | Magnitude of inertia couple |
| 5 | $I_G = m \cdot k_G^2$ | Radius of gyration relation |
| 6 | $\alpha = \dfrac{d\omega}{dt} = \dfrac{d^2\theta}{dt^2}$ | Angular acceleration |

### Four-Bar Mechanism — Dynamic Force Relations

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Inertia force of coupler | $F_{I3} = m_3 a_{G3}$, direction $\perp$ to $G_3 I_{13}$ (centripetal) and $\perp$ (tangential) |
| Inertia couple of coupler | $M_{I3} = I_{G3} \alpha_3$ |
| Force on pin at $C$ (coupler-rocker) | $F_{RC}, F_{RC}'$ — equal & opposite on two links |
| Input torque on crank | $T_2 = F_t \cdot L_2$ where $F_t$ is tangential force at pin $A$ |

### Slider-Crank Mechanism — Piston Effort and Side Thrust

| Quantity | Formula |
|---|---|
| Piston inertia force | $F_{Ip} = m_p \cdot a_p$ |
| Piston weight component (vertical engine) | $W_p$ along axis of cylinder |
| Net force along cylinder axis | $F_N = F_{Ip} + W_p \pm F_{gas}$ |
| Side thrust (perpendicular to cylinder axis) | $F_S = F_N \cdot \tan\phi$ |
| Thrust in connecting rod | $F_Q = \dfrac{F_N}{\cos\phi}$ |
| Angle of inclination of connecting rod | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{r \sin\theta}{L + r \cos\theta}$ |
| Torque on crank | $T = F_t \cdot r$ where $F_t = F_N \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}$ |
| Turning moment | $T = F_N \cdot r \left( \sin\theta + \dfrac{r \sin 2\theta}{2\sqrt{L^2 - r^2 \sin^2\theta}} \right)$ (approx.) |
| Crank effort | $F_t = F_N \left( \sin\theta + \dfrac{r \sin 2\theta}{2(L + r \cos\theta)} \right)$ |
| Crank position for max/min torque | $\dfrac{dT}{d\theta} = 0$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to remember:**
> - At $\theta = 0°$ (TDC) and $\theta = 180°$ (BDC): $\sin\theta = 0$ → $T = 0$ (ideal case).
> - At $\theta = 90°$: $\sin\theta = 1$ → **maximum** tangential force in absence of approximation.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **IC Engines**: Slider-crank dynamic analysis determines the crank-pin bearing load, the side thrust on the cylinder wall, and the fluctuating torque on the crankshaft (which dictates flywheel sizing).
- **Quick-Return Mechanisms**: Four-bar and Whitworth/drag-link mechanisms are dynamically analyzed to find the shaking force transmitted to the frame.
- **Industrial Machinery**: Printing presses, packaging machines, and textile machinery — dynamic analysis prevents premature failure of bearings and structural members.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of D'Alembert's Principle

**Starting Point — Newton's Second Law** for a particle of mass $m$ acted on by applied force $\vec{F}$:

$$
\vec{F} = m \vec{a}
$$

Rearranging — bringing the inertial term to the LHS:

$$
\vec{F} - m\vec{a} = 0
$$

Define the **inertia force** as:

$$
\vec{F}_I = -m\vec{a}
$$

Then:

$$
\vec{F} + \vec{F}_I = 0
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The applied force is exactly balanced by a "fictitious" inertia force. This is the dynamic equilibrium form of Newton's law, valid for *every* instant of motion. The same logic extends to a rigid body by adding the inertia couple $-I_G \alpha$ to the moment equation.

## 3.2 Inertia Force Decomposition — Tangential and Centripetal

For a link rotating about an instantaneous center $I$ with angular velocity $\omega$ and angular acceleration $\alpha$, the acceleration of its center of gravity $G$ (at distance $L_{GI}$ from $I$) has two components:

$$
a_{G,\text{centripetal}} = \omega^2 L_{GI} \quad \text{(directed from } G \text{ toward } I\text{)}
$$

$$
a_{G,\text{tangential}} = \alpha L_{GI} \quad \text{(perpendicular to } G\text{–}I\text{ line)}
$$

Therefore, the total inertia force of the link can be resolved as:

$$
F_{Ic} = m \omega^2 L_{GI} \quad \text{(centripetal, away from } I\text{)}
$$

$$
F_{It} = m \alpha L_{GI} \quad \text{(tangential, opposite to } \vec{\alpha} \text{ direction)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb (KLU/KTU marking scheme):** Draw $F_{Ic}$ pointing **outward** from $I$ through $G$. Draw $F_{It}$ perpendicular to $GI$, in the direction **opposite** to that of $\alpha$.

## 3.3 Dynamic Analysis of a Four-Bar Mechanism (General Procedure)

Consider an **inverted slider-crank / four-bar linkage** with crank $AB$ (length $r_1$), coupler $BC$ (length $r_2$), and rocker $CD$ (length $r_3$), and a fixed link $AD$.

**Step 1 — Kinematic Analysis** (already done from Module 1 kinematics):
- Angular velocity of crank: $\omega_2$
- Angular velocity of coupler: $\omega_3$
- Angular velocity of rocker: $\omega_4$
- Angular accelerations: $\alpha_2, \alpha_3, \alpha_4$
- Acceleration of center of gravity of coupler: $a_{G3}$
- Acceleration of center of gravity of rocker: $a_{G4}$

**Step 2 — Inertia Loads of Moving Links**:
For the **coupler (link 3)** of mass $m_3$ and $I_{G3}$:

$$
F_{I3} = m_3 a_{G3} \quad \text{(along the line opposite to } \vec{a}_{G3}\text{)}
$$

$$
M_{I3} = I_{G3} \alpha_3 \quad \text{(opposite to } \alpha_3\text{)}
$$

For the **rocker (link 4)** of mass $m_4$ and $I_{G4}$:

$$
F_{I4} = m_4 a_{G4}, \quad M_{I4} = I_{G4} \alpha_4
$$

**Step 3 — FBD of Each Link** (showing all pin reactions, external loads, weight, and inertia terms).

**Step 4 — Equilibrium Equations** — typically starting with the simplest link (often the rocker if it has no external load), then propagating to the input link (crank).

**Step 5 — Input Torque**: Sum moments about the crank pivot $A$:

$$
T_2 = F_{32,t} \cdot r_1
$$

where $F_{32,t}$ is the tangential component of the force exerted by the coupler on the crank at pin $B$.

## 3.4 Dynamic Analysis of a Slider-Crank Mechanism (Standard Textbook Form)

Consider a **single-cylinder reciprocating engine** with:
- Crank length = $r$, rotating at constant $\omega$
- Connecting rod length = $L$
- Crank angle from TDC = $\theta$
- Piston mass = $m_p$, connecting rod mass = $m_c$, crank mass = $m_{cr}$ (balanced counterweight is used in practice; for analysis we ignore it here)
- $I_G$ of connecting rod about its $G$
- $F_{gas}$ = gas force on piston
- $W_p, W_c$ = weights of piston and connecting rod (if engine is vertical)

### Step 1 — Position of Piston

Position of piston $P$ from crank center $O$, along the cylinder axis:

$$
x = (r \cos\theta) + (L \cos\phi)
$$

Using the geometric identity $\sin\phi = (r/L)\sin\theta$, and differentiating twice:

### Step 2 — Acceleration of Piston

$$
a_p = r\omega^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{r \cos 2\theta}{\sqrt{L^2 - r^2 \sin^2\theta}} \right)
$$

For the **approximate analysis** ($L \gg r$):

$$
\boxed{a_p \approx r\omega^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta \right)}
$$

### Step 3 — Piston Inertia Force

$$
F_{Ip} = m_p \cdot a_p = m_p r\omega^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta \right)
$$

### Step 4 — Connecting Rod Inertia Force and Couple

Assume $G_c$ is at distance $L_G$ from the small end $B$ and $L - L_G$ from the big end $A$.

Angular velocity and acceleration of connecting rod:

$$
\dot{\phi} = \frac{r\omega \cos\theta}{L \cos\phi}, \quad \ddot{\phi} = \frac{r\omega^2 \sin\theta}{L \cos\phi} \quad \text{(approx., small terms neglected)}
$$

Centripetal acceleration of $G_c$ along $BA$:

$$
a_{cn} = (L - L_G) \omega_c^2 = (L - L_G) \dot{\phi}^2
$$

Tangential acceleration of $G_c$ perpendicular to $BA$:

$$
a_{ct} = (L - L_G) \ddot{\phi}
$$

Therefore:

$$
F_{Ic} = m_c a_{cn}, \quad F_{It} = m_c a_{ct}, \quad M_{Ic} = I_{Gc} \ddot{\phi}
$$

### Step 5 — FBD of the Connecting Rod

The rod $AB$ is a two-force member modified by $M_{Ic}$. Resolving forces along the rod axis (compression $F_Q$) and perpendicular to it (side force $F_S$):

**Force along the connecting rod (Newton's law on rod as free body):**

$$
F_Q - F_{Ip}' - F_{Ic} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad F_Q = F_{Ip}' + F_{Ic}
$$

where $F_{Ip}'$ is the force from the piston on the small end of the rod, equal to the net gas force + piston inertia + piston weight component.

### Step 6 — Force on the Crank Pin (Big End of Connecting Rod)

The big end of the rod transmits to the crank a force of magnitude $F_Q$ along the rod axis, plus a couple $M_{Ic}$ from the rod's rotational inertia.

**Tangential component on crank pin** (perpendicular to crank):

$$
F_t = F_Q \sin(\theta + \phi)
$$

**Crank-pin turning moment**:

$$
\boxed{T = F_t \cdot r = F_Q \cdot r \sin(\theta + \phi)}
$$

### Step 7 — Side Thrust on Cylinder Walls

The horizontal (or perpendicular to cylinder axis) force pushing the piston against the cylinder wall:

$$
\boxed{F_S = F_N \tan\phi = F_Q \sin\phi}
$$

This **side thrust** causes wear on the cylinder walls and is a critical design parameter.

> [!NOTE]
> **Approximate turning moment (very common in KTU problems):**
> Using $\sin(\theta + \phi) = \sin\theta \cos\phi + \cos\theta \sin\phi$ and the small-angle approximation $\cos\phi \approx 1$, $\sin\phi \approx (r/L)\sin\theta$:
> $$\boxed{T \approx F_N \cdot r \left( \sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta \right)}$$
> This is the famous **Norton / Shamble** form that examiners love to test.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical — Quick Reference

**Example (KTU-typical, slider-crank):** Crank $r = 100$ mm, connecting rod $L = 400$ mm, $\omega = 1500$ rpm, piston mass $m_p = 1.5$ kg, gas force on piston $F_g = 1000$ N, $\theta = 30°$. Find: (i) piston acceleration, (ii) piston inertia force, (iii) turning moment (neglect rod inertia).

**Solution sketch:**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 1500}{60} = 50\pi \approx 157.08 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\sin\phi = \frac{r}{L}\sin\theta = \frac{100}{400}\sin 30° = 0.125 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = 7.18°
$$

$$
\cos 2\theta = \cos 60° = 0.5
$$

$$
a_p \approx r\omega^2 \left( \cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta \right) = 0.1 \times (157.08)^2 \times \left( 0.866 + 0.25 \times 0.5 \right)
$$

$$
a_p = 0.1 \times 24674 \times 0.991 = 2445.5 \text{ m/s}^2
$$

$$
F_{Ip} = 1.5 \times 2445.5 = 3668 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_N = F_{Ip} + F_g = 3668 + 1000 = 4668 \text{ N}
$$

$$
T \approx F_N \cdot r \left( \sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta \right) = 4668 \times 0.1 \times \left( 0.5 + 0.125 \times 0.866 \right)
$$

$$
T = 466.8 \times 0.608 = 283.8 \text{ N·m}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Dynamic Force Analysis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Kinematic Analysis] --> B[Step 2: Compute Accelerations aG and Angular Acc alpha for each link]
    B --> C[Step 3: Compute Inertia Force FI = m aG and Inertia Couple MI = IG alpha]
    C --> D[Step 4: Apply DAlemberts Principle Add FI and MI as External Loads on FBD]
    D --> E[Step 5: Draw FBD of Each Link with all Pin Reactions Weights and Loads]
    E --> F[Step 6: Write Equilibrium Equations Sum Fx Sum Fy Sum M equals zero]
    F --> G[Step 7: Solve System of Equations from Simplest Link to Input Link]
    G --> H[Step 8: Determine Input Torque and Shaking Force and Side Thrust]
    H --> I[Output: Dynamic Design Loads for Bearings Pins and Frame]
```

## 4.2 Four-Bar Mechanism — Inertia Force Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Crank Link AB rotates with omega2 alpha2] --> B[Pin B Reaction Force]
    B --> C[Coupler Link BC with mass m3 and IG3]
    C --> D[Inertia Force FI3 opposite to aG3]
    C --> E[Inertia Couple MI3 opposite to alpha3]
    C --> F[Pin C Reaction Force]
    F --> G[Rocker Link CD with mass m4 and IG4]
    G --> H[Inertia Force FI4]
    G --> I[Inertia Couple MI4]
    G --> J[Pin D Reaction Force to Ground]
    C --> K[Equilibrium of coupler gives pin force at B]
    K --> L[Input Torque T2 = F32t times r1]
```

## 4.3 Slider-Crank Mechanism — Force Flow Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Gas Force Fg on Piston Head] --> B[Net Axial Force on Piston FN = Fg + FIp + Wp]
    B --> C[Piston Pushes on Small End of Connecting Rod]
    C --> D[Connecting Rod AB transmits axial force FQ]
    D --> E[Big End of Rod exerts force on Crank Pin]
    E --> F[Tangential Component Ft = FQ sin theta plus phi]
    F --> G[Turning Moment T = Ft times r]
    G --> H[Input Torque Required to Drive Crank]
    D --> I[Side Thrust FS = FQ sin phi on Cylinder Wall]
    I --> J[Cylinder Liner Wear and Friction]
    E --> K[Centrifugal Reaction on Crank Bearings]
    K --> L[Shaking Force on Engine Block]
```

## 4.4 Free Body Diagram Concept — Slider-Crank

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph PistonAssembly[Piston Free Body]
        P1[Gas Force Fg acts on top] --> P2[Net Force FN acts downward along axis]
        P2 --> P3[Reaction from Small End of Rod N1]
    end
    subgraph RodAssembly[Connecting Rod Free Body]
        R1[Force N1 from Piston on small end] --> R2[Inertia Force FIc and FIt and Couple MIc at Gc]
        R2 --> R3[Reaction at Big End N2 along rod axis]
    end
    subgraph CrankAssembly[Crank Free Body]
        C1[Force N2 from rod big end] --> C2[Tangential Force Ft on Crank Pin]
        C2 --> C3[Input Torque T at Crank Shaft]
    end
    PistonAssembly --> RodAssembly
    RodAssembly --> CrankAssembly
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define D'Alembert's principle and state the equation of dynamic equilibrium for a rigid link in plane motion.**

**Model Answer:**

D'Alembert's principle states that if the inertia force ($F_I = -m\,a_G$) and the inertia couple ($M_I = -I_G\,\alpha$) of a moving body are considered as additional external loads, then the body can be treated as being in static equilibrium.

$$
\boxed{\sum \vec{F} + \sum \vec{F}_I = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad \sum \vec{M}_G + \sum \vec{M}_I = 0}
$$

where $\vec{F}_I = -m\vec{a}_G$ acts at $G$ and $\vec{M}_I = -I_G \vec{\alpha}$.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**What is meant by 'inertia force' and 'inertia couple' in the dynamic analysis of mechanisms? Give their expressions.**

**Model Answer:**

**Inertia force** is a fictitious force, equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the resultant of the products of the masses of particles and their accelerations. For a body of mass $m$ with center of gravity acceleration $a_G$:

$$
\vec{F}_I = -m \vec{a}_G
$$

**Inertia couple** is the fictitious couple, equal in magnitude and opposite to the product of mass moment of inertia $I_G$ about the center of gravity and the angular acceleration $\alpha$ of the body:

$$
\vec{M}_I = -I_G \vec{\alpha}
$$

Both are *inertial resistances* — the body's reaction to the change in motion.

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) — Four-Bar Mechanism

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Similar Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the step-by-step procedure for the dynamic force analysis of a four-bar mechanism using D'Alembert's principle. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In a four-bar mechanism $ABCD$, the crank $AB$ rotates at a constant angular velocity $\omega_2 = 20$ rad/s. Link data: $AB = 100$ mm, $BC = 300$ mm, $CD = 250$ mm, $AD = 350$ mm. The crank angle $\theta_2 = 60°$ (from $AD$). The coupler $BC$ has mass $m_3 = 4$ kg with $I_{G3} = 0.05$ kg·m² and center of gravity at $G_3$ located 150 mm from $B$ and 150 mm from $C$. The rocker $CD$ has mass $m_4 = 3$ kg with $I_{G4} = 0.03$ kg·m² and $G_4$ at 125 mm from $C$ and 125 mm from $D$. Compute: (i) the inertia force of the coupler, (ii) the inertia couple of the coupler, and (iii) the force at pin $C$ (dynamic). Given: angular accelerations $\alpha_3 = 10$ rad/s² (CCW) and $\alpha_4 = 8$ rad/s² (CW); acceleration of $G_3$: $a_{G3} = 15$ m/s² directed at 30° to link $BC$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Procedure for Dynamic Analysis of a Four-Bar Mechanism:**

1. **Kinematic Analysis** (velocity & acceleration) — Find $\omega_i, \alpha_i$ of each link and $a_{Gi}$ of each center of gravity.
2. **Inertia Loads** — Compute $F_{Ii} = m_i a_{Gi}$ and $M_{Ii} = I_{Gi} \alpha_i$ for each moving link.
3. **Apply D'Alembert's Principle** — Show $F_{Ii}$ and $M_{Ii}$ on the FBD of each link, opposite to the directions of $a_{Gi}$ and $\alpha_i$ respectively.
4. **FBDs** — Mark all pin reactions, weights, external loads, and inertia terms.
5. **Equilibrium Equations** — Write $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, $\sum M = 0$ for every link.
6. **Solve Sequentially** — Start with the simplest link (no external load), then propagate.
7. **Input Torque** — Sum moments about the fixed pivot of the driving crank to find the required input torque.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Inertia force of coupler (link 3):**

$$
F_{I3} = m_3 \cdot a_{G3} = 4 \times 15 = 60 \text{ N}
$$

Direction: **opposite** to $\vec{a}_{G3}$ — i.e., at 30° on the *other side* of link $BC$ from $a_{G3}$.

**(ii) Inertia couple of coupler:**

$$
M_{I3} = I_{G3} \cdot \alpha_3 = 0.05 \times 10 = 0.5 \text{ N·m}
$$

Direction: **opposite** to $\alpha_3$ — i.e., if $\alpha_3$ is CCW, then $M_{I3}$ is CW.

**(iii) Force at pin $C$:**

Taking FBD of the rocker (link 4), the forces acting on it are:
- Reaction at $D$ ($R_{D,x}, R_{D,y}$)
- Force from coupler at $C$ ($R_{C,x}, R_{C,y}$)
- Inertia force $F_{I4} = 3 \times a_{G4}$ (assume $a_{G4} = 10$ m/s² from rocker kinematics → $F_{I4} = 30$ N)
- Inertia couple $M_{I4} = 0.03 \times 8 = 0.24$ N·m

Moment equilibrium of rocker about $D$:

$$
\sum M_D = 0: \quad R_C \cdot d_{CD} \cdot \sin\psi - F_{I4,t} \cdot d_{G_4D} - M_{I4} = 0
$$

Substituting (assume link $CD$ makes 45° with horizontal, $d_{G_4D} = 0.125$ m):

$$
R_C \times 0.25 \times \sin 45° - 30 \times 0.125 - 0.24 = 0
$$

$$
R_C \times 0.177 - 3.75 - 0.24 = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_C = 22.5 \text{ N}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Four-Bar):** Most students lose marks by (i) drawing the inertia force in the **same direction** as $a_G$ instead of opposite, (ii) forgetting the inertia couple for rotating links, and (iii) using static forces only. **Always** mark $F_I$ and $M_I$ explicitly on the FBD.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Slider-Crank Mechanism

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Similar Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive an expression for the turning moment on the crank of a single-cylinder reciprocating engine, neglecting the inertia of the connecting rod. State all assumptions used. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A horizontal single-cylinder engine has crank length $r = 150$ mm, connecting rod length $L = 600$ mm, piston mass $m_p = 12$ kg, crank speed $N = 1200$ rpm. The piston is subjected to a gas force $F_g = 2500$ N along the cylinder axis. Determine at a crank angle $\theta = 40°$ from TDC: (i) the piston acceleration, (ii) the net force on the piston, (iii) the thrust in the connecting rod, (iv) the side thrust on the cylinder, and (v) the turning moment on the crankshaft. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation of Turning Moment (Rod Inertia Neglected):**

**Assumptions:**
1. Crank rotates at constant angular velocity $\omega$ (no angular acceleration of crank).
2. Connecting rod is massless (or its inertia is neglected).
3. Piston moves without friction.
4. Gas pressure is uniform on the piston face.

**Step 1 — Position of Piston** from crank center along cylinder axis:

$$
x = r\cos\theta + L\cos\phi
$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate twice** using $\sin\phi = (r/L)\sin\theta$:

$$
\dot{x} = -r\omega\sin\theta - L\dot{\phi}\sin\phi
$$

$$
a_p = \ddot{x} = -r\omega^2\cos\theta - L(\ddot{\phi}\sin\phi + \dot{\phi}^2\cos\phi)
$$

**Step 3 — Approximate** (for $L \gg r$):

$$
\boxed{a_p \approx r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)}
$$

**Step 4 — Net force on piston** (horizontal engine, weight neglected):

$$
F_N = F_g + m_p a_p
$$

**Step 5 — Geometry of connecting rod** — angle $\phi$ satisfies:

$$
\sin\phi = \frac{r}{L}\sin\theta
$$

**Step 6 — Thrust in rod** (from piston equilibrium):

$$
F_Q = \frac{F_N}{\cos\phi}
$$

**Step 7 — Turning moment** — the tangential component of $F_Q$ at the crank pin:

$$
F_t = F_Q \sin(\theta + \phi) = \frac{F_N \sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}
$$

$$
\boxed{T = F_t \cdot r = \frac{F_N \cdot r \cdot \sin(\theta + \phi)}{\cos\phi}}
$$

**Step 8 — Approximate form** (using $\sin\phi \approx (r/L)\sin\theta$):

$$
T \approx F_N r \left( \sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta \right)
$$

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

$$
\omega = \frac{2\pi \times 1200}{60} = 40\pi \approx 125.66 \text{ rad/s}
$$

$$
\sin\phi = \frac{150}{600}\sin 40° = 0.25 \times 0.643 = 0.1607 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \phi = 9.25°
$$

$$
\cos\phi = 0.987, \quad \cos 2\theta = \cos 80° = 0.1736, \quad \sin\theta = 0.643, \quad \sin 2\theta = 0.985
$$

**(i) Piston acceleration:**

$$
a_p = 0.15 \times (125.66)^2 \times \left( \cos 40° + 0.25 \times \cos 80° \right)
$$

$$
a_p = 0.15 \times 15790 \times (0.766 + 0.25 \times 0.1736) = 0.15 \times 15790 \times 0.8094
$$

$$
\boxed{a_p \approx 1917 \text{ m/s}^2 \text{ (directed from piston to crank center)}}
$$

**(ii) Net force on piston** (horizontal engine, weight ignored):

$$
F_{Ip} = m_p \cdot a_p = 12 \times 1917 = 23004 \text{ N}
$$

$$
F_N = F_g + F_{Ip} = 2500 + 23004 = \boxed{25504 \text{ N}}
$$

**(iii) Thrust in connecting rod:**

$$
F_Q = \frac{F_N}{\cos\phi} = \frac{25504}{0.987} = \boxed{25840 \text{ N}}
$$

**(iv) Side thrust on cylinder:**

$$
F_S = F_N \tan\phi = 25504 \times \tan 9.25° = 25504 \times 0.1628 = \boxed{4152 \text{ N}}
$$

**(v) Turning moment on crank:**

$$
T = F_N r \left( \sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta \right)
$$

$$
T = 25504 \times 0.15 \times \left( 0.643 + 0.125 \times 0.985 \right)
$$

$$
T = 3825.6 \times (0.643 + 0.123) = 3825.6 \times 0.766
$$

$$
\boxed{T \approx 2930 \text{ N·m}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Slider-Crank):**
> 1. **Forgetting the piston inertia force** — A common error is to set $F_N = F_g$ only. The inertia term $m_p a_p$ can be **larger** than $F_g$ at high speeds.
> 2. **Confusing the direction of side thrust** — Side thrust pushes the piston *against* the cylinder wall; it is **not** along the rod axis.
> 3. **Sign error on $\cos 2\theta$** — Use the correct sign depending on TDC or BDC side; examiners deduct for careless signs.
> 4. **Mixing up crank angle convention** — State whether $\theta$ is measured from TDC or from the line of stroke; both are valid but must be **consistent** throughout.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 1 (Dynamic Analysis)**

- **D'Alembert's Principle** — Dynamic problem is converted to a *static* one by adding inertia force ($F_I = -m a_G$) and inertia couple ($M_I = -I_G \alpha$).
- **Procedure Order** — Kinematics **first** (find $a_G, \alpha$ for every link), then compute inertia loads, then draw FBDs, then write $\sum F = 0, \sum M = 0$.
- **Direction Rule** — $F_I$ **opposes** $\vec{a}_G$; $M_I$ **opposes** $\vec{\alpha}$. Centripetal component $F_{Ic}$ points **outward** from the instant center.
- **Four-bar key trick** — Analyze rocker first (often no external load), then propagate pin forces to the coupler, then to the crank.
- **Slider-crank piston acceleration (approx)**:
  $$a_p = r\omega^2\left(\cos\theta + \frac{r}{L}\cos 2\theta\right)$$
- **Piston inertia force**: $F_{Ip} = m_p a_p$ — *always* present, *always* along the cylinder axis.
- **Net axial force**: $F_N = F_{Ip} + F_g + W_p(\text{vertical engine, sign depends on position})$.
- **Thrust in rod**: $F_Q = F_N / \cos\phi$, where $\sin\phi = (r/L)\sin\theta$.
- **Side thrust** (cylinder wall reaction): $F_S = F_N \tan\phi = F_Q \sin\phi$.
- **Turning moment** (approximate, the famous form):
  $$T = F_N \cdot r \left( \sin\theta + \frac{r}{2L}\sin 2\theta \right)$$
- **Input torque on four-bar crank**: $T_2 = F_{32,t} \times r_1$ where $F_{32,t}$ is the tangential force at pin $B$.
- **Units consistency** — Lengths in **m**, masses in **kg**, angles in **rad**, force in **N**, torque in **N·m**. Mixing mm and m is the **#1** mark-losing error in KTU papers.
- **Engine type** — Vertical vs horizontal: vertical engines add/subtract piston weight from $F_N$ depending on TDC/BDC; horizontal engines don't.
- **Why dynamic analysis matters** — Static analysis underestimates the actual load by a factor that can exceed 3× at high speeds.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gyroscopic Couple: Gyroscopes, Effect of Gyroscopic Couple on Bearings, Stability of Two-Wheel and Four-Wheel Vehicles; Balancing of Rotating Masses: Single Mass, Several Masses in Same and in Different Planes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEIET413 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Gyroscopic Couple: Gyroscopes, Effect of Gyroscopic Couple on Bearings, Stability of Two-Wheel and Four-Wheel Vehicles; Balancing of Rotating Masses: Single Mass, Several Masses in Same and in Different Planes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Gyroscopic Couple — Formal Definition

A **gyroscopic couple** is the reactive couple experienced by a spinning rotor when it is subjected to forced precession (a forced change in the direction of its axis of spin). Mathematically, it is the time rate of change of **angular momentum vector** $\vec{H}$ of the spinning body.

$$\vec{C} = \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}$$

For a symmetric rotor spinning with angular velocity $\omega$ about its own axis and forced to precess with angular velocity $\omega_p$ about a perpendicular axis, the gyroscopic couple magnitude is:

$$C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p \sin\theta$$

where:
- $I$ = mass moment of inertia of the rotor about its spin axis (kg·m²)
- $\omega$ = angular velocity of spin (rad/s)
- $\omega_p$ = angular velocity of precession (rad/s)
- $\theta$ = angle between spin axis and precession axis

When the spin axis and precession axis are mutually perpendicular ($\theta = 90°$), which is the standard engineering case:

$$C = I\,\omega\,\omega_p$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The direction of the gyroscopic couple is determined by **right-hand rule** on the rate of change of angular momentum vector. It always acts in a direction **opposite** to the active turning couple (Newton's third law applied to rotational systems).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Spinning Top

Imagine you are riding a bicycle at high speed. The wheels are massive gyroscopes spinning at hundreds of rpm. Now, when you tilt the bicycle to the left while moving forward, instead of simply falling over, the front wheel **steers** into the curve. This mysterious automatic turning is the gyroscopic effect. The spinning wheel "resists" the change in its plane of rotation and produces a couple that turns the wheel about the steering axis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Gyroscopic Devices:**
> - **Aircraft** — turn indicators, heading indicators, attitude gyros
> - **Ships** — anti-rolling stabilizers (gyro stabilizers)
> - **Missiles & Rockets** — spin stabilization
> - **Rifled bullets** — maintain trajectory by gyroscopic stability
> - **Two-wheelers & Four-wheelers** — directional stability and turning dynamics

## 1.3 Balancing of Rotating Masses — Formal Definition

**Balancing** is the process of redistributing the masses of a rotating body (or adding compensating masses) so that the **centrifugal forces** developed by the rotating masses produce **zero net resultant force** and **zero net resultant couple** on the shaft. The goal is to eliminate (or minimize) the dynamic forces transmitted to the bearings.

- **Static balancing** — eliminates the **resultant centrifugal force** (force polygon closes)
- **Dynamic balancing** — eliminates both the **resultant centrifugal force** AND the **resultant couple** (both force and couple polygons close)

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — Washing Machine Spinning

Place a single wet sock on one side of an empty washing machine drum. When the drum spins, the unbalanced sock causes violent shaking — that's an **unbalanced** rotor. Now place a second sock diametrically opposite the first. The drum runs smoothly — that is **statically balanced**. But if the two socks are at *different distances* from the rotation axis, the drum may still wobble sideways because of an unbalanced **couple** — requiring **dynamic balancing** with two correction masses in two different axial planes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Mandate:** The following balancing sub-topics are examinable:
> 1. Balancing of a single rotating mass by a single mass in the same plane
> 2. Balancing of several masses in the **same plane** (static balance)
> 3. Balancing of several masses in **different planes** (dynamic balance)
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Gyroscope — Concept Breakdown

A **gyroscope** is a symmetric rotor (usually a disc or flywheel) mounted in a system of gimbals that allow it to rotate freely about three mutually perpendicular axes:
1. **Spin axis** — about which the rotor itself rotates (angular velocity $\omega$)
2. **Precession axis** — about which the spin axis rotates (angular velocity $\omega_p$)
3. **Torque axis** — perpendicular to both, along which the gyroscopic couple acts

### Why Does a Gyroscopic Couple Arise?

- The rotor has angular momentum $\vec{H} = I\vec{\omega}$ directed along the spin axis.
- When the frame forces the spin axis to rotate (precess), the tip of $\vec{H}$ must sweep out a cone.
- This requires a continuous **change in the direction** of $\vec{H}$ (not its magnitude).
- The time rate of change of a vector of constant magnitude rotating at $\omega_p$ is $H \cdot \omega_p$.
- Therefore $C = I\omega\omega_p$ — this is the **active (applied) couple** needed to force the precession.
- By Newton's third law, the rotor exerts an equal and opposite **reactive gyroscopic couple** on its supports (bearings).

### Vector Representation

$$\vec{C} = \vec{\omega_p} \times \vec{H} = \vec{\omega_p} \times (I\vec{\omega})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign Convention (KTU Standard):** When the active couple rotates the spin axis in the same sense as the spin (i.e., it tries to tilt the axis "in the direction of spin"), the reactive gyroscopic couple tilts the axis "in the direction opposite to spin." This is what causes the **precession** observed in spinning tops.

## 2.2 Effect of Gyroscopic Couple on Bearings of an Engine/Propeller Shaft

Consider an **aero-engine** (or marine propeller) mounted on a shaft supported by two bearings. When the aircraft (or ship) takes a turn, the entire engine precesses about a vertical axis.

### Setup
- Engine/propeller spins about a horizontal axis (fore-aft) with angular velocity $\omega$.
- Aircraft turns (or rolls) about a vertical axis with angular velocity $\omega_p$.
- Spin axis ⊥ precession axis → maximum gyroscopic couple.

### Reactions on Bearings
The gyroscopic couple $C = I\omega\omega_p$ must be reacted by a **couple at the two bearings**. The reactions form an equal and opposite couple, so each bearing carries a force:

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{C}{L} = \frac{I\omega\omega_p}{L}$$

where $L$ = distance between the two bearings.

- One bearing experiences a **vertical upward** force.
- The other bearing experiences a **vertical downward** force.
- These forces **add to** or **subtract from** the static bearing reactions caused by the weight of the engine.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget that the gyroscopic couple produces a **couple at the bearings** (two equal, opposite, parallel forces), not a single net force. The magnitude of each bearing reaction = $C/L$.

### Sign Convention for Engine Applications
- **Pitching** (nose up/down) — gyroscopic couple acts in the plane of pitching
- **Yawing** (nose left/right turning) — gyroscopic couple acts vertically (affects rolling)
- **Rolling** — gyroscopic couple acts in the plane of pitching (affects yawing)

## 2.3 Stability of Two-Wheel Vehicles (Motorcycle/Bicycle)

When a two-wheeler moves on a curved path, the gyroscopic effect of the spinning wheels creates a **gyroscopic couple** $C = I\omega\omega_p$ that tends to **topple** the vehicle outward. The rider must **lean inward** to counteract this.

### Derivation Setup
Let the rider + vehicle have:
- Total mass $m$ acting at center of gravity G, at height $h$ above ground
- Wheelbase $b$ (distance between wheel contact points)
- Vehicle leans inward by angle $\phi$ from vertical
- Vehicle negotiates a curve of radius $R$ at velocity $v$

**For equilibrium (steady turning):**

1. **Centrifugal force** acting outward at G: $F_c = \dfrac{mv^2}{R}$
2. **Weight** acting downward at G: $W = mg$

Taking moments about the outer wheel contact point:

$$mg \cdot b\sin\phi = \frac{mv^2}{R} \cdot h\cos\phi$$

This gives the **stability condition**:

$$\tan\phi = \frac{v^2}{gR}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **With gyroscopic effect included**, the equation becomes:

$$mg \cdot b\sin\phi - C\cos\phi = \frac{mv^2}{R} \cdot h\cos\phi$$

where the gyroscopic couple $C = I\omega\omega_p = I\omega(v/R)$ acts to **topple** the vehicle outward. Rearranging:

$$\tan\phi \geq \frac{v^2}{gR} + \frac{I\omega}{m b R}\cos\phi$$

For small angles ($\phi$ small, $\cos\phi \approx 1$):

$$\phi \approx \frac{v^2}{gR} + \frac{I\omega v}{mg b R}$$

**Minimum radius for stability (no lean, $\phi = 0$):**

The vehicle will topple outward unless the gyroscopic couple is reacted. The condition for **no toppling** at zero lean is:

$$\frac{mv^2}{R} \cdot h \leq mg \cdot b \quad \Rightarrow \quad R_{min} = \frac{v^2 h}{gb}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-Life Insight:** This is why motorcyclists lean into curves and why a high-speed bicycle becomes "stiff" and harder to steer — the gyroscopic couple opposes the steering input.

## 2.4 Stability of Four-Wheel Vehicles (Car/Truck)

For a four-wheeler, the gyroscopic couple from the four wheels can cause two distinct failure modes during a turn:

### Failure Mode 1: Overturning (Toppling)
The centrifugal force creates an overturning moment about the outer wheels. The vehicle overturns when:

$$\frac{mv^2}{R} \cdot h = mg \cdot \frac{b}{2}$$

$$\boxed{R_{overturn} = \frac{2hv^2}{gb}}$$

where $b$ = track width (distance between left and right wheels), $h$ = height of CG.

### Failure Mode 2: Skidding (Sideways Slipping)
The vehicle skids outward when the required centripetal force exceeds the available friction:

$$\frac{mv^2}{R} = \mu mg$$

$$\boxed{R_{skid} = \frac{v^2}{\mu g}}$$

### Combined Stability Criterion
- If $R_{skid} > R_{overturn}$ → vehicle **overturns first** (rare for cars; common for tall trucks/buses)
- If $R_{skid} < R_{overturn}$ → vehicle **skids first** (typical for low cars)
- **Critical speed** (at which both occur simultaneously): 

$$v_{critical} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu g b}{2}}$$

### Including Gyroscopic Couple of Wheels
When including the gyroscopic couple $C = I\omega(v/R)$ from the four wheels (each of moment of inertia $I$ spinning at $\omega = v/r_{wheel}$):

The overturning moment must overcome:

$$\frac{mv^2}{R} \cdot h + C = mg \cdot \frac{b}{2}$$

This is typically a small correction for cars but significant for high-speed vehicles.

## 2.5 Balancing of Rotating Masses — Theory

### A. Single Mass in One Plane Balanced by a Single Mass in the Same Plane

The rotating mass $m$ at radius $r$ produces centrifugal force $F = mr\omega^2$ acting radially outward. A balance mass $m_b$ at radius $r_b$ in the same plane (diametrically opposite) produces force $F_b = m_b r_b \omega^2$.

For static balance (forces equal and opposite):

$$m \cdot r = m_b \cdot r_b$$

The product $m \cdot r$ is called the **mass-radius product** or **unbalance** (kg·m).

### B. Several Masses in the Same Plane

For $n$ masses $m_1, m_2, \dots, m_n$ at radii $r_1, r_2, \dots, r_n$ with angular positions $\theta_1, \theta_2, \dots, \theta_n$ (measured from a reference axis):

**Static balance conditions:**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$

**Graphical method:** Draw the force polygon by laying out vectors of magnitude $m_i r_i$ at angles $\theta_i$. The closing side of the polygon (in opposite direction) gives the magnitude and position of the **balance mass-radius product**.

**Resultant unbalance:**

$$m_b r_b = \sqrt{\left(\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i\right)^2 + \left(\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i\right)^2}$$

$$\theta_b = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i}{\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i}\right)$$

### C. Several Masses in Different Planes — Dynamic Balancing

When masses lie in **different axial planes**, even if statically balanced (force polygon closes), an unbalanced **couple** can exist. This is because the centrifugal forces, while equal in magnitude and opposite in direction, may not be **collinear** (since they act in different planes).

**Unbalanced couple** of mass $m_i$ at distance $l_i$ from reference plane:

$$\text{Couple} = m_i r_i l_i \omega^2$$

**Dynamic balance conditions** (must satisfy FOUR equations):

$$\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(force closure — X)}$$

$$\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(force closure — Y)}$$

$$\sum m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(couple closure — X)}$$

$$\sum m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i = 0 \quad \text{(couple closure — Y)}$$

**Graphical method:** 
1. Draw **force polygon** — gives magnitude and angular position of the balance force (= balance mass-radius product in the **reference plane**).
2. Draw **couple polygon** using vectors $m_i r_i l_i$ at angles $\theta_i$ — gives the unbalanced couple in the reference plane.
3. The balance mass must be split into **two masses in two planes** so that:
   - Their **resultant force** equals the unbalanced force (in opposite sense).
   - Their **resultant couple** about the reference plane equals the unbalanced couple (in opposite sense).

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Angular momentum | $H = I\omega$ | kg·m²/s |
| Gyroscopic couple | $C = I\omega\omega_p\sin\theta$ | General form |
| Gyroscopic couple (⊥ axes) | $C = I\omega\omega_p$ | $\theta = 90°$ case |
| Bearing reaction (engine) | $R = \dfrac{I\omega\omega_p}{L}$ | Each bearing |
| Two-wheeler lean angle | $\tan\phi = \dfrac{v^2}{gR} + \dfrac{I\omega v}{mgbR}\cos\phi$ | Stability condition |
| Min. radius (no lean) | $R_{min} = \dfrac{v^2 h}{gb}$ | Toppling limit |
| Four-wheeler overturn | $R_{overturn} = \dfrac{2hv^2}{gb}$ | Track width $b$ |
| Four-wheeler skid | $R_{skid} = \dfrac{v^2}{\mu g}$ | Friction coefficient $\mu$ |
| Critical speed | $v_{cr} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\mu g b}{2}}$ | Overturn = Skid |
| Single mass balance | $m_b r_b = m \cdot r$ | Same plane |
| Multi-mass force balance (X) | $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ | Static balance |
| Multi-mass force balance (Y) | $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ | Static balance |
| Resultant unbalance | $m_b r_b = \sqrt{(\sum m_i r_i\cos\theta_i)^2 + (\sum m_i r_i\sin\theta_i)^2}$ | Magnitude |
| Balance angle | $\theta_b = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{\sum m_i r_i\sin\theta_i}{\sum m_i r_i\cos\theta_i}\right)$ | Position |
| Unbalanced couple | $T_i = m_i r_i l_i \omega^2$ | About ref. plane |
| Dynamic balance (X) | $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ | Force closure |
| Dynamic balance (Y) | $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ | Force closure |
| Dynamic balance couple (X) | $\sum m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i = 0$ | Couple closure |
| Dynamic balance couple (Y) | $\sum m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ | Couple closure |
| Mass moment of inertia (disc) | $I = mr^2/2$ | Solid disc |
| Mass moment of inertia (hoop) | $I = mr^2$ | Thin ring |
| Mass moment of inertia (sphere) | $I = \dfrac{2}{5}mr^2$ | Solid sphere |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Magnitude of Gyroscopic Couple

### Starting Point — Newton's Second Law for Rotation

A rigid body rotating with angular velocity $\vec{\omega}$ possesses angular momentum:

$$\vec{H} = I\vec{\omega}$$

The **time rate of change** of angular momentum equals the **applied couple** (Euler's equation):

$$\vec{T} = \frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}$$

### Case: Forced Precession

Consider a disc spinning with constant spin $\omega$ about axis $OA$. The frame is rotated about a perpendicular axis $OB$ with angular velocity $\omega_p$.

- The tip of $\vec{H} = I\omega \hat{a}$ (where $\hat{a}$ is unit vector along $OA$) traces a circle of radius $H = I\omega$ in time $T_p = 2\pi/\omega_p$.
- The arc length swept per unit time = $H \cdot \omega_p$ (this is the magnitude of $\frac{d\vec{H}}{dt}$).
- Therefore, the applied couple needed to force this precession is:

$$\boxed{T = I\omega\omega_p}$$

### Couple Direction
The direction of $\vec{T}$ is along $\vec{\omega_p} \times \vec{H}$ (cross product).

The **reactive gyroscopic couple** exerted by the disc on its frame is:

$$\vec{C} = -\vec{T} = -I(\vec{\omega_p} \times \vec{\omega})$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Bearing Reactions in a Turning Aero-Engine

### Setup
- Engine + propeller: total moment of inertia about spin axis = $I$, spinning at $\omega$ rad/s
- Distance between two bearings A and B = $L$
- Aircraft yaws (turns) at angular velocity $\omega_p$ rad/s about vertical axis

### Analysis

The gyroscopic couple $C = I\omega\omega_p$ must be reacted by a couple at the bearings. By statics:

$$R_A \cdot L = C \quad \text{and} \quad R_B = R_A \text{ (equal and opposite)}$$

$$\boxed{R_A = R_B = \frac{I\omega\omega_p}{L}}$$

One bearing pushes **up**, the other pushes **down** (or vice versa, depending on the turn direction).

## 3.3 Derivation: Two-Wheeler Stability (Including Gyroscopic Effect)

### Setup
- Total mass of vehicle + rider = $m$ kg
- Height of CG from ground = $h$
- Wheelbase = $b$ (between front and rear wheel contact)
- Vehicle leans inward by angle $\phi$
- Negotiates curve of radius $R$ at velocity $v$
- Angular velocity of precession = $\omega_p = v/R$
- Mass moment of inertia of each wheel about spin axis = $I$
- Spin angular velocity of each wheel = $\omega = v/r$ (where $r$ = wheel radius)

### Free Body Diagram (Forces at Lean Angle $\phi$)

1. Weight $W = mg$ acts **downward** at G.
2. Centrifugal force $F_c = mv^2/R$ acts **outward** (horizontally) at G.
3. Gyroscopic couple $C = I\omega\omega_p = I\omega(v/R)$ acts at the wheel axle, tending to **topple** the vehicle outward (out of the turn plane).
4. Reactions at the wheel contact points.

### Moment Equation (about outer wheel contact point O')

Taking counterclockwise (inward) as positive:

$$mg \cdot b\sin\phi - C\cos\phi = \frac{mv^2}{R} \cdot h\cos\phi$$

Rearranging:

$$mg \cdot b\sin\phi = \frac{mv^2}{R} \cdot h\cos\phi + C\cos\phi$$

Dividing both sides by $mg\cos\phi$:

$$b\tan\phi = \frac{v^2 h}{gR} + \frac{C}{mg}$$

Substituting $C = I\omega v/R$:

$$b\tan\phi = \frac{v^2 h}{gR} + \frac{I\omega v}{mgR}$$

$$\boxed{\tan\phi = \frac{v^2 h}{gbR} + \frac{I\omega v}{mgbR}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting Case 1 (no gyroscopic effect, $I = 0$):** $\tan\phi = \dfrac{v^2 h}{gbR}$ → standard lean angle formula
> 
> **Limiting Case 2 (no lean, $\phi = 0$):** Toppling occurs when $\dfrac{mv^2 h}{R} = mgb \cdot \tan\phi_{critical}$

### Minimum Radius Without Toppling (no lean, $\phi = 0$)

The vehicle can travel in a circle of radius $R$ without toppling **without leaning** only if:

$$\frac{mv^2 h}{R} \leq mg \cdot b$$

$$\boxed{R_{min} = \frac{v^2 h}{gb}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation: Four-Wheeler Stability

### Overturning Condition

Consider a vehicle of mass $m$ at height $h$ turning at velocity $v$ on radius $R$. Track width = $b$. The centrifugal force $mv^2/R$ acts outward at G.

**Moments about outer wheel contact** (overturning vs. restoring):

$$\frac{mv^2}{R} \cdot h = mg \cdot \frac{b}{2}$$

(Outer wheel contact is the pivot; the weight $mg$ acts through CG which is $b/2$ inward from this pivot, creating a restoring moment.)

Solving for $R$ at the verge of overturning:

$$\boxed{R_{overturning} = \frac{2hv^2}{gb}}$$

### Skidding Condition

The required centripetal force equals the maximum friction force:

$$\frac{mv^2}{R} = \mu mg$$

$$\boxed{R_{skidding} = \frac{v^2}{\mu g}}$$

### Critical Speed

Setting $R_{overturning} = R_{skidding}$:

$$\frac{2hv^2}{gb} = \frac{v^2}{\mu g}$$

$$2h\mu = b \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{v_{critical} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu g b}{2}}}$$

- $v < v_{critical}$ → vehicle **skids** before overturning (typical cars)
- $v > v_{critical}$ → vehicle **overturns** before skidding (rare; tall vehicles)

## 3.5 Derivation: Balancing of Several Masses in the Same Plane

### Vector Formulation

For $n$ masses, the centrifugal force on each is $F_i = m_i r_i \omega^2$ acting radially outward at angle $\theta_i$. 

**Force balance equations** (Newton's second law for the rotating system):

$$\sum F_x = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$$

$$\sum F_y = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$$

### Resultant Unbalance

The closing side of the **force polygon** gives the resultant $m_b r_b$:

$$m_b r_b = \sqrt{\left(\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i\right)^2 + \left(\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i\right)^2}$$

The angle of the balance mass from the reference:

$$\theta_b = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i}{\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i}\right)$$

The balance mass must be placed at angle $(\theta_b + 180°)$ from the reference.

## 3.6 Derivation: Balancing of Masses in Different Planes (Dynamic Balance)

### Why Dynamic Balance is Needed

Even if the **force polygon closes** (static balance achieved), the centrifugal forces may not be **collinear** (because they act in different axial planes). This creates an unbalanced **couple** about the reference plane.

### Couple About Reference Plane

For mass $m_i$ at axial distance $l_i$ from reference plane and angular position $\theta_i$:

$$\text{Couple about Y-axis} = m_i r_i l_i \omega^2 \cos\theta_i$$

$$\text{Couple about X-axis} = m_i r_i l_i \omega^2 \sin\theta_i$$

### Complete Dynamic Balance Conditions

For full dynamic balance, **all four** of the following must be zero:

$$\begin{aligned}
\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i &= 0 \\
\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i &= 0 \\
\sum m_i r_i l_i \cos\theta_i &= 0 \\
\sum m_i r_i l_i \sin\theta_i &= 0
\end{aligned}$$

### Solution: Two Balance Masses in Two Planes

We need two balance masses $m_b^{(1)}$ and $m_b^{(2)}$ in two planes at distances $l_1$ and $l_2$ from the reference plane.

Let $F$ = unbalanced force (force polygon closing vector) and $T$ = unbalanced couple (couple polygon closing vector). Both are vectors in the 2D cross-sectional plane.

**Decomposition:**

1. The two balance masses produce a **resultant force** $F_b$ (in opposite sense to $F$).
2. The two balance masses produce a **resultant couple** $T_b$ (in opposite sense to $T$).

**Standard approach** (choosing one mass in reference plane):

- Let balance mass $m_b^{(1)}$ be in reference plane ($l_1 = 0$): produces force $F_b$ only.
- Balance mass $m_b^{(2)}$ at distance $l_2$ from reference: produces both force and couple.

Solving:

$$m_b^{(1)} r_b^{(1)} = F_b \quad \text{(at angle opposite to } F\text{)}$$

$$m_b^{(2)} r_b^{(2)} l_2 = T_b \quad \text{(at angle opposite to } T\text{)}$$

The second balance mass is typically placed perpendicular to the first (or as required by the geometry).

## 3.7 Worked Example: Single Mass Balancing

**Problem:** A shaft carries a mass of 12 kg at a radius of 250 mm. Find the mass and radius of the balance mass if it is to be placed at a radius of 300 mm in the same plane.

**Solution:**

Given:
- $m = 12$ kg, $r = 250$ mm = 0.25 m
- $r_b = 300$ mm = 0.30 m

Applying the balance equation:

$$m_b \cdot r_b = m \cdot r$$

$$m_b \cdot 0.30 = 12 \times 0.25$$

$$m_b = \frac{12 \times 0.25}{0.30} = \frac{3.0}{0.30} = 10 \text{ kg}$$

**Answer:** Balance mass $m_b = 10$ kg, placed diametrically opposite to the original mass.

## 3.8 Worked Example: Four-Wheeler Stability

**Problem:** A car of mass 1500 kg has a wheelbase (in the turning sense) such that its CG is at height 0.6 m and track width 1.5 m. It travels at 72 km/h on a curve of radius 100 m. Coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.4$. Determine if the car will overturn or skid.

**Solution:**

Convert velocity: $v = 72 \text{ km/h} = 20 \text{ m/s}$

**Check overturning:**

$$R_{overturn} = \frac{2hv^2}{gb} = \frac{2 \times 0.6 \times (20)^2}{9.81 \times 1.5} = \frac{480}{14.715} = 32.62 \text{ m}$$

Since actual $R = 100$ m $> R_{overturn} = 32.62$ m → **no overturning**.

**Check skidding:**

$$R_{skid} = \frac{v^2}{\mu g} = \frac{(20)^2}{0.4 \times 9.81} = \frac{400}{3.924} = 101.94 \text{ m}$$

Since actual $R = 100$ m $< R_{skid} = 101.94$ m → **vehicle will SKID**.

**Critical speed check:**

$$v_{critical} = \sqrt{\frac{\mu g b}{2}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.4 \times 9.81 \times 1.5}{2}} = \sqrt{2.943} = 1.716 \text{ m/s}$$

Wait, recalculation: $v_{critical} = \sqrt{\frac{0.4 \times 9.81 \times 1.5}{2}} = \sqrt{2.943} = 1.716$ m/s = 6.18 km/h. Since actual speed 72 km/h >> critical speed, the car **skids** (as expected for typical cars).

## 3.9 Worked Example: Two Masses in Same Plane

**Problem:** Two masses 8 kg and 6 kg are mounted on a shaft at radii 200 mm and 150 mm, with angular positions 60° and 150° from a reference. Find the magnitude and position of the balance mass if its radius is 100 mm.

**Solution:**

Given: $m_1 = 8$ kg, $r_1 = 0.20$ m, $\theta_1 = 60°$; $m_2 = 6$ kg, $r_2 = 0.15$ m, $\theta_2 = 150°$

Compute mass-radius products:

$$m_1 r_1 = 8 \times 0.20 = 1.6 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$m_2 r_2 = 6 \times 0.15 = 0.9 \text{ kg·m}$$

Compute components:

$$\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 1.6\cos 60° + 0.9\cos 150°$$

$$= 1.6 \times 0.5 + 0.9 \times (-0.866) = 0.800 - 0.779 = 0.021 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 1.6\sin 60° + 0.9\sin 150°$$

$$= 1.6 \times 0.866 + 0.9 \times 0.5 = 1.386 + 0.450 = 1.836 \text{ kg·m}$$

Resultant:

$$m_b r_b = \sqrt{(0.021)^2 + (1.836)^2} = \sqrt{0.000441 + 3.371} = \sqrt{3.371} = 1.836 \text{ kg·m}$$

$$m_b = \frac{1.836}{0.10} = 18.36 \text{ kg}$$

Angle of resultant:

$$\theta_{res} = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1.836}{0.021}\right) = \tan^{-1}(87.43) = 89.34°$$

Balance mass position (opposite side): $\theta_b = 89.34° + 180° = 269.34°$ (or equivalently $-90.66°$).

**Answer:** $m_b = 18.36$ kg at $r_b = 100$ mm, angular position $269.34°$ from reference.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define gyroscopic couple. Derive its magnitude for the case when the spin axis and precession axis are mutually perpendicular.**

**Model Answer:**

A **gyroscopic couple** is the reactive couple produced by a spinning body when its axis of rotation is forced to change direction (precess). It arises because the time rate of change of the angular momentum vector $\vec{H}$ must equal the applied couple.

For a rotor with mass moment of inertia $I$ spinning at $\omega$ rad/s, the angular momentum is $H = I\omega$ along the spin axis. If the rotor is forced to precess at $\omega_p$ rad/s about a perpendicular axis, the tip of $\vec{H}$ traces a circle of radius $H$ in time $2\pi/\omega_p$.

The time rate of change of $\vec{H}$ is:

$$C = H \cdot \omega_p = I\omega \cdot \omega_p$$

$$\boxed{C = I\omega\omega_p}$$

The reactive gyroscopic couple on the bearings is equal in magnitude and opposite in direction to the active couple forcing the precession.

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Distinguish between static and dynamic balancing of a rotating shaft.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Balancing | Dynamic Balancing |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Elimination of resultant centrifugal force | Elimination of both resultant force and couple |
| **Conditions** | $\sum m_i r_i \cos\theta_i = 0$, $\sum m_i r_i \sin\theta_i = 0$ | Four conditions: force closure (X, Y) AND couple closure (X, Y) |
| **Applies to** | Masses in same plane OR coplanar | Masses in different planes (non-coplanar) |
| **Number of balance masses** | One balance mass in same plane | Two balance masses in two different planes |
| **Graphical test** | Force polygon closes | Both force AND couple polygons close |
| **Verification** | Shaft can rest in any angular position | Shaft runs vibration-free at all speeds |

A shaft can be **statically balanced but NOT dynamically balanced** (single plane correction cannot fix a couple unbalance). A dynamically balanced shaft is always statically balanced, but the converse is not true.

## Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) **[Module 2 — Internal Choice Option A]**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**(a)** A two-wheeler with rider has total mass 250 kg with CG at 0.7 m above ground level. The wheelbase is 1.3 m and the moment of inertia of each wheel about its spin axis is 1.2 kg·m². The wheel diameter is 600 mm. The vehicle takes a turn of radius 30 m at a speed of 54 km/h. Determine:
   (i) The angle of lean necessary for stability, with gyroscopic effect.
   (ii) The minimum radius of curvature for stability if the vehicle does not lean.
   **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the effect of the gyroscopic couple on the bearings of an aero-engine when the aircraft takes a left turn. State the formula for the magnitude of the bearing reaction. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a)

**Given:**
- $m = 250$ kg, $h = 0.7$ m, $b = 1.3$ m
- $I = 1.2$ kg·m² (per wheel)
- Wheel diameter $= 600$ mm, so $r = 0.3$ m
- $R = 30$ m, $v = 54$ km/h $= 15$ m/s
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Compute spin and precession angular velocities:**

$$\omega = \frac{v}{r} = \frac{15}{0.3} = 50 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_p = \frac{v}{R} = \frac{15}{30} = 0.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute gyroscopic couple:**

$$C = I\omega\omega_p = 1.2 \times 50 \times 0.5 = 30 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 3 — Apply stability equation:**

$$mg \cdot b \sin\phi = \frac{mv^2}{R} \cdot h \cos\phi + C\cos\phi$$

$$250 \times 9.81 \times 1.3 \times \sin\phi = \frac{250 \times 15^2}{30} \times 0.7 \times \cos\phi + 30\cos\phi$$

$$3188.25 \sin\phi = 1312.5 \cos\phi + 30 \cos\phi$$

$$3188.25 \sin\phi = 1342.5 \cos\phi$$

$$\tan\phi = \frac{1342.5}{3188.25} = 0.4210$$

$$\boxed{\phi = 22.84°}$$

**Step 4 — Minimum radius without leaning (no gyroscopic effect for limit case):**

For toppling without lean, $\phi = 0$, so the moment equation reduces to checking when the centrifugal moment equals the maximum restoring moment.

The vehicle topples when:

$$\frac{mv^2 h}{R} = mgb \quad \text{(at } \phi = 0\text{, max restoring moment)}$$

Wait — for $\phi = 0$, the restoring moment is zero. So the vehicle **always** topples at $\phi = 0$ unless... let me reconsider.

Actually, the minimum radius for stability WITH some lean is found by setting $\phi$ to the maximum practical value (say, $\phi = 90°$ at extreme). For toppling without lean, the vehicle would topple for **any** finite centrifugal force. The correct interpretation:

**Minimum radius at $\phi = 0$ (no lean, with gyroscopic effect) is undefined because no moment balance is possible without lean.** The minimum radius for **any** lean is when $\phi$ approaches a small angle, but practically:

The minimum radius for stability **at the verge of toppling** is when the lean angle becomes physically impractical. The standard formula is:

$$R_{min} = \frac{v^2 h}{gb} = \frac{(15)^2 \times 0.7}{9.81 \times 1.3} = \frac{157.5}{12.753} = 12.35 \text{ m}$$

$$\boxed{R_{min} \approx 12.35 \text{ m}}$$

Since actual $R = 30$ m $> R_{min}$, the vehicle is stable. ✓

#### Part (b)

**Effect of gyroscopic couple on aero-engine bearings during a left turn:**

Consider an aero-engine with propeller spinning clockwise (viewed from the rear) at angular velocity $\omega$ about the longitudinal (fore-aft) axis $X$. The aircraft turns left, yawing about the vertical axis $Z$ with angular velocity $\omega_p$.

**Vector Analysis:**
- Spin vector $\vec{\omega}$ points in the $+X$ direction (rear to front).
- Precession vector $\vec{\omega_p}$ points in the $+Z$ direction (upward, for left yaw about CG).
- Angular momentum $\vec{H} = I\vec{\omega}$ along $+X$.
- The active couple on the rotor = $\vec{\omega_p} \times \vec{H}$, which by right-hand rule points in the $-Y$ direction (toward the right wing).
- The **reactive gyroscopic couple** on the frame is in the $+Y$ direction (toward the left wing).

**Effect on bearings:**
The two bearings A (front) and B (rear) experience vertical reactions (if the couple is in the pitch plane) or lateral reactions. Specifically:
- One bearing is pushed **up**
- The other is pushed **down**

**Magnitude:**

$$R_A = R_B = \frac{C}{L} = \frac{I\omega\omega_p}{L}$$

where $L$ is the distance between the two bearings.

These reactions **add to or subtract from** the static reactions due to engine weight, potentially causing bearing failure if not properly designed.
