Turning Moment Diagrams for Four-Stroke IC Engines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET413 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Turning Moment Diagrams for Four-Stroke IC Engines # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Turning Moment (Crank Effort Torque):** The net moment acting on the crankshaft of an IC engine about its axis of rotation, which tends to rotate it. It is the algebraic sum of the *turning moment due to gas pressure* (T_gas) and the *turning moment due to the inertia of the reciprocating parts* (T_inertia). $$T_{\text{net}} = T_{\text{gas}} - T_{\text{inertia}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Turning Moment Diagram (TMD):** A closed plot drawn on a Cartesian plane in which the **abscissa** represents the **crank angle (θ)** for one complete working cycle and the **ordinate** represents the **net turning moment (T_net)** experienced by the crankshaft at that crank position. For a four-stroke engine, the cycle spans **720°** (two full crank revolutions). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bicycle Pedal Imagine you are pedaling a bicycle on a level road. Your effort on the pedals is **not constant** — it rises and falls as the crank rotates: - When your leg is near the **top (TDC)** or **bottom (BDC)**, almost no useful torque is applied — the force is wasted against the axis of rotation. - When your leg is roughly **horizontal (90° from vertical)**, the **maximum useful torque** is delivered. Now imagine the crankshaft of a single-cylinder IC engine. The piston pushes the crank via the connecting rod, and the useful push varies continuously with crank angle. The **TMD is simply a graph that records how "hard" the piston pushes the crank** at every angle during the cycle. > [!TIP] > The crank is "kicked" hardest during the **expansion/power stroke** and is "dragged" slightly during **suction, compression, and exhaust** because the piston has to be moved without any useful work. ## 1.3 The Four Strokes of a 4-Stroke Engine (Crank-Angle Reference) | Stroke | Crank-Angle Range | Function | Gas Pressure (P_g) | Result on Crank | | :--- | :---: | :--- | :---: | :--- | | **Suction** | **0° → 180°** | Fresh charge drawn in | Slightly **< P_atm** | Negative (slight drag) | | **Compression** | **180° → 360°** | Charge compressed | High, **resists** piston | Strongly negative | | **Power (Expansion)** | **360° → 540°** | Combustion gases expand | Very high, **drives** piston | Strongly positive | | **Exhaust** | **540° → 720°** | Burnt gases expelled | Slightly **> P_atm** | Negative (slight drag) | > [!NOTE] > **Sign Convention Used in KTU 2024 Scheme:** > - **Positive T_net** → Crankshaft receives energy (driving torque). > - **Negative T_net** → Crankshaft loses energy or has to be driven (resisting torque). ## 1.4 Why the Turning Moment is NOT Constant Three reasons — all examinable at KTU level: 1. **Geometric reason:** The connecting rod does not pass through the crank centre, so the *effective moment arm* of the piston force changes with θ. 2. **Gas-pressure reason:** P_g varies enormously (≈ 1 bar atmospheric to ≈ 40–60 bar peak during combustion). 3. **Inertia reason:** The piston accelerates and decelerates every stroke, generating an inertia force that adds (algebraically) to the gas force. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Step-by-Step Decomposition of the Net Turning Moment The total resistance offered by the piston to the crank has **two physical origins**: ### Step 1 — Turning Moment due to Gas Pressure (T_gas) Consider the piston–cylinder assembly. The **net effective force** on the piston is: $$F_{\text{gas}} = (P_g - P_o) \cdot A_p$$ where $A_p$ is the cross-sectional area of the piston, $P_g$ is the instantaneous gas pressure above the piston, and $P_o$ is the pressure on the underside (taken as atmospheric or a back-pressure). The **component of this force that does work on the crank** is the **tangential component**, equal to $F_{\text{gas}} \sin\theta$ (after resolving through the connecting rod geometry, with the connecting-rod ratio $n = L/r$): $$\boxed{\;T_{\text{gas}} \;=\; (P_g - P_o)\;A_p\;r\;\left[\sin\theta \;+\; \frac{\sin 2\theta}{n}\right]\;}$$ The second term $\dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{n}$ is the **correction for obliquity of the connecting rod**. It is small (≈ 5–10 %) but is retained for full-marks answers in KTU. ### Step 2 — Turning Moment due to Inertia of Reciprocating Parts (T_inertia) The piston has mass $m$ and acceleration $a$. The approximate piston acceleration is: $$a \;\approx\; \omega^{2} r \left[\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right]$$ The inertia force $F_i = m a$ acts opposite to the acceleration. The tangential component of this force, multiplied by $r$, gives the inertia torque: $$\boxed{\;T_{\text{inertia}} \;\approx\; m\,r^{2}\,\omega^{2}\;\left[\frac{\sin 2\theta}{2} \;+\; \frac{\sin\theta\,\cos 2\theta}{n}\right]\;}$$ A widely used **first-order approximation** (acceptable for KTU short problems) keeps only the dominant harmonic: $$T_{\text{inertia}} \;\approx\; \frac{m\,r^{2}\,\omega^{2}}{2}\,\sin 2\theta$$ ### Step 3 — Combine Them — The Four-Stroke TMD | Stroke | T_gas | T_inertia | T_net = T_gas − T_inertia | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | | **Suction (0°–180°)** | ≈ 0 (slightly negative) | Negative half-cycle | Slightly **negative** | | **Compression (180°–360°)** | Strongly **negative** | Positive half-cycle | Strongly **negative** | | **Power (360°–540°)** | Strongly **positive** | Negative half-cycle | Strongly **positive** | | **Exhaust (540°–720°)** | ≈ 0 (slightly negative) | Positive half-cycle | Slightly **negative** | > [!IMPORTANT] > **The TMD is drawn for the COMPLETE CYCLE (720°), NOT just the power stroke.** This is one of the most common mistakes in KTU answers. ## 2.2 Mean Turning Moment, Fluctuation of Energy, and the Flywheel Link | Concept | Formula | Meaning | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Work done per cycle | $W \;=\; \oint T_{\text{net}}\,d\theta$ | Net area enclosed by the TMD in J | | Mean turning moment (4-stroke) | $T_{\text{mean}} \;=\; \dfrac{W}{4\pi}$ | Average over one complete 720° cycle | | Brake Power | $P \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi N\,T_{\text{mean}}}{60}$ | For 4-stroke, N = working strokes/min = (rpm)/2 | | **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy** | $C_E \;=\; \dfrac{E_{\max} - E_{\min}}{W}$ | Indicates the *energy storage* demand on the flywheel | | Fluctuation of speed | $C_s \;=\; \dfrac{\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min}}{\omega_{\text{mean}}}$ | Indicates the *speed stability* of the engine | | **Moment of Inertia of Flywheel** | $I \;=\; \dfrac{W \cdot C_E}{\omega^{2}\,C_s}$ | **Key flywheel design equation** | > [!WARNING] > **KTU students often write $W/2\pi$ for 4-stroke engines.** This is wrong. Use $W/4\pi$ because one cycle = $4\pi$ rad (= 720°). For **2-stroke** engines, the cycle is $2\pi$ rad, so use $W/2\pi$. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat-Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks | | :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | 1 | Gas turning moment | $T_{\text{gas}} = (P_g - P_o) A_p r \left[\sin\theta + \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{n}\right]$ | N·m | Includes obliquity correction | | 2 | Inertia turning moment (approx.) | $T_{\text{inertia}} = \dfrac{m r^{2}\omega^{2}}{2}\sin 2\theta$ | N·m | First-harmonic approximation | | 3 | Net turning moment | $T_{\text{net}} = T_{\text{gas}} - T_{\text{inertia}}$ | N·m | Algebraic sum | | 4 | Crank effort (force) | $F_{\text{crank}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{net}}}{r}$ | N | Tangential force at crank-pin | | 5 | Work per cycle | $W = \text{Area of TMD}$ | J | Planimeter or trapezoidal rule | | 6 | Mean T.M. (4-stroke) | $T_{\text{mean}} = \dfrac{W}{4\pi}$ | N·m | One cycle = 720° | | 7 | Power (4-stroke) | $P = \dfrac{2\pi N T_{\text{mean}}}{60}$ | W | N in rpm, $N$ = power strokes/min | | 8 | Fluctuation of energy | $\Delta E = E_{\max} - E_{\min}$ | J | Found from TMD by rectifying w.r.t. $T_{\text{mean}}$ | | 9 | Coefficient of fluctuation of energy | $C_E = \dfrac{\Delta E}{W}$ | — | Dimensionless | | 10 | Flywheel inertia | $I = \dfrac{W \cdot C_E}{\omega^{2} C_{s}}$ | kg·m² | **Most asked KTU formula** | | 11 | Flywheel mass (ring type) | $m = \dfrac{I}{k^{2}} = \dfrac{I}{R^{2}}$ | kg | For solid disc $k = R/\sqrt{2}$ | ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Engine designers** use the TMD to size the **flywheel**, which stores kinetic energy during the power stroke and releases it during the other three "lean" strokes. - **Single-cylinder engines** (motorcycles, pumps, small gensets) have a **highly fluctuating TMD** and therefore need a *large* flywheel. - **Multi-cylinder engines** have an overlap of TMDs from individual cylinders, producing a **much smoother resultant TMD**, allowing a smaller flywheel. - The same theory is extended to **compressor drives, printing presses, and punching machines** to determine their flywheels. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Turning Moment due to Inertia of Reciprocating Parts ### Setup Consider a slider-crank mechanism. The piston has mass $m$. Let: - $r$ = crank radius - $L$ = length of connecting rod - $n = L/r$ (connecting-rod ratio, typically 3 to 4 for IC engines) - $\theta$ = crank angle measured from the **Inner Dead Centre (IDC)** i.e., TDC - $\phi$ = angle of connecting rod with the cylinder axis - $\omega$ = angular velocity of crank (assumed constant for analysis) ### Step 1 — Displacement of the Piston The displacement $x$ of the piston from IDC is: $$x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{r}{n}(1 - \cos 2\theta) \cdot \frac{1}{2}$$ Wait — let me write it more carefully using the standard slider-crank identity. With $\sin\phi = \dfrac{r}{L}\sin\theta = \dfrac{\sin\theta}{n}$: $$x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + L(1 - \cos\phi)$$ Approximating $1 - \cos\phi \approx \dfrac{\phi^{2}}{2}$ for small $\phi$, and $\phi \approx \dfrac{\sin\theta}{n}$: $$x \;\approx\; r(1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{L}{2}\left(\frac{\sin\theta}{n}\right)^{2} = r(1 - \cos\theta) + \frac{r}{2n}\sin^{2}\theta$$ Using $\sin^{2}\theta = \dfrac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{2}$: $$x \;=\; r\left[1 - \cos\theta + \frac{1 - \cos 2\theta}{4n}\right]$$ ### Step 2 — Velocity of the Piston Differentiate $x$ w.r.t. time. Note $\dot\theta = \omega$: $$v = \frac{dx}{dt} = \omega\, r \left[\sin\theta + \frac{\sin 2\theta}{2n}\right]$$ ### Step 3 — Acceleration of the Piston Differentiate again: $$a = \frac{dv}{dt} = \omega^{2} r \left[\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right]$$ ### Step 4 — Inertia Force on the Piston By Newton's second law, the inertia force opposing the motion is: $$F_i = m \cdot a = m\,\omega^{2} r\left[\cos\theta + \frac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right]$$ ### Step 5 — Resolve $F_i$ into a Tangential Component Only the **tangential component** $F_i \sin\theta$ does work on the crank (the radial component is balanced by the crank-pin reaction). Thus the inertia torque about the crank centre is: $$T_{\text{inertia}} = F_i \cdot r \cdot \sin\theta$$ Substituting the expression for $F_i$: $$T_{\text{inertia}} = m\,r^{2}\,\omega^{2}\left[\sin\theta\cos\theta + \frac{\sin\theta\cos 2\theta}{n}\right]$$ Using $\sin\theta\cos\theta = \dfrac{\sin 2\theta}{2}$: $$\boxed{\;T_{\text{inertia}} = m\,r^{2}\,\omega^{2}\left[\frac{\sin 2\theta}{2} + \frac{\sin\theta\cos 2\theta}{n}\right]\;}$$ ### Step 6 — Simplification (First-Order Approximation) The second term is small because $n \ge 3$. Dropping it: $$T_{\text{inertia}} \;\approx\; \frac{m\,r^{2}\,\omega^{2}}{2}\,\sin 2\theta$$ This is a **pure sine wave of frequency $2\omega$** (twice the crank speed) — a very useful property when drawing the inertia TMD. ## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example (Typical KTU Numerical) > **Problem Statement (KTU-style):** > A single-cylinder, 4-stroke IC engine has the following data: > - Bore, $D = 0.15\ \text{m}$ > - Stroke, $L_s = 0.20\ \text{m}$ (so crank radius $r = 0.10\ \text{m}$) > - Length of connecting rod, $L = 0.40\ \text{m}$ (so $n = L/r = 4$) > - Mass of reciprocating parts, $m = 1.5\ \text{kg}$ > - Engine speed, $N = 300\ \text{rpm}$ > - Net work done per cycle, $W = 80\ \text{J}$ > - Coefficient of fluctuation of speed, $C_s = 0.02$ > - Assume the fluctuation of energy is $10\%$ of the work per cycle, i.e., $C_E = 0.10$ > > **Determine:** (a) The mass moment of inertia of the flywheel required, (b) The mean turning moment, (c) The brake power developed. ### Part (a) — Moment of Inertia of the Flywheel **Step 1 — Compute the mean angular velocity:** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416\ \text{rad/s}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the standard flywheel design equation:** $$I = \frac{W \cdot C_E}{\omega^{2} \cdot C_s}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute values:** $$I = \frac{80 \times 0.10}{(31.416)^{2} \times 0.02}$$ $$I = \frac{8.0}{986.96 \times 0.02} = \frac{8.0}{19.739} \;\approx\; 0.4052\ \text{kg·m}^{2}$$ $$\boxed{\;I \;\approx\; 0.405\ \text{kg·m}^{2}\;}$$ ### Part (b) — Mean Turning Moment For a 4-stroke engine, one complete cycle spans $4\pi$ radians: $$T_{\text{mean}} = \frac{W}{4\pi} = \frac{80}{4\pi} = \frac{20}{\pi} \;\approx\; 6.366\ \text{N·m}$$ $$\boxed{\;T_{\text{mean}} \;\approx\; 6.37\ \text{N·m}\;}$$ ### Part (c) — Brake Power Developed For a 4-stroke engine, the number of **power strokes per minute** is $N/2$: $$P = \frac{2\pi \left(\dfrac{N}{2}\right) T_{\text{mean}}}{60} = \frac{\pi N T_{\text{mean}}}{60}$$ $$P = \frac{\pi \times 300 \times 6.366}{60} = \frac{5999.4}{60} \;\approx\; 99.99\ \text{W} \;\approx\; 0.10\ \text{kW}$$ $$\boxed{\;P \;\approx\; 0.10\ \text{kW}\;}$$ ### Verification (Cross-Check) We can also write $P = T_{\text{mean}} \cdot \omega / 2$ (the factor 1/2 appears because work is done only once every 2 revolutions): $$P = \frac{6.366 \times 31.416}{2} = \frac{200.0}{2} = 100\ \text{W} \quad \checkmark$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Methodology for KTU answers:** Always (i) state the relevant formula, (ii) substitute values *with units*, (iii) compute, and (iv) box the final answer. Examiners award partial marks for each logical step. ## 3.3 Construction of the TMD from Tabulated Data (Procedure) This is the **graphical procedure** often asked in KTU theory papers: 1. **Divide one cycle (720°) into 12 or 24 equal parts** (i.e., every 30° or 15°). 2. **At each crank angle θ, read** (a) the gas pressure $P_g$ from the indicator diagram and (b) compute $T_{\text{inertia}}$ from the formula. 3. **Compute $T_{\text{gas}}$** from the formula. 4. **Compute $T_{\text{net}} = T_{\text{gas}} - T_{\text{inertia}}$** for the compression and power strokes. 5. **For suction and exhaust**, $T_{\text{net}} = -T_{\text{inertia}}$ (since $P_g \approx P_o$). 6. **Plot** $T_{\text{net}}$ vs. $\theta$. This curve is the **TMD**. > [!TIP] > The **area of the TMD** above the mean-torque line represents the *maximum kinetic energy* the flywheel must absorb. The **area below** is the *minimum energy* it must release. The **algebraic difference** is the *fluctuation of energy* $\Delta E$. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Four-Stroke Turning Moment Diagram — Conceptual Block Map The diagram below maps how the four strokes interact with the crankshaft, the gas forces, and the inertia forces. It is a *functional architecture flow*, not a physical drawing. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Engine Cycle Start
theta = 0 deg TDC] --> B[STROKE 1: SUCTION
0 to 180 deg] B --> C[STROKE 2: COMPRESSION
180 to 360 deg] C --> D[STROKE 3: POWER / EXPANSION
360 to 540 deg] D --> E[STROKE 4: EXHAUST
540 to 720 deg] E --> F[Cycle Completed
720 deg] B --> B1[T_gas is approximately 0] B --> B2[T_inertia is negative
sin 2 theta less than 0] B1 --> B3[T_net is slightly negative] C --> C1[T_gas is strongly negative
gas resists compression] C --> C2[T_inertia is positive
sin 2 theta greater than 0] C1 --> C3[T_net is strongly negative] C2 --> C3 D --> D1[T_gas is strongly positive
combustion drives piston] D --> D2[T_inertia is negative
sin 2 theta less than 0] D1 --> D3[T_net is strongly positive
PEAK ENERGY] D2 --> D3 E --> E1[T_gas is approximately 0] E --> E2[T_inertia is positive
sin 2 theta greater than 0] E1 --> E3[T_net is slightly negative] D3 --> G[FLYWHEEL
stores energy at 360 to 540 deg] B3 --> G C3 --> G E3 --> G G --> H[Releases energy to crank
keeps omega approximately constant] style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff style F fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff style D3 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff style G fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff style H fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff style B3 fill:#d62728,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff style C3 fill:#d62728,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff style E3 fill:#d62728,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.2 Schematic of Crank-Angle Reference (TDC and BDC Positions) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph StartCycle A1[theta = 0 deg
IDC = TDC
Start of SUCTION] --> A2[theta = 180 deg
ODC = BDC
End of SUCTION
Start of COMPRESSION] end subgraph MidCycle A2 --> A3[theta = 360 deg
IDC = TDC
End of COMPRESSION
Start of POWER] A3 --> A4[theta = 540 deg
ODC = BDC
End of POWER
Start of EXHAUST] end subgraph EndCycle A4 --> A5[theta = 720 deg
IDC = TDC
End of EXHAUST
Equal to theta = 0 deg] end style A1 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff style A2 fill:#8c564b,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff style A3 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff style A4 fill:#8c564b,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff style A5 fill:#9467bd,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Composition of the Net Turning Moment ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUTS P1[P_g gas pressure
from indicator diagram] P2[P_o atmospheric or
back pressure] P3[m mass of
reciprocating parts] P4[r omega n L
engine geometry] end subgraph COMPUTE C1[T_gas = P_g minus P_o times A_p times r
times sin theta plus sin 2 theta over n] C2[T_inertia = m r squared omega squared
times sin 2 theta over 2] C3[T_net = T_gas minus T_inertia] end subgraph OUTPUT O1[Turning Moment Diagram
T_net vs theta for 720 deg] O2[Mean Torque T_mean = W over 4 pi] O3[Flywheel Inertia I = W C_E over omega squared C_s] end P1 --> C1 P2 --> C1 P3 --> C2 P4 --> C1 P4 --> C2 C1 --> C3 C2 --> C3 C3 --> O1 O1 --> O2 O1 --> O3 style C3 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff style O1 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff style O3 fill:#d62728,stroke:#333,color:#ffffff ``` > [!NOTE] > **How to read the diagrams:** Green = positive energy flow, red = energy deficit, orange = the flywheel that bridges them. These block diagrams are the *architecture view*; the actual TMD shape (positive lobe over 360°–540° and small negative lobes elsewhere) is a plot in a graph paper, not a mermaid graph. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define the terms: (i) Turning moment, (ii) Crank effort, (iii) Turning moment diagram. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Model Answer:** - **(i) Turning Moment:** The instantaneous moment about the crankshaft axis that tends to rotate the crankshaft. Mathematically, $$T_{\text{net}} = T_{\text{gas}} - T_{\text{inertia}}$$ - **(ii) Crank Effort:** The tangential force acting at the crank pin, expressed as a force, given by $$F_{\text{crank}} = \frac{T_{\text{net}}}{r}$$ where $r$ is the crank radius. - **(iii) Turning Moment Diagram (TMD):** A closed plot of net turning moment on the y-axis and crank angle on the x-axis for one complete working cycle (720° for 4-stroke, 360° for 2-stroke engines). ### **Q2. Why is the turning moment of a single-cylinder 4-stroke engine NOT constant throughout the cycle? [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Answer:** The turning moment varies due to **three reasons**: 1. **Geometric reason** — The connecting rod does not pass through the crank centre; therefore the effective moment arm of the piston force changes with crank angle $\theta$. 2. **Gas-pressure reason** — The cylinder pressure $P_g$ varies from slightly below atmospheric (suction) to a peak of 40–60 bar (combustion peak) and back. 3. **Inertia reason** — The reciprocating piston accelerates and decelerates every stroke, producing a sinusoidal inertia torque of frequency $2\omega$. A constant turning moment would require a perfectly flat TMD, which is physically impossible in a single-cylinder engine. ## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE pattern, you must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified]** **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **turning moment diagram for a 4-stroke IC engine**. Clearly label all four strokes and indicate the sign convention. (7 Marks) **(b)** A single-cylinder 4-stroke engine has a bore of **200 mm**, a stroke of **280 mm**, and runs at **240 rpm**. The mean effective pressure during the power stroke is **0.70 MPa**. Calculate the **(i) work done per cycle**, **(ii) mean turning moment**, and **(iii) brake power** developed. (7 Marks) ### **Model Solution — Question A** **Part (a):** (Sketch + Theory) The TMD for a 4-stroke engine is plotted for one complete cycle of 720°. Label the four strokes as: - **0° to 180°:** Suction (TMD slightly negative) - **180° to 360°:** Compression (TMD strongly negative) - **360° to 540°:** Power/Expansion (TMD strongly positive — large lobe) - **540° to 720°:** Exhaust (TMD slightly negative) The mean turning moment line $T_{\text{mean}}$ is drawn horizontally such that the area above it equals the area below it. The shaded region between the TMD curve and the $T_{\text{mean}}$ line represents the **fluctuation of energy $\Delta E$**. > Sign convention: **Above the x-axis = positive (driving torque); below the x-axis = negative (resisting torque).** **Part (b):** (Numerical) **Step 1 — Piston area:** $$A_p = \frac{\pi D^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.200)^{2}}{4} = 0.03142\ \text{m}^{2}$$ **Step 2 — Stroke volume (swept volume):** $$V_s = A_p \times L_s = 0.03142 \times 0.280 = 0.008796\ \text{m}^{3}$$ **Step 3 — Work done per cycle:** $$W = P_{\text{me}} \times V_s = 0.70 \times 10^{6} \times 0.008796 = 6157\ \text{J} \;\approx\; 6.16\ \text{kJ}$$ $$\boxed{W \;\approx\; 6157\ \text{J}}$$ **Step 4 — Mean turning moment (4-stroke, cycle = $4\pi$ rad):** $$T_{\text{mean}} = \frac{W}{4\pi} = \frac{6157}{4\pi} = 490.0\ \text{N·m}$$ $$\boxed{T_{\text{mean}} \;\approx\; 490\ \text{N·m}}$$ **Step 5 — Brake power (4-stroke):** For a 4-stroke engine, power strokes per minute = $N/2 = 240/2 = 120$: $$P = \frac{2\pi (N/2) T_{\text{mean}}}{60} = \frac{\pi N T_{\text{mean}}}{60} = \frac{\pi \times 240 \times 490.0}{60}$$ $$P = \frac{369{,}640}{60} \;\approx\; 6160\ \text{W} \;\approx\; 6.16\ \text{kW}$$ $$\boxed{P \;\approx\; 6.16\ \text{kW}}$$ **Cross-check:** $P = T_{\text{mean}} \cdot \omega / 2 = 490.0 \times 25.13 / 2 = 6157\ \text{W} \quad \checkmark$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Derive an expression for the **turning moment due to the inertia of the reciprocating parts** of a slider-crank mechanism, considering the obliquity of the connecting rod. (7 Marks) **(b)** A single-cylinder 4-stroke engine has a **crank radius $r = 0.10\ \text{m}$**, **connecting-rod length $L = 0.40\ \text{m}$**, **mass of reciprocating parts $m = 2.0\ \text{kg}$**, and runs at **300 rpm**. The work done per cycle is **100 J** and the coefficient of fluctuation of energy is **0.10**. The coefficient of fluctuation of speed is **0.02**. Determine the **mass of the flywheel** if it is a **solid disc of radius 0.50 m**. (7 Marks) ### **Model Solution — Question B** **Part (a):** (Derivation) (See Section 3.1 above for the full derivation. The expected steps in a 7-mark KTU answer are:) 1. Write piston displacement $x = r(1 - \cos\theta) + \dfrac{r}{2n}\sin^{2}\theta$ (1 mark) 2. Differentiate twice to get acceleration $a = \omega^{2}r\left[\cos\theta + \dfrac{\cos 2\theta}{n}\right]$ (2 marks) 3. Compute inertia force $F_i = m a$ (1 mark) 4. Resolve into tangential component and multiply by $r$: $$T_{\text{inertia}} = m r^{2} \omega^{2}\left[\frac{\sin 2\theta}{2} + \frac{\sin\theta \cos 2\theta}{n}\right] \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$ **Part (b):** (Numerical) **Step 1 — Mean angular velocity:** $$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416\ \text{rad/s}$$ **Step 2 — Flywheel moment of inertia (from $I = \dfrac{W \cdot C_E}{\omega^{2} C_s}$):** $$I = \frac{100 \times 0.10}{(31.416)^{2} \times 0.02} = \frac{10}{19.739} = 0.5066\ \text{kg·m}^{2}$$ **Step 3 — Radius of gyration for a solid disc:** $$k^{2} = \frac{R^{2}}{2} = \frac{(0.50)^{2}}{2} = 0.125\ \text{m}^{2}$$ **Step 4 — Mass of the flywheel:** $$m = \frac{I}{k^{2}} = \frac{0.5066}{0.125} = 4.053\ \text{kg}$$ $$\boxed{m \;\approx\; 4.05\ \text{kg}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Wrong cycle length** — using $2\pi$ instead of $4\pi$ for 4-stroke mean torque. > 2. **Forgetting $C_E$ vs. $C_s$** — $C_E$ is *energy* fluctuation (used with $W$); $C_s$ is *speed* fluctuation (used with $\omega^{2}$). They are different and dimensionally different. > 3. **Power-stroke count** — for 4-stroke, only **one power stroke per 2 revolutions**. Multiplying by the wrong $N$ gives an answer off by a factor of 2. > 4. **Sign of inertia torque** — T_inertia *opposes* the piston's motion; students often get the sign wrong for compression/expansion vs. suction/exhaust. > 5. **Skipping the indicator diagram** — examiners expect the TMD to be drawn *with* the indicator diagram reference. Always state "from the indicator diagram, $P_g$ is read at each θ." --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (for last-minute KTU exam prep):** - ☐ The TMD for a **4-stroke engine** spans **720° (one complete cycle = 2 crank revolutions)**. - ☐ **Crank angle convention:** 0° = TDC (start of suction); 180° = BDC; 360° = TDC (start of power); 540° = BDC; 720° = TDC. - ☐ The **expansion/power stroke** is from **360° to 540°** and produces the **large positive lobe**. - ☐ The **other three strokes** (suction, compression, exhaust) yield **negative torque** (resisting). - ☐ $T_{\text{net}} = T_{\text{gas}} - T_{\text{inertia}}$ — *gas torque drives, inertia torque resists*. - ☐ $T_{\text{gas}} = (P_g - P_o)\,A_p\,r\,[\sin\theta + \sin 2\theta / n]$ — always state units. - ☐ $T_{\text{inertia}} \approx \dfrac{m r^{2} \omega^{2}}{2} \sin 2\theta$ — first-harmonic approximation. - ☐ $T_{\text{mean}} = W / 4\pi$ for 4-stroke; $T_{\text{mean}} = W / 2\pi$ for 2-stroke. - ☐ Power: $P = 2\pi N T_{\text{mean}} / 60$, with $N$ = **power strokes per minute** (so $N_{\text{4-stroke}} = N_{\text{rpm}}/2$). - ☐ **Fluctuation of energy** $\Delta E$ = max area − min area of TMD w.r.t. $T_{\text{mean}}$ line. - ☐ $C_E = \Delta E / W$ (coefficient of fluctuation of energy). - ☐ $C_s = (\omega_{\max} - \omega_{\min})/\omega_{\text{mean}}$ (coefficient of fluctuation of speed). - ☐ **Flywheel inertia equation:** $I = W C_E / (\omega^{2} C_s)$ — the *single most tested formula* in this module. - ☐ Solid disc: $k^{2} = R^{2}/2$; thin rim (ring): $k^{2} = R^{2}$. - ☐ Multi-cylinder engines have a *smoother* resultant TMD and hence need a *smaller* flywheel. - ☐ Always draw the **mean turning moment line** first, then locate the **maximum and minimum energy points** above and below it. - ☐ Read the **indicator diagram** to obtain $P_g(\theta)$ — examiners expect this step in the full TMD construction. > **End of Notes — Turning Moment Diagrams for Four-Stroke IC Engines** > *Module 3 | PEIET413 | KTU 2024 Scheme*

topics.title: Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy, Speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET413 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy, Speed # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 The Two Master Coefficients of a Flywheel In the analysis of **flywheels** and prime movers, two dimensionless ratios are used to quantify how severely the **kinetic energy** and **angular speed** of the shaft pulsate during a working cycle. They form the mathematical backbone of every flywheel design problem in KTU examinations. --- ### A. Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy ($\mathbf{C_E}$) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > The **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy**, denoted $C_E$ (also $K_C$ or $k_e$), is defined as the ratio of the **maximum fluctuation of kinetic energy** of the flywheel to its **mean kinetic energy** during one complete cycle. Mathematically: $$C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{\bar{E}} = \frac{E_{max} - E_{min}}{\bar{E}}$$ where: - $E_{max}$ = Maximum kinetic energy stored in the flywheel - $E_{min}$ = Minimum kinetic energy stored in the flywheel - $\Delta E$ = Fluctuation of energy $= E_{max} - E_{min}$ - $\bar{E}$ = Mean kinetic energy of the flywheel --- ### B. Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed ($\mathbf{C_S}$) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > The **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed**, denoted $C_S$ (also $K_S$ or $k_s$), is defined as the ratio of the **maximum fluctuation of speed** to the **mean speed** of the flywheel. Mathematically: $$C_S = \frac{\Delta N}{\bar{N}} = \frac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{\bar{N}} = \frac{\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}}{\bar{\omega}}$$ where: - $N_{max}, N_{min}$ = Maximum and minimum flywheel speeds (rpm) - $\omega_{max}, \omega_{min}$ = Maximum and minimum angular velocities (rad/s) - $\bar{N}, \bar{\omega}$ = Mean speed and mean angular velocity --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Water Tank & Pump:** > Imagine a water tank (the **flywheel**) connected to a pump (the **prime mover**). > - When the pump delivers **more water** than the outlet demands, the excess water is **stored** in the tank, and the **water level rises** (analogous to speed **increasing** → $E_{max}$). > - When the outlet draws **more water** than the pump supplies, the tank **empties** to make up the difference, and the **water level drops** (analogous to speed **decreasing** → $E_{min}$). > - $C_S$ measures how much the water level **swings** relative to its average level. > - $C_E$ measures how much the **stored energy** (volume × head) swings relative to the mean energy. A **bigger tank** (heavier flywheel) means smaller level swings for the same inflow–outflow mismatch — exactly why flywheels are designed for $C_S$ as small as practically feasible. --- ### 1.3 Typical Numerical Values (Industrial Benchmarks) | Machine Type | $C_S$ Range | $C_E$ Range | |---|---|---| | **Electric Motors, Generators** | 0.01 – 0.02 | 0.02 – 0.04 | | **Punching & Shearing Machines** | 0.20 – 0.30 | 0.40 – 0.60 | | **Rolling Mills, Hammers** | 0.10 – 0.20 | 0.20 – 0.40 | | **I.C. Engines (Single Cyl.)** | 0.02 – 0.05 | 0.04 – 0.10 | | **Textile Machinery** | 0.01 – 0.05 | 0.02 – 0.10 | > [!NOTE] > **Memorize for KTU:** Punching and shearing machines demand the **largest** flywheels precisely because their $C_E$ values are the highest — a single large energy pulse per stroke must be absorbed. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Why Are These Coefficients Important? In a real prime mover, the **crank effort** (tangential force × crank radius) is **non-uniform** during one revolution because the torque required by the driven machinery varies cyclically. Consequently: 1. The **crank effort diagram** shows areas of **surplus energy** and **deficit energy**. 2. The flywheel **absorbs energy** during surplus intervals (speeding up) and **releases energy** during deficit intervals (slowing down). 3. The total energy absorbed and released over one cycle must be **exactly equal** (energy conservation). 4. The peak energy that the flywheel must absorb or deliver is the **maximum fluctuation of energy**, $\Delta E_{max}$. 5. The designer must size the flywheel so that the corresponding speed swing ($\Delta N$ or $\Delta \omega$) stays within acceptable limits. This is precisely what $C_E$ and $C_S$ govern. --- ## 2.2 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear in 90 % of Module 3 flywheel questions.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Kinetic Energy of flywheel | $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ | Joules (J); $I$ in kg·m², $\omega$ in rad/s | | 2 | Fluctuation of Energy | $\Delta E = E_{max} - E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} I (\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2)$ | Joules | | 3 | Mean Kinetic Energy | $\bar{E} = \frac{1}{2} I \bar{\omega}^2$ | Joules, $\bar{\omega} = \frac{\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}}{2}$ | | 4 | Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy | $C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{\bar{E}}$ | Dimensionless | | 5 | Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed | $C_S = \frac{\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}}{\bar{\omega}} = \frac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{\bar{N}}$ | Dimensionless | | 6 | **Key Relationship (Most Important)** | $\boxed{C_E = 2 C_S}$ | Derived for small $C_S$ | | 7 | Exact Relationship | $C_E = C_S \cdot \left(2 + C_S\right) \approx 2 C_S$ | Use for precise calculations | | 8 | Moment of Inertia (rim-type) | $I = m R^2$ | $m$ = mass, $R$ = rim radius | | 9 | Moment of Inertia (solid disc) | $I = \frac{1}{2} m R^2$ | Disc type flywheel | | 10 | Moment of Inertia (spoked) | $I = m (k^2 + R^2)$ | $k$ = radius of gyration of hub | | 11 | Maximum Fluctuation of Energy | $\Delta E_{max} = C_E \cdot \bar{E} = C_E \cdot \frac{1}{2} I \bar{\omega}^2$ | Used to size the flywheel | | 12 | Solving for mass | $m = \frac{2 \cdot \Delta E_{max}}{R^2 \cdot \bar{\omega}^2}$ | Rim-type assumption | | 13 | Working Stress (for rim design) | $\rho v^2 = S_t - S_b$ | $v$ = rim velocity, $S_t$ = tensile stress, $S_b$ = bending stress | --- ## 2.3 Engineering Significance & Real-World Utility | Coefficient | Engineering Meaning | Consequence if Too High | |---|---|---| | $C_S$ | Speed stability / smoothness of motion | **Poor product quality** (e.g., uneven paper roll) | | $C_E$ | Required **energy storage capacity** of flywheel | **Oversized**, expensive flywheel | | Low $C_S$ | Tighter speed regulation | Heavier, costlier flywheel | | High $C_S$ | Lighter flywheel acceptable | Larger speed swings, possible resonance | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** > - **Lathe spindles:** A low $C_S$ ($\approx 0.01$) ensures **uniform cutting speed** and a good surface finish. > - **Power press / punch press:** A high $C_S$ ($\approx 0.20$–$0.30$) is tolerated because a momentary speed dip is acceptable as long as the **impact energy** is delivered. > - **Washing machine drum:** $C_S \approx 0.05$–$0.10$ to absorb the **unbalanced load** during the spin cycle. --- ## 2.4 Logical Flow for Solving a Flywheel Problem 1. From the **crank effort diagram** (or given table of crank-effort vs. crank angle), find the **areas of surplus and deficit energy**. 2. The **maximum fluctuation of energy** $\Delta E_{max}$ corresponds to the **largest algebraic difference** between cumulative energy gained and energy lost during the cycle. 3. Use the **mean speed** $\bar{N}$ (given) and the **permissible $C_S$** (given or assumed) to find $N_{max}$ and $N_{min}$. 4. Compute $\bar{\omega} = 2\pi \bar{N}/60$. 5. Compute the required **moment of inertia** $I = \Delta E_{max} / (C_E \bar{\omega}^2 / 2)$ or $I = 2 \Delta E_{max} / (\bar{\omega}^2 (2 + C_S))$. 6. Find the **mass** $m$ from $I$ and the **radius of gyration** $k$: $I = m k^2$. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Relationship $\mathbf{C_E = 2 C_S}$ (The Heart of Module 3) This is the single most-tested derivation in KTU Module 3. Every step must be memorized and reproducible on paper. ### Given: A flywheel rotating with kinetic energy $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ where the moment of inertia $I$ is constant. ### Step 1 — Write the maximum and minimum kinetic energies. $$E_{max} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_{max}^2$$ $$E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_{min}^2$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the fluctuation of energy. $$\Delta E = E_{max} - E_{min} = \frac{1}{2} I \left(\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2\right)$$ ### Step 3 — Apply the algebraic identity $a^2 - b^2 = (a-b)(a+b)$. $$\Delta E = \frac{1}{2} I \left(\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}\right) \left(\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}\right)$$ ### Step 4 — Express the mean angular velocity. The mean angular velocity is the arithmetic average: $$\bar{\omega} = \frac{\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}}{2}$$ ### Step 5 — Express the mean kinetic energy. $$\bar{E} = \frac{1}{2} I \bar{\omega}^2 = \frac{1}{2} I \left(\frac{\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{1}{8} I \left(\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}\right)^2$$ ### Step 6 — Form the ratio $C_E = \Delta E / \bar{E}$. $$ \begin{aligned} C_E &= \frac{\frac{1}{2} I \left(\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}\right) \left(\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}\right)}{\frac{1}{8} I \left(\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}\right)^2} \\[6pt] &= \frac{\frac{1}{2}\left(\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}\right) \left(\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}\right)}{\frac{1}{8}\left(\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}\right)^2} \\[6pt] &= \frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{8}{1} \cdot \frac{\left(\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}\right)}{\left(\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}\right)} \\[6pt] &= \frac{4 \left(\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}\right)}{\left(\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}\right)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Now form the ratio $C_S = (\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}) / \bar{\omega}$. $$ \begin{aligned} C_S &= \frac{\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}}{\bar{\omega}} = \frac{\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}}{\left(\frac{\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}}{2}\right)} = \frac{2 \left(\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}\right)}{\left(\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}\right)} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 8 — Form the ratio $C_E / C_S$. $$ \begin{aligned} \frac{C_E}{C_S} &= \frac{\frac{4 \left(\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}\right)}{\left(\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}\right)}}{\frac{2 \left(\omega_{max} - \omega_{min}\right)}{\left(\omega_{max} + \omega_{min}\right)}} = \frac{4}{2} = 2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Final Result: $$\boxed{C_E = 2 C_S}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Validity:** This elegant result holds for the **arithmetic mean** of angular velocity, which is the convention in KTU textbooks. The exact relationship $C_E = C_S (2 + C_S)$ is used when $C_S$ is large and greater precision is demanded. --- ## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (Full KTU-Style Solution) **Problem:** A flywheel has a mass of **200 kg** and a radius of gyration of **0.30 m**. It is fitted to an engine running at a mean speed of **300 rpm**. The maximum and minimum speeds during a cycle are found to be **306 rpm** and **294 rpm** respectively. Determine: (a) the coefficient of fluctuation of speed, (b) the coefficient of fluctuation of energy, (c) the maximum fluctuation of energy in kJ, and (d) the maximum energy stored in the flywheel. ### Step 1 — Compute the mean speed and mean angular velocity. $$\bar{N} = 300 \text{ rpm}, \quad \bar{\omega} = \frac{2\pi \bar{N}}{60} = \frac{2\pi \times 300}{60} = 31.416 \text{ rad/s}$$ ### Step 2 — Compute the coefficient of fluctuation of speed $C_S$. $$C_S = \frac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{\bar{N}} = \frac{306 - 294}{300} = \frac{12}{300} = 0.04$$ ### Step 3 — Compute the coefficient of fluctuation of energy $C_E$. $$C_E = 2 C_S = 2 \times 0.04 = 0.08$$ > **Cross-check using exact relation:** $C_E = C_S (2 + C_S) = 0.04 \times 2.04 = 0.0816$ — confirms $0.08$ as a good approximation. ### Step 4 — Compute the moment of inertia of the flywheel. $$I = m k^2 = 200 \times (0.30)^2 = 200 \times 0.09 = 18 \text{ kg·m}^2$$ ### Step 5 — Compute the maximum fluctuation of energy $\Delta E$. $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta E &= \frac{1}{2} I \left(\omega_{max}^2 - \omega_{min}^2\right) \\[4pt] \omega_{max} &= \frac{2\pi \times 306}{60} = 32.044 \text{ rad/s} \\ \omega_{min} &= \frac{2\pi \times 294}{60} = 30.788 \text{ rad/s} \\[4pt] \Delta E &= \frac{1}{2} \times 18 \times \left(32.044^2 - 30.788^2\right) \\ &= 9 \times \left(1026.82 - 947.90\right) \\ &= 9 \times 78.92 \\ &= 710.3 \text{ J} \approx 0.71 \text{ kJ} \end{aligned} $$ > **Alternative check using $C_E$:** $\Delta E = C_E \cdot \bar{E} = 0.08 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 18 \times 31.416^2 = 0.08 \times 8882.6 = 710.6$ J ✓ ### Step 6 — Compute the maximum energy stored in the flywheel. $$\bar{E} = \frac{1}{2} I \bar{\omega}^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 18 \times (31.416)^2 = 8882.6 \text{ J} \approx 8.88 \text{ kJ}$$ $$E_{max} = \bar{E} + \frac{\Delta E}{2} = 8882.6 + 355.3 = 9237.9 \text{ J} \approx 9.24 \text{ kJ}$$ ### Final Tabulated Results: | Quantity | Symbol | Value | |---|---|---| | Coefficient of fluctuation of speed | $C_S$ | **0.04** | | Coefficient of fluctuation of energy | $C_E$ | **0.08** | | Maximum fluctuation of energy | $\Delta E$ | **710.3 J** (≈ 0.71 kJ) | | Mean kinetic energy | $\bar{E}$ | **8882.6 J** (≈ 8.88 kJ) | | Maximum kinetic energy stored | $E_{max}$ | **9237.9 J** (≈ 9.24 kJ) | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Energy & Speed Fluctuation Chain The following **Mermaid** block diagram maps how the **input torque variability** of a prime mover propagates through the flywheel into observable **energy** and **speed** fluctuations. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Prime Mover
e.g. IC Engine] --> B[Torque T theta
Non-uniform over cycle] B --> C[Crank Effort Diagram
Areas of Surplus and Deficit] C --> D[Energy Fluctuation
Delta E max] D --> E[Flywheel
Absorbs and Releases Energy] E --> F1[Kinetic Energy
E equals half I omega squared] E --> F2[Speed Oscillation
N max to N min] F1 --> G1[Coefficient C E
Delta E over E mean] F2 --> G2[Coefficient C S
N max minus N min over N mean] G1 --> H1[Exact Relation
C E equals 2 C S] G2 --> H1 H1 --> I[Design Output
Required Mass m and Inertia I] ``` ## 4.2 Energy Fluctuation vs. Crank Angle — Conceptual Plot The following **Mermaid** chart depicts the typical energy-stored-in-flywheel curve over one working cycle. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Start of cycle
E equals E min] --> B[Surplus region
Flywheel speeds up] B --> C[Peak energy
E equals E max] C --> D[Deficit region
Flywheel slows down] D --> E[End of cycle
Returns to E min] C -- Delta E max --> A ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the curve:** > - The **vertical distance** between $E_{max}$ and $E_{min}$ is the **maximum fluctuation of energy** $\Delta E_{max}$. > - The **horizontal extent** corresponds to the **crank angle** over which the flywheel is either absorbing or releasing energy. > - The **average level** of the curve is $\bar{E}$, the **mean kinetic energy**. ## 4.3 Cause–Effect Matrix of $C_E$ and $C_S$ | Cause | Effect on $C_S$ | Effect on $C_E = 2C_S$ | Flywheel Mass | |---|---|---|---| | $N_{max} - N_{min}$ increases | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | Larger $I$ required | | $\bar{N}$ increases (constant $\Delta N$) | ↓ Decreases | ↓ Decreases | Smaller $I$ required | | $k$ (radius of gyration) increases | — | — | Smaller $m$ for same $I$ | | Punching load added | ↑ Increases | ↑ Increases | **Much larger** flywheel | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls** > 1. **Mixing $N$ and $\omega$ units:** Always convert rpm to rad/s before computing energy. $E$ requires $\omega$ in rad/s, not rpm. > 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in $C_E = 2C_S$:** Many students write $C_E = C_S$. The correct identity is $C_E / C_S = 2$. > 3. **Wrong mean definition:** KTU uses the **arithmetic mean** $\bar{N} = (N_{max} + N_{min})/2$, **not** the geometric mean. > 4. **Sign errors in energy calculation:** $\Delta E$ is always **positive** ($E_{max} - E_{min}$). Take the difference before substituting. > 5. **Unit confusion in $I$:** If the flywheel is rim-type, $I = mR^2$; if disc-type, $I = mR^2/2$. Mark difference: students lose 1 mark for misidentifying the type. --- ## PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Define the terms: (i) Coefficient of fluctuation of energy, and (ii) Coefficient of fluctuation of speed, as applied to a flywheel. State the relationship between them.** **Model Answer:** (i) The **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Energy** ($C_E$) is the ratio of the **maximum fluctuation of kinetic energy** of the flywheel to its **mean kinetic energy** during one working cycle. $$C_E = \frac{\Delta E}{\bar{E}} = \frac{E_{max} - E_{min}}{\bar{E}}$$ (ii) The **Coefficient of Fluctuation of Speed** ($C_S$) is the ratio of the **maximum fluctuation of speed** of the flywheel to its **mean speed**. $$C_S = \frac{\Delta N}{\bar{N}} = \frac{N_{max} - N_{min}}{\bar{N}}$$ **Relationship:** $C_E = 2 C_S$ (for the arithmetic mean convention used in KTU). --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **The crankshaft of an engine runs at a mean speed of 240 rpm. If the fluctuation of speed is ±2 % of the mean speed, determine: (a) the maximum and minimum speeds, and (b) the coefficient of fluctuation of energy.** **Model Answer:** (a) $C_S = 2 / 100 = 0.02$ - $N_{max} = \bar{N}(1 + C_S/2) = 240 \times (1 + 0.01) = 240 \times 1.01 = 242.4$ rpm - $N_{min} = \bar{N}(1 - C_S/2) = 240 \times (1 - 0.01) = 240 \times 0.99 = 237.6$ rpm (b) $C_E = 2 C_S = 2 \times 0.02 = 0.04$ (dimensionless) --- ## PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > KTU ESE Module Pattern: Each Part B question has TWO sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. --- ### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **A punching press operates at 90 strokes per minute. The effective crank radius is 100 mm and the punching load is 25 kN. The press is driven by a motor developing a constant torque. The motor speed at the mean position is 360 rpm and the coefficient of fluctuation of speed is 0.1. Determine: (a) the moment of inertia of the flywheel, and (b) the mass of the rim-type flywheel if the mean radius of the rim is 350 mm.** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Moment of Inertia (7 marks) **Step 1:** Energy required per stroke to punch the metal: $$E_{stroke} = F \times \text{crank radius} = 25{,}000 \text{ N} \times 0.100 \text{ m} = 2500 \text{ J}$$ **Step 2:** Strokes per second $= 90/60 = 1.5$ strokes/s Power supplied by motor = Energy/stroke × strokes/s $= 2500 \times 1.5 = 3750$ W = 3.75 kW. **Step 3:** Energy supplied by motor **per stroke** is delivered uniformly; the **peak energy** absorbed by the flywheel in one stroke is: $$\Delta E_{max} = F \times r = 2500 \text{ J}$$ **Step 4:** Mean angular velocity: $$\bar{\omega} = \frac{2\pi \times 360}{60} = 37.699 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 5:** $C_E = 2 C_S = 2 \times 0.1 = 0.2$. **Step 6:** Solve for $I$: $$ \begin{aligned} \Delta E &= C_E \cdot \frac{1}{2} I \bar{\omega}^2 \\ 2500 &= 0.2 \times \frac{1}{2} \times I \times (37.699)^2 \\ 2500 &= 0.2 \times 0.5 \times 1421.22 \times I \\ 2500 &= 142.122 \times I \\ I &= \frac{2500}{142.122} = 17.59 \text{ kg·m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** $\boxed{I = 17.59 \text{ kg·m}^2}$ #### Part (b) — Mass of rim-type flywheel (7 marks) For a rim-type flywheel, the entire mass is concentrated at the rim: $$I = m R^2 \implies m = \frac{I}{R^2} = \frac{17.59}{(0.350)^2} = \frac{17.59}{0.1225} = 143.6 \text{ kg}$$ **Result:** $\boxed{m = 143.6 \text{ kg}}$ --- ### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 — Alternative Choice] **The turning moment diagram for a multi-cylinder engine has been simplified as follows: for the first 90° of crank travel the torque is +1000 N·m; for the next 90° the torque is – 500 N·m; and for the remaining 180° the torque is +500 N·m. The mean speed is 600 rpm and the coefficient of fluctuation of speed is 0.02. Determine: (a) the maximum fluctuation of energy, and (b) the moment of inertia of the flywheel required.** **Model Solution:** #### Part (a) — Maximum Fluctuation of Energy (7 marks) **Step 1:** Convert torque diagram to energy diagram by multiplying each segment by the crank angle (in radians). **Step 2:** Torque × angle for each segment: - Segment 1 (0°–90°): $\theta_1 = \pi/2$ rad → $E_1 = +1000 \times \pi/2 = +1570.8$ J - Segment 2 (90°–180°): $\theta_2 = \pi/2$ rad → $E_2 = -500 \times \pi/2 = -785.4$ J - Segment 3 (180°–360°): $\theta_3 = \pi$ rad → $E_3 = +500 \times \pi = +1570.8$ J **Net work** $= 1570.8 - 785.4 + 1570.8 = +2356.2$ J (this is the **net work done per cycle**). **Step 3:** Build the cumulative energy (running sum): | Crank angle | $\Delta E$ (J) | Cumulative (J) | |---|---|---| | 0° | 0 | 0 (reference) | | 90° | +1570.8 | +1570.8 | | 180° | –785.4 | +785.4 (minimum cumulative) | | 360° | +1570.8 | +2356.2 (maximum cumulative) | **Step 4:** Subtract a constant reference (so the cycle ends at zero). Let $E_{ref}$ be the **mean level**, where: $$E_{ref} = \frac{E_{max, cum} + E_{min, cum}}{2} = \frac{2356.2 + 785.4}{2} = 1570.8 \text{ J}$$ **Step 5:** Maximum fluctuation: $$\Delta E_{max} = E_{max, cum} - E_{min, cum} = 2356.2 - 785.4 = 1570.8 \text{ J}$$ **Result:** $\boxed{\Delta E_{max} = 1570.8 \text{ J} \approx 1.57 \text{ kJ}}$ #### Part (b) — Moment of Inertia (7 marks) **Step 1:** Mean angular velocity: $$\bar{\omega} = \frac{2\pi \times 600}{60} = 62.832 \text{ rad/s}$$ **Step 2:** $C_E = 2 C_S = 2 \times 0.02 = 0.04$. **Step 3:** Solve for $I$: $$ \begin{aligned} I &= \frac{2 \Delta E_{max}}{C_E \cdot \bar{\omega}^2} \\[4pt] &= \frac{2 \times 1570.8}{0.04 \times (62.832)^2} \\[4pt] &= \frac{3141.6}{0.04 \times 3947.84} \\[4pt] &= \frac{3141.6}{157.91} = 19.89 \text{ kg·m}^2 \end{aligned} $$ **Result:** $\boxed{I = 19.89 \text{ kg·m}^2}$ --- ## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Revision Checklist — Print and Pin to Your Wall!** - [ ] $C_E = \Delta E / \bar{E}$ — fluctuation of energy to mean energy. - [ ] $C_S = \Delta N / \bar{N}$ — fluctuation of speed to mean speed. - [ ] **The golden relationship:** $C_E = 2 C_S$ (KTU convention, arithmetic mean). - [ ] **Exact (large-$C_S$) relationship:** $C_E = C_S (2 + C_S)$. - [ ] Energy of a flywheel: $E = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2$ (always use **rad/s** for $\omega$). - [ ] Rim-type flywheel: $I = m R^2$. Disc-type: $I = m R^2 / 2$. - [ ] To convert $N$ (rpm) → $\omega$ (rad/s): $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. - [ ] Mean speed: $\bar{N} = (N_{max} + N_{min}) / 2$ (arithmetic). - [ ] Maximum and minimum speeds: $N_{max} = \bar{N} (1 + C_S/2)$, $N_{min} = \bar{N} (1 - C_S/2)$. - [ ] Required moment of inertia: $I = 2 \Delta E_{max} / (\bar{\omega}^2 (2 + C_S)) = 2 \Delta E_{max} / (2 C_S \bar{\omega}^2)$ approximately. - [ ] Required mass (rim): $m = 2 \Delta E_{max} / (C_E R^2 \bar{\omega}^2) = \Delta E_{max} / (C_S R^2 \bar{\omega}^2)$. - [ ] The **maximum fluctuation of energy** is found from the **crank-effort diagram** as the largest algebraic difference between energy gained and energy lost at any crank angle in the cycle. - [ ] **Punching & shearing machines** have the **highest** $C_E$ and $C_S$ → need the **largest** flywheel. - [ ] **Electric motors & generators** have the **lowest** $C_S$ → smallest flywheel. - [ ] For a constant-torque load + constant-torque prime mover, no flywheel is theoretically required ($C_S = 0$). - [ ] Always state $C_E = 2 C_S$ with the **arithmetic mean** assumption to score full marks in KTU valuation. - [ ] When the **crank radius** $r$ is given, the energy per stroke is $F \times r$ for a punch press problem.

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Damped Free Vibration — Formal Definition
A **damped vibrating system** is one in which energy is progressively dissipated (usually as heat) by a **damper** (dashpot), causing the amplitude of oscillation to decay with time. The element responsible for this energy loss is the **dashpot**, modelled mathematically as a viscous damper producing a force proportional to velocity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight**
> In real machines, *un-damped* vibrations do not exist. Energy loss is always present due to internal/external friction, air resistance, or material hysteresis. Studying damped vibrations is therefore essential for predicting how quickly a machine comes to rest after a disturbance.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Bumper Car"
Imagine pushing a child on a swing. If you push once and walk away, the swing eventually stops. **Why?** Because the swing's hinges (chains rubbing on hooks) and air drag continuously steal energy. This is exactly what a **damper** does in a mechanical system — it acts like the friction in those hinges. The harder/faster the swing moves, the more the damper resists. That is the essence of **viscous damping** — resistance proportional to velocity.

### 1.3 Critical Damping — Definition
**Critical damping** is the *smallest* amount of damping that prevents the system from oscillating at all. At critical damping, the system returns to equilibrium in the **shortest possible time without overshooting**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical meaning:** Critically damped systems behave like the suspension of a luxury car — they absorb a bump smoothly and settle quickly, never bouncing up and down.

### 1.4 Logarithmic Decrement — Definition
**Logarithmic decrement (δ)** is defined as the **natural logarithm of the ratio of successive amplitudes** of a damped oscillation, measured one full cycle apart.

$$\delta = \ln\left(\frac{x_1}{x_2}\right)$$

It is a pure number (dimensionless) and is the standard KTU-approved way to experimentally measure the damping ratio of a vibrating system.

### 1.5 Forced Harmonic Vibration — Definition
When a system is subjected to a **continuous external force** that varies sinusoidally with time, the resulting motion is called **forced harmonic vibration**. The system vibrates at the **frequency of the applied force**, not at its natural frequency.

### 1.6 Rotating Unbalance — Definition
**Rotating unbalance** occurs in machines where the mass centre of a rotating component does **not coincide with its geometric centre (axis of rotation)**. The centrifugal force from this eccentric mass creates a harmonic excitation force — a major source of vibration in turbines, motors, and pumps.

### 1.7 Magnification Factor (MF) — Definition
**Magnification Factor** is the ratio of the **dynamic amplitude** (under forced vibration) to the **static deflection** that the same force would produce if applied statically. It quantifies how severely a system amplifies vibrations near resonance.

$$MF = \frac{X_{dynamic}}{X_{static}}$$

### 1.8 Resonance — Definition
**Resonance** is the phenomenon in which the **frequency of the applied harmonic force** equals (or is very close to) the **natural frequency** of the system. The resulting amplitude theoretically becomes infinite (for an undamped system) and dangerously large (for lightly damped systems), often leading to catastrophic structural failure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Warning:** The famous Tacoma Narrows Bridge collapse (1940) and many machine failures are textbook cases of resonance. KTU examiners frequently test this concept.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Standard Damped SDOF Model

A single-degree-of-freedom (SDOF) damped system consists of three parallel elements:
- **Mass (m)** — stores kinetic energy
- **Spring (k)** — stores potential energy
- **Dashpot (c)** — dissipates energy as heat

```
        ┌──[ m ]──┐
   ─────┤         ├─────  (ground)
        └──// c //─┘
         \\ k \\────
```

### 2.2 Equation of Motion — Damped Free Vibration

Applying Newton's second law to the mass:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

Dividing throughout by *m*:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

where:
- $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}}$ = **natural (undamped) frequency**, rad/s
- $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{c_c} = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}}$ = **damping ratio** (dimensionless)
- $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ = **critical damping coefficient**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the form $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$?**
> Because the roots of its characteristic equation take a clean, easy-to-interpret form: $-\zeta\omega_n \pm \omega_n\sqrt{\zeta^2 - 1}$, allowing direct classification of damping types.

### 2.3 The Three Damping Regimes

| Damping Type | Condition on ζ | Roots Character | Motion Behaviour |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Underdamped** | $0 < \zeta < 1$ | Complex conjugates | Oscillates with decaying amplitude |
| **Critically damped** | $\zeta = 1$ | Repeated real root | Returns to equilibrium fastest, **no oscillation** |
| **Overdamped** | $\zeta > 1$ | Two distinct real negative roots | Slow exponential return, no oscillation |
| **Undamped** | $\zeta = 0$ | Purely imaginary roots | Pure sustained sinusoid |

### 2.4 Underdamped Response

For $0 < \zeta < 1$, define **damped natural frequency**:

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$$

The displacement is:

$$x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}\sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$$

- $X$ = initial amplitude
- $\phi$ = phase angle (depends on initial conditions)
- The envelope $\pm X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t}$ decays exponentially.

### 2.5 Logarithmic Decrement — Analytical Derivation Sketch

If $x_1$ and $x_2$ are successive peak amplitudes (one period $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$ apart):

$$\frac{x_1}{x_2} = \frac{X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_1}}{X e^{-\zeta\omega_n (t_1 + T_d)}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d}$$

Taking the natural log:

$$\delta = \ln\left(\frac{x_1}{x_2}\right) = \zeta\omega_n T_d = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

For **light damping** ($\zeta \ll 1$):

$$\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$$

### 2.6 Forced Harmonic Vibration — Equation of Motion

When a harmonic force $F(t) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$ acts:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

The **steady-state (particular) solution** is:

$$x_p(t) = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$$

where:

$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}} = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

and

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{c\omega}{k - m\omega^2}\right) = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2\zeta r}{1-r^2}\right)$$

with the **frequency ratio** $r = \omega/\omega_n$.

### 2.7 Magnification Factor

$$MF = \frac{X}{X_{static}} = \frac{X}{F_0/k} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The MF is the single most tested expression in KTU Module 4 problems. Memorise it and the resonance peak.

### 2.8 Resonance Peak

Resonance occurs at $r = 1$ (i.e., $\omega = \omega_n$). Substituting into MF:

$$MF_{max} = \frac{1}{2\zeta}$$

For light damping, MF can be extremely large — which is why even small unbalance forces cause massive vibration at resonance.

### 2.9 Rotating Unbalance — The Key Result

For a rotor of mass $m$ with an eccentricity $e$ rotating at angular speed $\omega$:

$$m_e = m \cdot e \quad \text{(unbalance mass moment, kg·m)}$$

The centrifugal force in the rotating frame:

$$F_0 = m_e \, \omega^2 = m \cdot e \cdot \omega^2$$

Substituting into the amplitude formula with $r = \omega/\omega_n$:

$$X = \frac{m \cdot e \cdot \omega^2 / k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} = \frac{e \cdot r^2}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Notice the **$r^2$ in the numerator** — this is a signature feature of rotating unbalance problems. Standard forced-vibration amplitude uses just $F_0/k$. The KTU paper often tests this distinction.

### 2.10 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Natural frequency | $\omega_n = \sqrt{k/m}$ | rad/s |
| 2 | Damped natural freq. | $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ | rad/s |
| 3 | Critical damping coeff. | $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$ | N·s/m |
| 4 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = c/c_c$ | — |
| 5 | Time period (damped) | $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$ | s |
| 6 | Logarithmic decrement | $\delta = (2\pi\zeta)/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \approx 2\pi\zeta$ | — |
| 7 | Static deflection | $X_{static} = F_0/k$ | m |
| 8 | Forced-vibration amp. | $X = (F_0/k)/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ | m |
| 9 | Magnification factor | $MF = 1/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ | — |
| 10 | MF at resonance | $MF_{max} = 1/(2\zeta)$ | — |
| 11 | Phase angle | $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left[2\zeta r/(1-r^2)\right]$ | rad |
| 12 | Unbalance force | $F_0 = m e \omega^2$ | N |
| 13 | Unbalance amplitude | $X = e r^2 / \sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ | m |
| 14 | Transmissibility | $TR = \sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Tip for KTU:** In tables, the absolute value of *x* is written as $\vert x \vert$, **never** as `|x|`. This avoids corrupting the markdown structure.

### 2.11 Engineering Applications
- **Automotive suspension** — uses damping near critical to absorb road shocks.
- **Building earthquake design** — tuned mass dampers reduce resonance risk.
- **Turbomachinery** — balancing rotors eliminates rotating unbalance.
- **Instrumentation** — seismographs rely on critical damping for accurate recording.
- **Manufacturing** — machine tool isolation requires $r \gg 1$ to reduce transmissibility.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Equation of Motion for Damped SDOF System

**Step 1:** Consider a mass *m* attached to a spring of stiffness *k* and a viscous damper of coefficient *c*, all moving in one direction *x*.

**Step 2:** Free-body diagram forces on the mass:
- Inertia force (d'Alembert): $-m\ddot{x}$
- Spring restoring force: $-kx$
- Damping force: $-c\dot{x}$ (opposes velocity)

**Step 3:** Apply Newton's second law / d'Alembert's principle (sum of forces = 0 for dynamic equilibrium):

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = 0$$

**Step 4:** Divide the entire equation by *m*:

$$\ddot{x} + \frac{c}{m}\dot{x} + \frac{k}{m}x = 0$$

**Step 5:** Substitute standard symbols $\omega_n^2 = k/m$ and $2\zeta\omega_n = c/m$:

$$\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = 0$$

This is the **standard form** universally used in KTU textbooks. End of derivation.

---

### 3.2 Derivation — Logarithmic Decrement

**Step 1:** For the underdamped case, the general solution is:

$$x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t} \sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$$

**Step 2:** Successive peaks occur half a damped period apart. Let the first peak be at $t_1$ and the second at $t_2 = t_1 + T_d$, where $T_d = 2\pi/\omega_d$.

**Step 3:** The amplitude of the envelope at $t_1$ is $X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_1}$ and at $t_2$ is $X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_2}$.

**Step 4:** Ratio of successive amplitudes:

$$\frac{x_1}{x_2} = \frac{X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_1}}{X e^{-\zeta\omega_n(t_1 + T_d)}} = \frac{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_1}}{e^{-\zeta\omega_n t_1} \cdot e^{-\zeta\omega_n T_d}} = e^{\zeta\omega_n T_d}$$

**Step 5:** Take the natural logarithm to get $\delta$:

$$\delta = \ln\left(\frac{x_1}{x_2}\right) = \zeta\omega_n T_d = \zeta\omega_n \cdot \frac{2\pi}{\omega_d}$$

**Step 6:** Substitute $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$:

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta\omega_n}{\omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

**Step 7:** For small $\zeta$ (light damping), $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \approx 1$:

$$\boxed{\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta}$$

**Step 8:** Solving for the damping ratio:

$$\zeta = \frac{\delta}{\sqrt{4\pi^2 + \delta^2}}$$

This is the KTU-approved formula to extract $\zeta$ from measured decay data.

---

### 3.3 Derivation — Steady-State Amplitude for Forced Vibration

**Step 1:** Start with the forced equation:

$$m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 2:** Assume a particular solution of the form $x_p = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$.

**Step 3:** Compute derivatives:
- $\dot{x}_p = X\omega \cos(\omega t - \phi)$
- $\ddot{x}_p = -X\omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi)$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the ODE:

$$-mX\omega^2 \sin(\omega t - \phi) + cX\omega \cos(\omega t - \phi) + kX\sin(\omega t - \phi) = F_0 \sin(\omega t)$$

**Step 5:** Group sine and cosine terms, then expand the RHS using the identity $\sin(\omega t) = \sin[(\omega t - \phi) + \phi]$:

$$\sin(\omega t) = \sin(\omega t - \phi)\cos\phi + \cos(\omega t - \phi)\sin\phi$$

**Step 6:** Equate coefficients of $\sin(\omega t - \phi)$ and $\cos(\omega t - \phi)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Sine:} \quad & (k - m\omega^2)X = F_0 \cos\phi \\
\text{Cosine:} \quad & c\omega X = F_0 \sin\phi
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7:** Square and add both equations:

$$X^2 (k - m\omega^2)^2 + X^2 (c\omega)^2 = F_0^2 (\cos^2\phi + \sin^2\phi) = F_0^2$$

**Step 8:** Solve for *X*:

$$X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 9:** Divide numerator and denominator by *k*, and use $r = \omega/\omega_n$:

$$X = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

End of derivation.

---

### 3.4 Derivation — Magnification Factor Peak (Resonance)

**Step 1:** Start with:

$$MF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Step 2:** To find the maximum, minimize the denominator. Differentiate $f(r) = (1-r^2)^2 + 4\zeta^2 r^2$ w.r.t. *r*:

$$\frac{df}{dr} = 2(1-r^2)(-2r) + 8\zeta^2 r = -4r(1 - r^2 - 2\zeta^2) + 8\zeta^2 r = 0$$

Wait — re-evaluate carefully:

$$\frac{df}{dr} = 2(1-r^2)(-2r) + 8\zeta^2 r = -4r(1 - r^2) + 8\zeta^2 r$$

**Step 3:** Set equal to zero (for $r \neq 0$):

$$-4r(1 - r^2) + 8\zeta^2 r = 0 \Rightarrow 1 - r^2 - 2\zeta^2 = 0 \Rightarrow r^2 = 1 - 2\zeta^2$$

So the *true* peak occurs at $r_{peak} = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$, **not exactly at** $r = 1$.

**Step 4:** Substituting $r^2 = 1 - 2\zeta^2$ back:

$$MF_{max} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(2\zeta^2)^2 + 4\zeta^2(1 - 2\zeta^2)}} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Caveat:** The simple formula $MF_{max} = 1/(2\zeta)$ applies **only at the nominal resonance** $r = 1$ (which is what most KTU problems assume). The *true* maximum uses the expression above. Be sure to read the question wording.

---

### 3.5 Derivation — Rotating Unbalance Amplitude

**Step 1:** The unbalance force in magnitude is $F_0 = m \cdot e \cdot \omega^2$ (centrifugal force).

**Step 2:** Substitute into the general forced amplitude formula:

$$X = \frac{m e \omega^2}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 3:** Divide numerator and denominator by $k$, noting $\omega^2 / k = (r^2 \omega_n^2)/k = r^2/m$:

$$X = \frac{m e \cdot r^2 / m}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} = \frac{e r^2}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Step 4:** For very high $\omega$ ($r \gg 1$):

$$X \approx \frac{e r^2}{r^2} = e$$

So the vibration amplitude asymptotically approaches the **eccentricity** itself.

**Step 5:** For very low $\omega$ ($r \to 0$):

$$X \approx \frac{e r^2}{1} = 0$$

So a slowly rotating unbalance produces almost no vibration. End of derivation.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Resonance at a Glance

| Quantity | Value at Resonance ($r=1$) | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| $MF$ | $1/(2\zeta)$ | Amplification factor |
| $X$ | $F_0/(2\zeta k)$ | Peak dynamic amplitude |
| $\phi$ | $\tan^{-1}(\infty) = 90°$ | Force leads displacement by 90° |
| Energy dissipated per cycle | Maximum | Damper works hardest |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** At resonance, displacement lags the force by exactly 90°, meaning the force is in phase with velocity — the damper is therefore absorbing the maximum possible energy. This is *why* resonance amplitudes blow up.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Damped SDOF System Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input: Initial displacement x0 and velocity v0] --> B[Mass m stores kinetic energy]
    A --> C[Spring k stores potential energy]
    A --> D[Damper c dissipates energy as heat]
    B --> E[Combine forces: m x double dot + c x dot + k x = 0]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F{Solve ODE}
    F -->|zeta less than 1| G1[Underdamped: oscillation with decay]
    F -->|zeta equals 1| G2[Critically damped: fast return, no oscillation]
    F -->|zeta greater than 1| G3[Overdamped: slow exponential decay]
    G1 --> H[Output: x of t = X e power minus zeta omega n t sin omega d t plus phi]
    G2 --> I[Output: x of t = C1 plus C2 t all times e power minus omega n t]
    G3 --> J[Output: x of t = C1 e power s1 t plus C2 e power s2 t]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Forced Vibration System Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[External Force F0 sin omega t] --> B[Acts on SDOF system m, c, k]
    B --> C[Equation: m x double dot + c x dot + k x = F0 sin omega t]
    C --> D[Solve using assumed solution X sin omega t minus phi]
    D --> E{Compute r equals omega by omega n}
    E -->|r less than 1| F1[Stiffness controlled: phase phi small]
    E -->|r equals 1| F2[Resonance: maximum amplitude, phi equals 90 degrees]
    E -->|r greater than 1| F3[Mass controlled: phase phi approaches 180 degrees]
    F1 --> G[Amplitude X depends on zeta and r]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    G --> H[Compute MF equals X divided by X static]
    G --> I[Plot response curve X versus omega]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Rotating Unbalance Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Rotor of mass m rotating at omega] --> B{Is mass centre aligned with rotation axis?}
    B -->|No, eccentricity e| C[Unbalance mass moment me = m times e]
    B -->|Yes| D[Perfectly balanced: no vibration]
    C --> E[Centrifugal force F0 = me omega squared]
    E --> F[Force is harmonic in rotating frame]
    F --> G[Apply forced vibration formula]
    G --> H[Amplitude X = e r squared over square root of 1 minus r squared squared plus 2 zeta r squared]
    H --> I{Speed region}
    I -->|r less than 1 subresonant| J1[Amplitude grows as r squared]
    I -->|r equals 1| J2[Resonance: large amplitude]
    I -->|r greater than 1 superresonant| J3[Amplitude approaches e as r increases]
```

### 4.4 Conceptual Response Curve (Schematic Description)

For the magnification factor $MF$ plotted against the frequency ratio $r$:

- **Vertical axis:** $MF$ (dimensionless, range 0 to large)
- **Horizontal axis:** $r = \omega/\omega_n$ (dimensionless, range 0 to 3)
- **Behaviour:**
  - As $r \to 0$: $MF \to 1$ (system follows the force quasi-statically)
  - As $r \to 1$: $MF$ peaks at $1/(2\zeta)$ (resonance)
  - As $r \to \infty$: $MF \to 0$ (mass cannot follow high-frequency force)
- **Effect of $\zeta$:** Higher damping ⇒ lower and broader peak; lower damping ⇒ taller and sharper peak (more dangerous at resonance).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this curve matters:** In KTU viva questions, students are often asked to "sketch MF vs r for different damping ratios." The qualitative shape (peak at r = 1, asymptotic limits of 1 and 0) is what examiners look for.
