## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
> **Define logarithmic decrement. Derive its expression in terms of the damping ratio.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Logarithmic decrement $\delta$ is the natural logarithm of the ratio of any two successive peak amplitudes of a damped free vibration, separated by one full period $T_d$.

$$\delta = \ln\left(\frac{x_1}{x_2}\right) = \zeta\omega_n T_d = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$$

For small damping ($\zeta \ll 1$), this reduces to $\delta \approx 2\pi\zeta$.

**Mark split:** 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for derivation expression.

---

> **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
> **What is critical damping? Why is it important in instrument design?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Critical damping is the *minimum* damping required to prevent oscillation. Its coefficient is $c_c = 2\sqrt{km} = 2m\omega_n$, corresponding to damping ratio $\zeta = 1$.

A critically damped system returns to equilibrium in the **shortest possible time without overshooting**. This is essential in instruments like galvanometers, seismographs, and pressure gauges, where the pointer must settle to the correct reading quickly and accurately, without swinging back and forth. An under-damped instrument oscillates (annoying), while over-damped instruments are sluggish.

**Mark split:** 1 mark for definition + formula, 1 mark for importance, 1 mark for examples.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> **Q3. (A)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]
> **(a)** A machine component of mass **15 kg** is supported by a spring of stiffness **1200 N/m** and a damper. The system is observed to have a damping ratio of **ζ = 0.20**.
>   (i) Determine the natural frequency, damped natural frequency, and critical damping coefficient.
>   (ii) If the initial displacement is **0.05 m** and initial velocity is zero, find the displacement at **t = 1.5 s**.
>   (iii) Calculate the logarithmic decrement. **[7 marks]**

> **(b)** Define **magnification factor**. For a damped SDOF system subjected to a harmonic force, derive the expression for the magnification factor and find the frequency at which the peak amplitude occurs. What is the value of MF at resonance? **[7 marks]**

---

#### Solution to Q3(A)(a)

**Given:** $m = 15$ kg, $k = 1200$ N/m, $\zeta = 0.20$, $x_0 = 0.05$ m, $\dot{x}_0 = 0$, $t = 1.5$ s.

**(i) Natural frequency:**

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{1200}{15}} = \sqrt{80} \approx 8.944 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Damped natural frequency:**

$$\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} = 8.944 \cdot \sqrt{1 - 0.04} = 8.944 \cdot 0.9798 \approx 8.763 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Critical damping coefficient:**

$$c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2 \cdot 15 \cdot 8.944 = 268.32 \text{ N·s/m}$$

**(ii) Displacement at t = 1.5 s:**

For zero initial velocity, the general solution is:

$$x(t) = \frac{x_0}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \cdot e^{-\zeta\omega_n t} \cdot \sin(\omega_d t + \phi_0)$$

where $\phi_0 = \cos^{-1}(\zeta) = \cos^{-1}(0.2) \approx 1.3694$ rad.

$$\frac{x_0}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{0.05}{0.9798} \approx 0.05103 \text{ m}$$

$$\zeta\omega_n t = 0.2 \cdot 8.944 \cdot 1.5 = 2.6832 \Rightarrow e^{-2.6832} \approx 0.06843$$

$$\omega_d t = 8.763 \cdot 1.5 = 13.1445 \text{ rad}$$

$$\omega_d t + \phi_0 = 13.1445 + 1.3694 = 14.5139 \text{ rad}$$

$$\sin(14.5139) = \sin(14.5139 - 4\pi) = \sin(14.5139 - 12.5664) = \sin(1.9475) \approx 0.9287$$

$$x(1.5) = 0.05103 \cdot 0.06843 \cdot 0.9287 \approx 0.003243 \text{ m} \approx 3.24 \text{ mm}$$

**(iii) Logarithmic decrement:**

$$\delta = \frac{2\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 0.2}{0.9798} = \frac{1.2566}{0.9798} \approx 1.283$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistake:** Students often write $x_0$ as the amplitude directly. When initial velocity is zero, the amplitude of the sinusoid is $x_0/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$, not $x_0$. Forgetting the $\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ factor loses 1 mark.

---

#### Solution to Q3(A)(b)

**Definition:** Magnification Factor is the ratio of dynamic amplitude *X* under forced vibration to the static deflection $X_{static} = F_0/k$ that the same force would produce if applied statically.

**Derivation:** Starting from $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0\sin(\omega t)$, assume $x_p = X\sin(\omega t - \phi)$. Substituting and solving (as shown in Section 3.3):

$$X = \frac{F_0/k}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

Therefore:

$$MF = \frac{X}{F_0/k} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

**Frequency at peak amplitude:** Differentiate denominator-squared w.r.t. *r* and set to zero:

$$r_{peak} = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$

**MF at resonance (r = 1):**

$$MF_{max} = \frac{1}{2\zeta}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout (KTU)**
> 1. Many students confuse **$r_{peak}$** (true peak) with **resonance** (r = 1). KTU boards specifically test this by asking "frequency at which peak occurs" — if you write only $r = 1$, you lose 1 mark.
> 2. Always carry units in numerical answers (rad/s for frequency, N·s/m for damping).
> 3. Skipping the derivation of the phase angle $\phi$ when the question says "derive the expression" is a 2-mark deduction.

---

> **Q3. (B)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]
> **(a)** A single-cylinder reciprocating compressor has a rotating mass of **50 kg** with a mass centre offset of **0.4 mm** from the rotation axis. The supporting structure has an equivalent stiffness of **2 × 10⁵ N/m** and a damping ratio of **0.15**. The compressor runs at **1500 rpm**.
>   (i) Calculate the amplitude of forced vibration.
>   (ii) If the running speed is changed to **1800 rpm**, find the new amplitude. **[7 marks]**

> **(b)** Explain the phenomenon of **resonance** in forced vibrations. With the help of the magnification factor plot, discuss how damping influences resonance peak height. A machine has a natural frequency of **20 Hz** and is operated at **20 Hz** with damping ratio **0.05**. Find the magnification factor and comment on the safety of operation. **[7 marks]**

---

#### Solution to Q3(B)(a)

**Given:** $m = 50$ kg, $e = 0.4 \text{ mm} = 0.0004$ m, $k = 2 \times 10^5$ N/m, $\zeta = 0.15$.

**(i) At 1500 rpm:**

$$\omega = \frac{2\pi N}{60} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 1500}{60} = 157.08 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^5}{50}} = \sqrt{4000} = 63.246 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{157.08}{63.246} = 2.484$$

Unbalance force: $F_0 = m \cdot e \cdot \omega^2 = 50 \cdot 0.0004 \cdot (157.08)^2 = 50 \cdot 0.0004 \cdot 24674 = 493.5 \text{ N}$

Amplitude using the rotating-unbalance formula:

$$X = \frac{e \cdot r^2}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$$

$$r^2 = (2.484)^2 = 6.170$$

$$1 - r^2 = 1 - 6.170 = -5.170$$

$$(1 - r^2)^2 = 26.73$$

$$2\zeta r = 2 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 2.484 = 0.7452$$

$$(2\zeta r)^2 = 0.5553$$

$$\text{Denominator} = \sqrt{26.73 + 0.5553} = \sqrt{27.285} = 5.224$$

$$X = \frac{0.0004 \cdot 6.170}{5.224} = \frac{0.002468}{5.224} = 4.725 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 0.473 \text{ mm}$$

**(ii) At 1800 rpm:**

$$\omega' = \frac{2\pi \cdot 1800}{60} = 188.5 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$r' = \frac{188.5}{63.246} = 2.980$$

$$(r')^2 = 8.881$$

$$1 - (r')^2 = -7.881 \Rightarrow (1 - (r')^2)^2 = 62.11$$

$$2\zeta r' = 2 \cdot 0.15 \cdot 2.980 = 0.894 \Rightarrow (0.894)^2 = 0.799$$

$$\text{Denominator} = \sqrt{62.11 + 0.799} = \sqrt{62.91} = 7.932$$

$$X' = \frac{0.0004 \cdot 8.881}{7.932} = \frac{0.003552}{7.932} = 4.478 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m} \approx 0.448 \text{ mm}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common error:** Many KTU students use the *standard forced-vibration* amplitude formula here:
> $X = (F_0/k)/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$.
> But the question says **rotating unbalance** — the formula must use the **eccentricity e directly** (i.e., $X = e r^2/\sqrt{\cdots}$). Mixing the two forms is the most common mistake on this topic and costs 2–3 marks.

---

#### Solution to Q3(B)(b)

**Resonance Explanation:**
Resonance occurs when the forcing frequency $\omega$ matches the natural frequency $\omega_n$ of the system (i.e., $r = 1$). At this point, the energy input by the external force per cycle is maximum, and for an undamped system the amplitude theoretically grows to infinity. Even for lightly damped systems, amplitudes can be 10×–100× the static deflection, leading to rapid fatigue failure.

**Effect of Damping on the Resonance Peak (qualitative):**
- $\zeta = 0$: Peak is infinite (vertical asymptote).
- $\zeta = 0.05$: Peak height = $1/(2 \cdot 0.05) = 10$.
- $\zeta = 0.20$: Peak height = $1/(2 \cdot 0.20) = 2.5$.
- $\zeta = 1$: Peak height = $1/2$ (no resonance peak in the usual sense).

Higher damping reduces the peak height and broadens the resonance curve (more stable, less sensitive to small changes in $\omega$).

**Numerical Part:**

$$\zeta = 0.05, \quad r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = 1 \quad (\text{since machine is run at its natural frequency})$$

$$MF = \frac{1}{\sqrt{(1-1)^2 + (2 \cdot 0.05 \cdot 1)^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{0 + 0.01}} = \frac{1}{0.1} = 10$$

**Comment on Safety:** A magnification factor of 10 means vibration amplitudes are 10 times the static deflection. Even tiny unbalance forces (a few grams) can produce millimetres of motion, leading to bearing damage, fatigue cracks, and possibly catastrophic failure. **Operation is highly unsafe.** The machine must be:
- Re-balanced,
- Operated well away from 20 Hz (run-up and run-down must be quick),
- Or fitted with additional damping.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Final Word on Module 4**
> 1. Always clearly distinguish between **free damped** and **forced** vibration formulae.
> 2. In **rotating unbalance** problems, use the $e r^2$ form, not $F_0/k$.
> 3. State the **units** explicitly in all numerical answers.
> 4. The phase angle $\phi$ must be **derived**, not just stated, for full marks.
> 5. Logarithmic decrement questions: write the *full* expression $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$ first, then state the small-$\zeta$ approximation separately.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Damped ODE:** $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F(t)$ — universal starting point.
- **Standard form:** $\ddot{x} + 2\zeta\omega_n \dot{x} + \omega_n^2 x = F(t)/m$ — recognise on sight.
- **Three damping regimes:** Underdamped ($\zeta < 1$), Critically damped ($\zeta = 1$), Overdamped ($\zeta > 1$).
- **Underdamped solution:** $x(t) = X e^{-\zeta\omega_n t} \sin(\omega_d t + \phi)$, with $\omega_d = \omega_n\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}$.
- **Critical damping coefficient:** $c_c = 2m\omega_n = 2\sqrt{km}$.
- **Logarithmic decrement:** $\delta = 2\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2} \approx 2\pi\zeta$ for light damping.
- **Static deflection:** $X_{static} = F_0/k$ — always compute this first.
- **Forced amplitude:** $X = X_{static}/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$.
- **Magnification factor:** $MF = 1/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$.
- **Resonance condition:** $r = 1$, $MF_{max} = 1/(2\zeta)$.
- **True peak location:** $r = \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ (advanced — only if explicitly asked).
- **Rotating unbalance:** $F_0 = me\omega^2$; $X = e r^2/\sqrt{(1-r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}$ — distinctive $r^2$ in numerator.
- **Phase angle:** $\phi = \tan^{-1}\left[2\zeta r/(1-r^2)\right]$ — equals 90° at resonance.
- **High-speed limit:** $X \to e$ for rotating unbalance at $r \gg 1$.
- **Low-speed limit:** $X \to 0$ for rotating unbalance at $r \to 0$.
- **Engineering use:** Resonance avoided by ensuring operating $\omega$ is well above or below $\omega_n$, or by adding damping.
- **Sign convention:** Phase $\phi$ is measured from displacement *to* the force, *positive* means force leads.
- **Units check:** $\omega_n$ in rad/s, *k* in N/m, *c* in N·s/m, *m* in kg, $F_0$ in N, *X* in m.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Mantra for this Module:**
> *"In damped free vibration, ζ decides decay. In forced vibration, r decides amplification. At r = 1, beware the resonance peak — and for rotating unbalance, never forget the r²!"*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Whirling of Rotating Shafts, Vibration Isolation and Transmissibility
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEIET413 - DYNAMICS OF MACHINERY > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Whirling of Rotating Shafts, Vibration Isolation and Transmissibility # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Whirling of Rotating Shafts ### Formal Definition **Whirling** is the phenomenon of lateral (transverse) vibration exhibited by a rotating shaft carrying an unbalanced mass (rotor). When the rotational speed of the shaft coincides with the shaft's natural frequency of transverse vibration, the shaft experiences violent transverse oscillations called **whirling**, and the corresponding speed is known as the **Critical Speed** of the shaft. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (2024 Scheme):** > Every KTU problem on whirling revolves around the formula $\omega_c = \sqrt{g / \delta_{st}}$, where $\delta_{st}$ is the static deflection (in metres) of the shaft due to the weight of the rotor. The critical speed in **RPM** is $N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a child swinging on a swing. The swing has a *natural rhythm* — push it faster than its rhythm, and it gets angry (vibrates violently); push it exactly at its rhythm, and it goes wild. A rotating shaft is the same: - The "swing" is the flexible shaft. - The "pushing force" is the centrifugal pull from the unbalanced mass. - The "natural rhythm" is the shaft's natural frequency of bending. - When the rotation speed (pushing rhythm) matches the natural frequency, the shaft **whirls** — bending into a bowed shape that rotates with the shaft. ### Key Physical Quantities - **Static deflection** ($\delta_{st}$): measured in **metres (m)**. - **Critical angular velocity** ($\omega_c$): measured in **rad/s**. - **Critical speed in RPM** ($N_c$): measured in **revolutions per minute (rpm)**. - **Modulus of rigidity / Young's modulus**: **N/m²**. - **Stiffness of shaft** ($k$): **N/m**. > [!NOTE] > The assumption in whirling analysis is that **the mass of the shaft is negligible** compared to the rotor(s) mounted on it. The shaft acts purely as an elastic spring. ## 1.2 Vibration Isolation ### Formal Definition **Vibration Isolation** is the process of reducing the transmission of vibrations from a vibrating source to a recipient (or vice versa) by introducing an elastic mounting (spring and damper) between them. It is classified into two types: 1. **Active (Source) Isolation** — isolating a vibrating machine from its supporting foundation. 2. **Passive (Receiver) Isolation** — protecting delicate instruments from external ground vibrations. ### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Picture a washing machine (the source) placed on a soft rubber mat (the isolator) on the floor. The rubber mat absorbs the tremors so the floor doesn't shake. The same idea applies to car engine mounts, building base isolators in earthquake zones, and CNC machine feet. ## 1.3 Transmissibility ### Formal Definition **Transmissibility (TR)** is the dimensionless ratio of the force transmitted to the foundation (or the response amplitude of the isolated mass) to the excitation force (or input amplitude). It quantifies how effectively an isolation system performs. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Board Favourite:** When the frequency ratio $r = \omega / \omega_n > \sqrt{2}$, the transmissibility is **less than 1**, which is the practical *isolation zone*. KTU examiners love to ask: *"For what value of $r$ is the system said to be in the isolation region?"* — Answer: $r > \sqrt{2}$. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Whirling of Shafts — Underlying Theory ### 2.1.1 Causes of Whirling 1. **Unbalance of the rotor** — uneven mass distribution around the axis. 2. **Bent shaft** — geometric imperfection. 3. **Misalignment** between bearings and rotor centre line. 4. **Material inhomogeneity** in the shaft. ### 2.1.2 Effects of Whirling - Severe vibration at critical speed. - Bearing failure, fatigue, possible catastrophic fracture. - Reduced machine life, increased noise. ### 2.1.3 Why Critical Speed Occurs — The "Why" and "How" **How** it occurs: - A rotor with mass $m$ at distance $e$ from the axis of rotation (eccentricity) generates a centrifugal force $F_c = m \omega^2 e$. - This force causes the shaft to bend. The bending provides an elastic restoring force proportional to deflection $y$. - If the rotation $\omega$ equals the natural frequency of bending, the centrifugal force and the elastic force are in step → energy keeps building up → **resonance** → whirling. **Why** it matters: - All rotating machinery (turbines, compressors, electric motors, grinding wheels) must operate **well above or well below** the critical speed to avoid catastrophic failure. ### 2.1.4 Dunkerley's Method (for multiple rotors) When a shaft carries several rotors, the combined critical speed cannot be found by simply adding individual critical speeds. **Dunkerley's empirical rule** states: $$\frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \frac{1}{\omega_{c1}^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_{c2}^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_{c3}^2} + \cdots$$ where $\omega_{c1}, \omega_{c2}, \ldots$ are the critical speeds computed as if each rotor were acting **alone** on the shaft. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield:** Dunkerley's method is often tested in **14-mark Part B** questions. The Examiner expects: (i) calculate $\delta$ for each rotor due to its own weight, (ii) compute individual $\omega_{ci}$, (iii) substitute into Dunkerley's equation. ## 2.2 Vibration Isolation — Underlying Theory ### 2.2.1 Two Models of Isolation 1. **Force-excited (active) isolation** — a force $F_0 \sin\omega t$ acts on the mass; we seek the force transmitted to the base. 2. **Base-excited (passive) isolation** — the base moves with $y = Y \sin\omega t$; we seek the displacement amplitude $X$ of the mass. The transmissibility formula is **mathematically identical** for both models. ### 2.2.2 Significance of Damping in Isolation - Damping reduces the **peak** transmissibility at resonance. - However, damping **increases** transmissibility in the high-frequency isolation region. - Designers therefore select **low damping** for good isolation in the $r > \sqrt{2}$ region. - The optimum damping ratio for minimum peak response is $\zeta_{opt} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{r^2 - 1}}$ at the operating frequency ratio. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **#** | **Quantity / Concept** | **Formula** | **Units** | **When to Use** | |:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---| | 1 | Stiffness from static deflection | $k = \dfrac{mg}{\delta_{st}}$ | N/m | Given weight & static deflection | | 2 | Natural frequency of shaft | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ | rad/s | Single rotor on light shaft | | 3 | Critical speed in RPM | $N_c = \dfrac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{\delta_{st}}}$ | rpm | Board numericals | | 4 | Static deflection (Point load) | $\delta = \dfrac{W a^2 b^2}{3 E I L}$ | m | Single load off-centre | | 5 | Static deflection (centre load) | $\delta = \dfrac{W L^3}{48 E I}$ | m | Single load at mid-span | | 6 | Dunkerley's rule | $\dfrac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \sum \dfrac{1}{\omega_{ci}^2}$ | — | Multiple rotors on one shaft | | 7 | Equation of motion (forced) | $m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin\omega t$ | — | Active isolation | | 8 | Frequency ratio | $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n}$ | — | Dimensionless analysis | | 9 | Damping ratio | $\zeta = \dfrac{c}{2\sqrt{km}} = \dfrac{c}{c_c}$ | — | Identify damping level | | 10 | Transmissibility (damped) | $TR = \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$ | — | Full isolation calculation | | 11 | Transmissibility (undamped) | $TR = \dfrac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert}$ | — | $\zeta = 0$ case | | 12 | Isolation criterion | $r > \sqrt{2}$ | — | When TR < 1 | | 13 | Critical frequency ratio | $r_{cr} = \sqrt{2}$ | — | Threshold for isolation | | 14 | Transmitted force | $F_T = TR \cdot F_0$ | N | Magnitude of force into base | > [!NOTE] > In the formula table above, **absolute value** is written as $\vert 1 - r^2 \vert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax. The KTU answer sheet should display it as $|1 - r^2|$. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Whirling analysis** is critical in designing **steam/gas turbine shafts, centrifugal pumps, propeller shafts, and electric motor rotors** — all of which must avoid operating near their critical speeds. - **Vibration isolation** is the design philosophy behind **engine mounts in cars, base isolators in skyscrapers (earthquake protection), anti-vibration pads under HVAC chillers, and isolators under CNC machines**. - **Transmissibility** charts are used to select isolator stiffness for **electronic equipment on ships, aircraft, and military vehicles**. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Critical Speed of a Light Shaft Carrying a Single Rotor ### Step 1: Set Up the Physical Model Consider a uniform flexible shaft of negligible mass supported on two bearings, carrying a rotor of mass $m$ at its centre. Let the static deflection of the shaft due to the weight $W = mg$ of the rotor be $\delta_{st}$. ### Step 2: Identify the Stiffness The shaft behaves as a linear spring of stiffness $k$. The spring force equals the weight at equilibrium: $$ k \cdot \delta_{st} = mg $$ $$ \therefore \quad k = \frac{mg}{\delta_{st}} $$ ### Step 3: Write the Equation of Motion During Whirling When the shaft rotates at angular velocity $\omega$ and whirls with the rotor displaced a distance $y$ from the bearing axis, the **centrifugal force** acting on the rotor is: $$ F_c = m \omega^2 y $$ The shaft's elastic restoring force is: $$ F_e = k y $$ ### Step 4: Apply Equilibrium During Whirling At steady whirling, the centrifugal force is balanced by the elastic force: $$ m \omega^2 y = k y $$ Cancel $y$ (non-trivial solution) from both sides: $$ m \omega^2 = k $$ ### Step 5: Solve for the Critical Angular Velocity Substituting $k = \dfrac{mg}{\delta_{st}}$: $$ m \omega_c^2 = \frac{mg}{\delta_{st}} $$ $$ \omega_c^2 = \frac{g}{\delta_{st}} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}}\;} $$ ### Step 6: Convert to RPM $$ N_c = \frac{60 \omega_c}{2 \pi} = \frac{60}{2 \pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board Examiner's Insight:** Since $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$, the numerical form is > $\;N_c = 9.55 \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{\delta_{st}}} \;\text{rpm}\;$ (for $\delta_{st}$ in metres). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dunkerley's Equation (Composite Critical Speed) ### Step 1: Consider Multiple Rotors Let a shaft carry $n$ rotors of masses $m_1, m_2, \ldots, m_n$ at various sections. The total static deflection at the *i-th* rotor location due to **all** weights is $\delta_i$. ### Step 2: Dunkerley's Assumption Dunkerley assumed that the deflection $\delta_i$ can be expressed as the sum of deflections caused by each rotor acting **independently**: $$ \delta_i = \delta_{i1} + \delta_{i2} + \cdots + \delta_{in} $$ ### Step 3: Define Individual Critical Speeds If rotor $i$ alone were on the shaft, the static deflection at the *i-th* rotor location would be $\delta_{ii}$, and the corresponding critical speed would be: $$ \omega_{ci} = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{ii}}} $$ ### Step 4: Use Superposition of Strain Energy The total strain energy of the shaft equals the sum of strain energies due to each rotor. Combining with the fact that natural frequency squared is inversely proportional to total effective deflection, Dunkerley obtained: $$ \boxed{\;\frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \frac{1}{\omega_{c1}^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_{c2}^2} + \cdots + \frac{1}{\omega_{cn}^2}\;} $$ > [!NOTE] > **Why this method is used:** Dunkerley's method gives a slightly **lower** value of the actual critical speed (it neglects the shaft's own mass), so it is always *conservative* and hence safe for design. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Transmissibility of a Damped System ### Step 1: Equation of Motion (Force-Excited) Consider a mass $m$ supported on a spring of stiffness $k$ and damper of damping coefficient $c$, with a force $F(t) = F_0 \sin \omega t$ applied to the mass. Let $F_T$ be the force transmitted to the base through the spring-damper. The equation of motion is: $$ m\ddot{x} + c\dot{x} + kx = F_0 \sin\omega t $$ The force transmitted to the base is the vector sum of the spring force $kx$ and the damper force $c\dot{x}$: $$ F_T = \sqrt{(kx)^2 + (c\dot{x})^2} $$ ### Step 2: Assume Steady-State Solution For steady-state forced vibration, the displacement has the form $x(t) = X \sin(\omega t - \phi)$. Differentiating: $$ \dot{x} = \omega X \cos(\omega t - \phi) $$ ### Step 3: Magnitude of Transmitted Force The amplitude of the transmitted force is: $$ F_T = \sqrt{(kX)^2 + (c\omega X)^2} = X \sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2} $$ ### Step 4: Substitute the Steady-State Amplitude From the standard solution of single-DOF forced vibration: $$ X = \frac{F_0}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}} $$ ### Step 5: Form the Transmissibility Ratio $$ TR = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \frac{X \sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{F_0} $$ Substitute $X$: $$ TR = \frac{\sqrt{k^2 + (c\omega)^2}}{\sqrt{(k - m\omega^2)^2 + (c\omega)^2}} $$ ### Step 6: Introduce Non-Dimensional Parameters Divide numerator and denominator by $k$, with $r = \omega / \omega_n$ and $\zeta = c / (2\sqrt{km})$: $$ TR = \frac{\sqrt{1 + \left(2\zeta r\right)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} $$ $$ \boxed{\;TR = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}\;} $$ ### Step 7: Special Case — Undamped System ($\zeta = 0$) $$ TR = \frac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert} $$ - For $r < 1$: $TR = \dfrac{1}{1 - r^2} > 1$ (amplification). - For $r > 1$: $TR = \dfrac{1}{r^2 - 1} < 1$ when $r > \sqrt{2}$ (isolation). --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Critical Frequency Ratio for Isolation ### Step 1: Set Transmissibility Equal to Unity For isolation, we require $TR \le 1$. In the **undamped** case, set $TR = 1$: $$ \frac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert} = 1 $$ $$ \vert 1 - r^2 \vert = 1 $$ ### Step 2: Solve for $r$ Taking the positive root (since $r > 0$): $$ 1 - r^2 = -1 $$ $$ r^2 = 2 $$ $$ \boxed{\;r = \sqrt{2}\;} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **famous KTU result**: the threshold for isolation is $r = \sqrt{2}$. Operate the system with $r > \sqrt{2}$ for effective isolation. The numerical value is $r \approx 1.414$. --- ## 3.5 Solved Numerical Example (Model Board Problem) **Problem:** A steel shaft of length $1.2$ m and diameter $50$ mm simply supported at the ends carries a rotor of mass $40$ kg at the centre. Find the critical speed in rpm. Take $E = 2 \times 10^{11}$ N/m². **Step 1: Moment of Inertia of the Shaft Cross-Section** $$ I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (0.05)^4}{64} $$ $$ I = \frac{\pi \times 6.25 \times 10^{-6}}{64} = 3.068 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{m}^4 $$ **Step 2: Static Deflection at Centre (Simply Supported, Centre Load)** $$ \delta_{st} = \frac{W L^3}{48 E I} = \frac{(40 \times 9.81) \times (1.2)^3}{48 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 3.068 \times 10^{-7}} $$ $$ \delta_{st} = \frac{392.4 \times 1.728}{48 \times 6.136 \times 10^{4}} $$ $$ \delta_{st} = \frac{678.07}{2.9453 \times 10^{6}} = 2.302 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m} $$ **Step 3: Critical Speed in RPM** $$ N_c = \frac{60}{2 \pi}\sqrt{\frac{9.81}{2.302 \times 10^{-4}}} $$ $$ N_c = 9.55 \times \sqrt{42615.12} = 9.55 \times 206.43 $$ $$ \boxed{\;N_c \approx 1971 \; \text{rpm}\;} $$ --- ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for Quick Revision | **Feature** | **Active Isolation** | **Passive Isolation** | **Whirling** | |:---|:---|:---|:---| | **Excitation type** | Force on mass | Base motion | Centrifugal force | | **Goal** | Reduce $F_T$ to base | Reduce mass amplitude $X$ | Avoid resonance with rotor | | **Key formula** | $F_T = TR \cdot F_0$ | $X = TR \cdot Y$ | $N_c = 9.55 / \sqrt{\delta_{st}}$ | | **Isolation threshold** | $r > \sqrt{2}$ | $r > \sqrt{2}$ | Operate far from $N_c$ | | **Damping role** | Increases $F_T$ at high $r$ | Increases $X$ at high $r$ | Damps out whirling amplitude | | **Practical example** | Engine mount | Equipment on ship deck | Turbine rotor | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Whirling of Shaft — Physical & Functional Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Rotor with Mass m at Eccentricity e] --> B[Rotation at Angular Velocity omega] B --> C[Centrifugal Force: F = m omega squared y] C --> D[Shaft Bends Transversely] D --> E{Is omega equal to omega_n?} E -- Yes --> F[RESONANCE: Violent Whirling] E -- No --> G[Stable Operation with Minor Vibration] F --> H[Possible Shaft Failure] G --> I[Safe Working Speed Zone] H --> J[Design Modification Required] J --> K[Increase Stiffness k] J --> L[Reduce Static Deflection delta] J --> M[Shift Operating Speed Away from N_c] ``` ## 4.2 Vibration Isolation System — Block Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Vibrating Source
Machine Mass m] -->|Force F0 sin omega t| B[Spring of Stiffness k] A -->|Damping| C[Damper of Coefficient c] B --> D[Transmitted Force F_T] C --> D D --> E[Foundation or Receiver] F[Operating Frequency omega] --> G{Frequency Ratio r compared with sqrt of 2} G -- r less than 1 --> H[Amplification Region] G -- 1 less than r less than sqrt of 2 --> I[Transition Region] G -- r greater than sqrt of 2 --> J[ISOLATION ZONE] H --> K[TR greater than 1] I --> L[TR decreasing to 1] J --> M[TR less than 1] ``` ## 4.3 Transmissibility Curve — Behavioural Map ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Start Analysis] --> P1[Compute Natural Frequency omega_n = sqrt of k over m] P1 --> P2[Compute Operating Frequency omega] P2 --> P3[Compute Frequency Ratio r = omega over omega_n] P3 --> P4[Compute Damping Ratio zeta = c over 2 sqrt of km] P4 --> P5[Compute TR using Damped Formula] P5 --> Decision{Is r greater than sqrt of 2?} Decision -- Yes --> Output1[Effective Isolation Achieved] Decision -- No --> Output2[Amplification Occurs - Redesign Required] Output1 --> End1[Select Lower Stiffness k for Better Isolation] Output2 --> End2[Increase Stiffness or Shift Operating Speed] ``` ## 4.4 Dunkerley’s Method — Sequential Block Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Identify All Rotors on Shaft] --> S2[Compute Static Deflection delta_i for each Rotor] S2 --> S3[Compute Individual Critical Speeds omega_c_i = sqrt of g over delta_ii] S3 --> S4[Apply Dunkerley Sum: 1 over omega_c squared equals Sum of 1 over omega_c_i squared] S4 --> S5[Solve for Combined omega_c] S5 --> S6[Convert to N_c in rpm] S6 --> S7[Verify Operating Speed is well away from N_c] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam — May 2023]** Define the term **critical speed** of a rotating shaft. State the formula relating critical speed to the static deflection of the shaft. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Critical speed** of a rotating shaft is the speed at which the shaft undergoes **violent transverse vibration (whirling)** due to the resonance between the centrifugal force acting on the unbalanced rotor and the natural frequency of transverse vibration of the shaft. The formula relating critical speed to static deflection $\delta_{st}$ (in metres) is: $$ N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} = 9.55 \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{st}}} \; \text{rpm} $$ where $g = 9.81 \, \text{m/s}^2$. ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** What is **transmissibility** in vibration isolation? State the condition under which the isolation system becomes effective. **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Transmissibility (TR)** is defined as the ratio of the force transmitted to the foundation to the exciting force, mathematically: $$ TR = \frac{F_T}{F_0} = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} $$ The isolation system becomes effective when the **frequency ratio** satisfies $r = \dfrac{\omega}{\omega_n} > \sqrt{2}$, because under this condition **TR < 1**, meaning the transmitted force is less than the applied force. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (Choice 1) — Whirling of Shafts with Multiple Rotors **[KTU University Exam — June 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]** A shaft of length $1.8$ m, simply supported at the ends, carries three rotors of masses $20$ kg, $30$ kg, and $25$ kg located at distances $0.4$ m, $0.9$ m, and $1.4$ m from one end, respectively. The shaft is made of steel with $E = 2 \times 10^{11}$ N/m² and the diameter is $60$ mm. Determine the **critical speed** of the shaft using **Dunkerley's method**. #### Sub-Part (a) — Compute Static Deflection Contributions (7 marks) **Given:** - $L = 1.8$ m, $d = 0.06$ m, $E = 2 \times 10^{11}$ N/m² - $m_1 = 20$ kg at $a_1 = 0.4$ m, $b_1 = 1.4$ m - $m_2 = 30$ kg at $a_2 = 0.9$ m, $b_2 = 0.9$ m (centre) - $m_3 = 25$ kg at $a_3 = 1.4$ m, $b_3 = 0.4$ m **Step 1: Moment of Inertia** $$ I = \frac{\pi d^4}{64} = \frac{\pi (0.06)^4}{64} = 6.362 \times 10^{-7} \; \text{m}^4 $$ **Step 2: Standard Deflection Formula (point load on simply supported beam)** $$ \delta = \frac{W a^2 b^2}{3 E I L} $$ **Step 3: Deflection at Rotor 1 location due to Rotor 1 alone ($a = 0.4, b = 1.4, W_1 = 20 \times 9.81 = 196.2$ N)** $$ \delta_{11} = \frac{196.2 \times (0.4)^2 \times (1.4)^2}{3 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 6.362 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.8} $$ $$ \delta_{11} = \frac{196.2 \times 0.16 \times 1.96}{6.871 \times 10^{5}} = \frac{61.51}{6.871 \times 10^{5}} = 8.95 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m} $$ **Step 4: Deflection at Rotor 2 location due to Rotor 2 alone (centre, $W_2 = 30 \times 9.81 = 294.3$ N)** $$ \delta_{22} = \frac{W L^3}{48 E I} = \frac{294.3 \times (1.8)^3}{48 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 6.362 \times 10^{-7}} $$ $$ \delta_{22} = \frac{294.3 \times 5.832}{6.107 \times 10^{6}} = \frac{1716.4}{6.107 \times 10^{6}} = 2.811 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m} $$ **Step 5: Deflection at Rotor 3 location due to Rotor 3 alone (by symmetry with Rotor 1, $W_3 = 25 \times 9.81 = 245.25$ N)** $$ \delta_{33} = \frac{245.25 \times (0.4)^2 \times (1.4)^2}{3 \times 2 \times 10^{11} \times 6.362 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.8} = 1.118 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{m} $$ #### Sub-Part (b) — Apply Dunkerley’s Method (7 marks) **Step 6: Individual Critical Speeds** $$ \omega_{c1} = \sqrt{\frac{g}{\delta_{11}}} = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{8.95 \times 10^{-5}}} = \sqrt{1.096 \times 10^{5}} = 331.1 \; \text{rad/s} $$ $$ \omega_{c2} = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{2.811 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{3.490 \times 10^{4}} = 186.8 \; \text{rad/s} $$ $$ \omega_{c3} = \sqrt{\frac{9.81}{1.118 \times 10^{-4}}} = \sqrt{8.775 \times 10^{4}} = 296.2 \; \text{rad/s} $$ **Step 7: Dunkerley's Sum** $$ \frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \frac{1}{\omega_{c1}^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_{c2}^2} + \frac{1}{\omega_{c3}^2} $$ $$ \frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \frac{1}{331.1^2} + \frac{1}{186.8^2} + \frac{1}{296.2^2} $$ $$ \frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \frac{1}{1.096 \times 10^5} + \frac{1}{3.490 \times 10^4} + \frac{1}{8.775 \times 10^4} $$ $$ \frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = 9.124 \times 10^{-6} + 2.865 \times 10^{-5} + 1.140 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ \frac{1}{\omega_c^2} = 4.917 \times 10^{-5} $$ $$ \omega_c^2 = 2.034 \times 10^{4} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \omega_c = 142.6 \; \text{rad/s} $$ **Step 8: Critical Speed in RPM** $$ \boxed{\;N_c = \frac{60 \times 142.6}{2\pi} = \frac{8556}{6.283} \approx 1361 \; \text{rpm}\;} $$ --- ### Question B (Choice 2) — Vibration Isolation and Transmissibility **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]** A machine of mass $50$ kg is supported on a spring-damper isolator. The stiffness of the spring is $30{,}000$ N/m and the damping coefficient is $300$ N·s/m. The machine operates at a speed of $1800$ rpm. Determine: - (a) The natural frequency, damping ratio, and frequency ratio. - (b) The transmissibility of the system, and state whether the system provides effective isolation. #### Sub-Part (a) — System Parameters (7 marks) **Given:** - $m = 50$ kg - $k = 30{,}000$ N/m - $c = 300$ N·s/m - $N = 1800$ rpm $\Rightarrow \omega = \dfrac{2\pi \times 1800}{60} = 188.5$ rad/s **Step 1: Natural Frequency** $$ \omega_n = \sqrt{\frac{k}{m}} = \sqrt{\frac{30{,}000}{50}} = \sqrt{600} = 24.49 \; \text{rad/s} $$ **Step 2: Critical Damping Coefficient** $$ c_c = 2 \sqrt{km} = 2 \sqrt{30{,}000 \times 50} = 2 \sqrt{1.5 \times 10^6} = 2 \times 1224.7 = 2449.5 \; \text{N·s/m} $$ **Step 3: Damping Ratio** $$ \zeta = \frac{c}{c_c} = \frac{300}{2449.5} = 0.1225 $$ **Step 4: Frequency Ratio** $$ r = \frac{\omega}{\omega_n} = \frac{188.5}{24.49} = 7.70 $$ #### Sub-Part (b) — Transmissibility and Isolation Check (7 marks) **Step 5: Transmissibility Formula** $$ TR = \frac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}} $$ **Step 6: Compute Components** $$ 2\zeta r = 2 \times 0.1225 \times 7.70 = 1.887 $$ $$ (2\zeta r)^2 = 3.561 $$ $$ r^2 = 59.29 \quad \Rightarrow \quad 1 - r^2 = -58.29 \quad \Rightarrow \quad (1 - r^2)^2 = 3397.7 $$ **Step 7: Substitute** $$ TR = \frac{\sqrt{1 + 3.561}}{\sqrt{3397.7 + 3.561}} = \frac{\sqrt{4.561}}{\sqrt{3401.3}} $$ $$ TR = \frac{2.135}{58.32} $$ $$ \boxed{\;TR \approx 0.0366\;} $$ **Step 8: Isolation Verdict** Since $r = 7.70 \gg \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, the system is well within the **isolation zone**. Also, $TR = 0.0366 < 1$, confirming that only about **3.66%** of the disturbing force is transmitted to the foundation. **The isolation is highly effective.** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Unit trap:** Many students forget to convert rpm to rad/s when computing $\omega$. Always use $\omega = 2\pi N / 60$. A common error is to use $N$ directly in the formula, costing 2 marks. > 2. **Static deflection units:** $\delta_{st}$ **must be in metres**, not mm. A common slip-up is to plug in mm and obtain an absurdly low $N_c$, losing 2 marks. > 3. **Dunkerley’s sign:** Dunkerley’s rule uses reciprocals of **squares** of critical speeds, not critical speeds themselves. Writing $1/\omega_c = \sum 1/\omega_{ci}$ will cost 3 marks. > 4. **Threshold mis-statement:** The isolation condition is $r > \sqrt{2}$, **not** $r > 1$. State the value explicitly: $r > 1.414$. Examiners will deduct 1 mark for the wrong threshold. > 5. **Damping in isolation:** Students often claim "more damping is better". This is **wrong** for the high-frequency isolation region; damping should be kept low (typically $\zeta < 0.2$) in the operating range. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4** - **Whirling** is lateral vibration of a rotating shaft, occurring at the **critical speed** where rotation equals the natural frequency of transverse bending. - The single-rotor critical speed formula is: $\omega_c = \sqrt{g / \delta_{st}}$, and $N_c = 9.55 \sqrt{g / \delta_{st}}$ in rpm. - **Dunkerley’s method** for multiple rotors: $\dfrac{1}{\omega_c^2} = \sum \dfrac{1}{\omega_{ci}^2}$. It always gives a **conservative (lower-bound)** estimate of the true critical speed. - Static deflection for a **centre load** on a simply supported beam: $\delta = \dfrac{W L^3}{48 E I}$. - Static deflection for an **off-centre load**: $\delta = \dfrac{W a^2 b^2}{3 E I L}$. - **Vibration isolation** aims to reduce transmission of vibration using springs and dampers. - **Transmissibility** $TR$ is the ratio of transmitted force to exciting force. - The complete damped formula: $TR = \dfrac{\sqrt{1 + (2\zeta r)^2}}{\sqrt{(1 - r^2)^2 + (2\zeta r)^2}}$. - The undamped formula simplifies to: $TR = \dfrac{1}{\vert 1 - r^2 \vert}$. - The **isolation criterion** is $r > \sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$, which gives $TR < 1$. - At resonance ($r = 1$), $TR$ is maximum — operating speeds must **avoid** this point. - **Lower damping** is preferred in the isolation region because damping increases the high-frequency transmissibility. - Always check: $\delta_{st}$ in **metres**, $\omega$ in **rad/s**, $N_c$ in **rpm**, $E$ in **N/m²**, $I$ in **m⁴**. - KTU 14-mark questions often combine **two rotors** with Dunkerley's method — practice the deflection-superposition approach. - Real-world applications: **turbines, motors, pumps, vehicle suspensions, building base isolators, machine tool mounts**. ---
